players.name: Jeremiyah Love
players.lateround_profile: We won't get into the real-life ramifications of Arizona taking Jeremiyah Love third overall - this is a fantasy football guide. (But, man, that's not what I would've done with that roster.) Love, as a player, is the truth. There aren't really any red flags in his profile. His top-season prorated reception share of 12.4% could be better, but it still lands in the 65th percentile among ZAP Model running backs. His adjusted total yards per team play ranks in the 94th percentile, while his adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt sits in the 83rd. Non-ZAP metrics like him, too. Love posted an 88th percentile career avoided tackles per attempt rate, and his yards after contact per rush, per PFF, ranked in the 96th percentile. Given his draft capital, Love's ZAP score is beyond elite. Nearly every player to hit 97 or higher in the model's history has averaged at least 17 PPR points per game in one of their first three seasons - the lone exception is Ashton Jeanty, who's only played one NFL season. The situation in Arizona won't make you jump for joy, but it's workable. The Cardinals are now coached by Mike LaFleur, a Shanahan-tree play-caller - he's no stranger to an efficient run game. The offense may struggle until they find their quarterback, but even in what looked like a crowded backfield entering the draft, Love should see volume right away - especially as a receiver. There's just no doubt: Love is the 1.01 in any normal rookie draft format. Don't overthink it. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 83
players.etr_notes: Elite talent, but a crowded backfield and no long-term QB.
players.elevator_pitch:
2006 was the first year I published the RSP. Reggie Bush was the next big thing in football. Former 
pros who became analysts compared Bush to Gale Sayers. 
If you're subscriber under 40 and came to football on your own, it's likely you don't know who Sayers was. One of my 
friends is 35, and I had to explain who Sayers was to the history of the game and the running back position. 
It clicked for him when I said Sayers was revered in the same way his parents (who are my age) revere Barry Sanders. 
Broach the names Sayers or Sanders to fans who saw one or both, and they begin speaking with hushed tones. 
Bush earned those comparisons on-air in sports programming before clickbait was a legitimate word. It was my first season 
evaluating players when Bush was the most popular skill player in that draft class. 
While I'm positive my current level of experience would have made the evaluation different, I've watched some of those old 
highlights. Bush would have had an excellent score. 
When Bush arrived in New Orleans, he pressed too hard to be the savior of the football team and the city, trying to bounce 
and cutback runs for big plays that he had approached with more discipline at USC. 
Bush needed six seasons to generate three years of production that was, at best, in the general vicinity of his rookie 
expectations. Bush missed 21 games during those first 6 years in the league, and his knees were becoming a problem. 
Bush had a good career, but it was nowhere near the expectations pre-draft. For fans and media who remember this as well 
as I, Jeremiyah Love's style not only bears similarities to Bush, but there's a vibe that public who knew Bush's USC game 
are hoping Love has the leading role in "Reggie Bush II: This Time It's for Real." 
Considered the top skill-player in this draft class, Love is a versatile big-play threat with tremendous athletic ability for his 
size. If you could mix a lot of the stylistic elements of Bush and Alvin Kamara and blend their frames, you'd get a player like 
Love. 
Love is a slippery runner with quick feet, sudden deceleration and acceleration, hip mobility, sharp lateral cuts, and the hops 
to clear safeties aiming for his waist. He excels at making the first defender miss, even when it's in the backfield. 
Love has NFL starter-caliber skills as a gap runner. His work with Duo and other interior tight-crease blocking could use 
more patience to set up second-level blocks. 
Although there are lapses with patience, Love has shown more than enough time's that he will press within 1-2 steps of his 
blockers to set up creases. Once Love finds an open lane, he has good-to-great acceleration and elite speed to pull away 
from pursuit. 
He's not just a speedster. Love wins against contact, often multiple points of contact during the same touch. 
Love bounces off hits at all three levels of the defense because he attacks collisions. A lot of backs don't do this well 
anymore. 
61
players.pre_draft_advice:
For many, Love is the top prospect among the skill players in this class. Love is the most 
likely to have a high-ceiling impact as a rookie and to sustain starter production for consecutive years early in his career. 
If you're looking at having the rights to a player for 8-10 years and generating starter production for most of them, 
Fernando Mendoza could have more value depending on the league. If you're seeking 6-8 years with the most difference-
making talent relative to other skill positions, Carnell Tate is the best choice. 
Love and Tate are 2 of the first 3-5 picks in this class. If your team is a contender and needs one impactful piece right now, 
Love is the best choice. If you're still building, Tate is the guy. Super Flex? Likely Mendoza.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Love is an explosive athlete with a remarkable blend of balance and burst to be dynamic as both a runner and in the passing game. He is one of the most talented players in the 2026 draft class and projects as a high-upside NFL weapon.

players.name: Jordyn Tyson
players.lateround_profile: There's a real chance the ZAP Model is too bullish on Jordyn Tyson - but within the predictive analytics we care about, he looks the part. Outside of draft capital, Breakout Score carries the most weight in the wide receiver ZAP Model by a wide margin. Tyson's mark checks in at 95.1, the best in this year's class and good for the 99th percentile. That's largely because he produced at a high level throughout his collegiate career. As a young freshman at Colorado, Tyson led the team in receiving in just nine games. A devastating knee injury cut that season short and wiped out his second year, but he bounced back in Year 3 with a prorated adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt rate of 3.58 - the best top-season mark in this class. This is a rare kind of Breakout Score. Only nine ZAP Model wide receivers drafted in the top 20 have posted a mark above 90: Mike Evans, Sammy Watkins, Amari Cooper, Corey Davis, CeeDee Lamb, Ja'Marr Chase, Chris Olave, Jaxon Smith-Njigba, and Malik Nabers. That's elite company - and Tyson got there as a four-year player, a distinction only Olave and Davis share. The elephant in the room is the injury history. There's the knee, a broken collarbone, and a hamstring issue. You can subjectively ding his ZAP score because of it, but he's already returned from the knee and collarbone injuries, and the hamstring appears to be behind him. Some of the peripherals aren't perfect, either. Tyson has a bit of a yards-after-the- catch problem, and per PFF, he ranks in just the 7th percentile in avoided tackles per reception. That's not disqualifying - players like Chris Olave and DeVonta Smith graded poorly there, too - but it's not a plus. The Saints selected Tyson eighth overall, and it's a solid landing spot. He can line up all over the formation - he posted a 31% career slot rate in college - and, as Matt Harmon has shown with Reception Perception, Tyson thrives against zone coverage. Olave wins against man. That combination could stress defenses, and with little proven depth behind them, both receivers have a path to meaningful volume. Kellen Moore's offenses have also tended to play fast, which helps on the volume front. As an Olave truther, it's wild to say this, but Tyson has a path to eventually being the more impactful fantasy player. It probably won't happen right away, but the ceiling is there. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 36
players.etr_notes: Injury history is somewhat concerning, but still remarkably young after 4 years of college play.
players.elevator_pitch:
Considered one of the best, if not the best, wide receiver prospects in this draft class among much of 
the fanbase and media, Tyson offers big-play vertical prowess, promising route skills, and flashy athletic ability at the catch 
point. 
There's a lot to like. Tyson is acrobatic, fluid, and a dynamic mover with the ball in his hands. 
76
players.pre_draft_advice:
Tyson is a first-round pick in rookie drafts. There's a boom-bust volatility embedded into 
his grade relative to the receivers ranked ahead of him due to his injuries and more importantly his inconsistent execution 
of skills required of pro receivers. 
It's most likely that any issues from Tyson's bouts with lackluster technique won't hold him back from becoming an 
impactful NFL receiver. However, it's this lackluster work that could be an additional layer that potentially bogs down 
Tyson's development path that the other players lack. 
Tyson will be WR1 on some boards. He'll likely have a fair share of WR1 picks embedded in his ADP. I'd rather risk him at 
WR3 as my earliest pick. That's my line of demarcation. Good luck determining yours.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Tyson’s injury history is concerning, but on the field, he is a sleek, twitched-up athlete who can create his own separation and win outside his frame at the catch point. He projects as an NFL starter (inside or outside), with a skill set that reminds me of Stefon Diggs.

players.name: Fernando Mendoza
players.etr_notes: Premium setup with Klint Kubiak, Brock Bowers, and Ashton Jeanty.
players.elevator_pitch:
Smart, aggressive, and an accurate downfield creator who can maneuver a pocket, Mendoza is one of the best 
quarterbacks I've scouted during the past 5-7 years. Mendoza and Caleb Williams have nearly identical grades although their playing styles, 
strengths, and weaknesses are different. 
Williams is an aspiring Aaron Rodgers in Rodgers' prime. Mendoza is a mashup of Andrew Luck and Justin Herbert. 
For the casual football fan who follows a few college teams or reads college football previews during the summer, Mendoza seemingly came 
out of nowhere like his Indiana team to become one of the hottest commodities in the game. It gives Mendoza the vibe of a one-year 
wonder. 
Joe Burrow had this exact vibe from the public at LSU. Both players started with different teams the year before, but both teams lost 
enough games to avoid the limelight. Burrow lost three games at Ohio State, which is below expectations for one of the richest teams in 
talent and expectations in college football. 
Mendoza lost six games starting for the far less talented Cal Bears. Even so, all six losses were by a total margin of 17 points - five of those 
losses were by a margin of 9 points. I studied Mendoza at Cal and Indiana, and there's no mistaking him for a one-year wonder. 
In addition to his ability to execute a passing game at a high level, Mendoza is an underrated runner who can move the chains with his 
acceleration, and he has enough speed to generate big plays. He's not a breakaway runner, but he's going to have runs where he's gaining 
more than 5-15 yards. His burst and power through contact is underrated. 
Mendoza is a technically and conceptually sound quarterback who can play from center, Pistol, or shotgun. His drop game is advanced, and 
he has a full complement of play fakes. His range of accuracy and with good velocity is 55-60 yards. He can drive the deep post 56 yards 
from his release point - even with pressure bearing down. 
Mendoza is patient with unblocked edge pressure as he rolls toward it. He relies on good footwork to earn the separation and a passing 
lane. 
Mendoza is willing to take a hit to throw the ball downfield. He's mobile enough to roll to the opposite side of a compressed pocket (but no 
pressure) after cycling through three covered reads - downfield, intermediate, and check-down - and finds a one-on-one to target - 
drawing penalties in the process. 
When unblocked edge pressure is heading where Mendoza is rolling, Mendoza decelerates well enough to create inches of separation that's 
necessary for him to climb past and deliver a vertical-range accuracy that's catchable at 28-30 yards to the boundary. 
He climbs quickly from bookend pressure and when there's more pressure as he resets to fire, Mendoza is quick enough to flush and then 
break the pocket for a significant gain. He has climbed and rolled from pressure, reset, and delivered with deep accuracy. Climbing is 
Mendoza's default reaction to pressure, which is an excellent trait even if he finds himself retreating after his first climb step due to 
secondary pressure in his path. 
Mendoza manipulates coverage with pump fakes, shoulder fakes, opening his body to one side of the field, and with his eyes. Mendoza will 
even take the extra step to manipulate the defense when considering a check-down. He'll look at the shallow route, come of it to look 
deeper to hold the flat defender, and then target the shallow receiver. 
You'll see Mendoza take unnecessary risks with situational game management or underestimate coverage like the rest of the prospects. 
What sets Mendoza apart from most is his ability to continue playing aggressive football while learning from the mistake. 
Some of this aggression is due to his offense forcing the issue. A lot of Mendoza's bad decisions and sacks come on plays with long-
developing routes and no check-downs where he's forced to buy time until the shortest route breaks open. You hope that with time and the 
right offense, Mendoza will develop the skill to adjust pre-snap and have built-in hots. 
I expect he will. As was the case with Williams, Mendoza is headed to a bad team. Don't expect a rookie season like Justin Herbert's. 
Mendoza will likely have a mistake-filled rookie year on a team with a bad offensive line and limited or still-developing skill talent. 
He's good enough to survive the situation and the media onslaught that will question by Week 10 whether Mendoza even was good enough 
to be born onto this earth. As long as Tom Brady makes good enough decisions as part of the Raiders to build a good team around 
Mendoza, we should see him develop into a top-10 producer at the position with even better numbers if the front office improves the 
surrounding talent. 
Drops: Mendoza performs 1- , 2-, 3-, 5- and 7-step drops from shotgun and pistol. He'll also set up without a drop, execute a half-roll and 
then reset and fire. He embeds a partial roll away from the pressure with his three-step drop. He does this when he spots blitzes or when he 
has a pass rusher facing mismatch and he wants to embed more time from the potential pressure. 
47
players.pre_draft_advice:
Mendoza is a top five selection in this draft, regardless of the format. He's arguably the top selection in a 
Super Flex format. Like Caleb Williams, Mendoza can handle a bad organization for the first 1-2 years, muddle through it, and still grow 
from the experience.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Mendoza will need to prove himself with more full-field reads in the NFL, but his intuition, throwing accuracy and play confidence are superpowers and will smooth his transition to the next level. He projects as a mid-level NFL starter and a more mobile version of Bernie Kosar.

players.name: Carnell Tate
players.lateround_profile: We've seen Chris Olave, Garrett Wilson, Jaxon Smith-Njigba, Marvin Harrison Jr., and Emeka Egbuka go in Round 1 out of Ohio State over the last five years. Now we can add Carnell Tate to that list. Tate went fourth overall to Tennessee, and while that draft capital is as strong as any Buckeye receiver in this run, his ZAP score ranks second-lowest among them, ahead of only Egbuka. That's not necessarily a knock - his 65.3 Breakout Score sits in the 87th percentile - it's just that the others brought slightly stronger analytical profiles to the table. The biggest concern with Tate is volume, which obviously matters for fantasy. Yes, he played alongside Jeremiyah Smith, but his best-season target share still maxed out at just 21.7%. Among first-round wide receivers since 2018 (36 entering this year), that number only beats Henry Ruggs, Jaylen Waddle, Kadarius Toney, Jameson Williams, and Matthew Golden. It doesn't stop there. Tate earned a target on fewer than 20% of his routes run across his collegiate career. Among that same first-round cohort since 2018, the only others to hit that low of a targets per route run rate are Ruggs, Williams, Brian Thomas, Xavier Legette, Ricky Pearsall, Golden, and Travis Hunter. It's not all doom and gloom, though. Top-10 draft capital matters - a lot. Since 2011, wide receivers drafted in the top half of Round 1 who played at least three seasons have averaged a best-season output of 13.9 PPR points per game within their first three years. When you filter for players with Breakout Scores above 60, that jumps to 15.0. That gives Tate a strong, high-end median outcome. The landing spot in Tennessee isn't bad, either. Cam Ward is still a bit of a question mark, but the arm talent is there. Tate wins downfield and in contested situations - his collegiate contested catch rate ranked in the 98th percentile among ZAP Model wideouts since 2020. That should mesh well with Ward. And there's a path to volume. The Titans added Wan'Dale Robinson this offseason, but he'll operate more underneath. Elic Ayomanor flashed as a rookie, but Tate can take over the field-stretching role right away. And Calvin Ridley - ironically one of Tate's ZAP comps - looks closer to the end than the peak at this point. All told, a ZAP score of 88.3 is really good - like, really good. He's not quite a Legendary Performer, but this is a profile you can work with, even if there are some analytical concerns. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 37
players.etr_notes: Put up great numbers while playing with a slew of NFL-ers. Chance to be the immediate go-to option as a rookie.
players.elevator_pitch:
Carnell Tate might be a fake name. I have half the mind to call for an investigation of the Ohio State 
Athletic Department, because it wouldn't surprise me if Justin Jefferson went back to school and decided to play football on 
Saturdays under the stage name of "Carnell Tate." 
Carnell Tate and Justin Jefferson weigh 195 pounds. They run excellent routes, take a physical approach as run blockers, 
and make some of the most impressive catches you'll see. 
48
players.pre_draft_advice:
There are contingents who believe Tyson is the No. 1 receiver at this point of the year. 
Others believe it's Makai Lemon. Carnell Tate is my bet as the player most likely to see his stock rise during the offseason to 
challenge for that pole position. 
If you're drafting in a startup league, Tate would be my current choice for No. 1 overall. Fernando Mendoza is a compelling 
choice in Super-Flex formats. Jeremiyah Love makes sense if you're one back away from playoff prominence that could lead 
to dominance.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Tate is a long, technically proficient receiver who can win at all three levels using

players.name: Makai Lemon
players.lateround_profile: The Amon-Ra St. Brown comp for Makai Lemon may be a little played out, but it's still a quick way to frame his ceiling in fantasy. Both are technicians with elite short- area quickness and a relentless edge. Like St. Brown, Lemon has that dog in him. And like St. Brown, Lemon lived in the slot in college. His 75.8% career slot rate ranks in the 88th percentile among ZAP Model wideouts since 2020. That's rare territory for a first-rounder, too - only Jaylen Waddle, Kadarius Toney, Treylon Burks, Jaxon Smith- Njigba, and Emeka Egbuka have cleared a 70% slot rate since 2018. Fortunately, Lemon's profile separates from someone like Toney's pretty quickly. His career yards per route run sits at 3.02 (96th percentile), his first downs per route run is also in the 96th percentile, and he actually posted the best perimeter yards per route run in this year's class. On paper, that suggests he can win outside. The caveat: we've technically seen this archetype bust before. Since 2018, nine wideouts in the ZAP Model database picked in the top-100 have paired a 65% or better career slot rate with a perimeter yards per route run of at least 3.0. Lemon clears both with room to spare. Jaxon Smith-Njigba and Drake London are in that group - great. But so are Treylon Burks, Tutu Atwell, Elijah Moore, Mecole Hardman, Malachi Corley, Rondale Moore, and Jalin Hyatt. That's a mixed bag at best. The good news is that among first-rounders, the real comps are Burks, Smith-Njigba, and London. Two of those three have become elite NFL players. Lemon's peripherals also look stronger than Burks' did coming out. The bigger question is fit in Philadelphia. Jalen Hurts hasn't historically targeted the middle of the field at a high rate, which is where Lemon would ideally thrive. And since Hurts became the starter, the Eagles haven't posted a positive pass rate over expected in any season - volume isn't guaranteed. Philadelphia also drafted pass-catching tight end Eli Stowers in Round 2, and he'll likely occupy a similar area of the field as Lemon, limiting target upside. This pick does strongly signal that AJ Brown is on the way out, though. Lemon could step in as a top-two target alongside DeVonta Smith immediately. The pairing makes sense on paper, since Smith is a great field-stretcher and Lemon can win underneath. We just need the offense to evolve a bit for Lemon to truly hit, and that's no guarantee - it'd be nice to have a cleaner landing spot for such a talent. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 38
players.etr_notes: 3-year WR with a breakout. The AJB replacement, but doesn't play anything like him. Concerned about fit with Hurts.
players.elevator_pitch:
NFL Draft season -- and arguably the NFL Draft itself -- is a football beauty pageant that becomes 
hyper-focused on factors that aren't as important as the public, analysts, and teams make them. Some of you will argue 
that draft capital plays out and is an indicator of talent. 
Draft capital is a rigged game. Although considered a top, if not the top, wide receiver prospect in the 2026 NFL Draft, 
Makai Lemon does not fit the classic archetype of the NFL Draft season's beauty pageant contestant. 
53
players.pre_draft_advice:
I expect Makai Lemon will remain an early-round prospect through "draft season," but it 
won't shock me if his workout metrics don't thrill in the swimsuit competition of the Underwear Olympics. Harold Fannin Jr. 
was a beloved prospect until the Combine Accountants got a hold of his numbers and didn't understand the operational 
context of his skills. 
The Fannin love disappeared overnight, except for those who understood how to balance film with the metrics. Fast forward 
to today, and Fannin might be the best part of the Browns' lowly passing offense. 
If Makai Lemon's draft capital takes a hit, buy into the fear. You'll be happy you did.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Lemon doesn’t wow with his size or athletic profile, but he is a smooth, manipulative route runner and catches everything thrown his way. Similar in ways to fellow former Trojans receiver Amon-Ra St. Brown, he already plays like a pro and projects as an NFL starter (Z or slot).

players.name: KC Concepcion
players.lateround_profile: The fantasy football world probably won't love KC Concepcion landing in Cleveland, but there were definitely worse spots. The Browns have a beatable depth chart, even with Denzel Boston, so Concepcion could realistically lead the team in target share as early as this season. And new head coach Todd Monken is coming off a three-year run as Baltimore's offensive coordinator, where he helped Zay Flowers produce multiple fantasy-relevant campaigns. Flowers, like Concepcion, was a versatile playmaker in college who didn't fit the traditional NFL wide receiver mold. And make no mistake: the ZAP Model likes Concepcion more as a prospect than it liked Flowers. Concepcion is an early declare with a 54.3 Breakout Score (78th percentile), while Flowers posted a lower mark as a four-year player. For a first-round wideout, though, Concepcion's Breakout Score sits right on the line between hit and miss. Among back-half-of-the-first-round receivers in ZAP Model history, nine have posted a score between 45 and 65. That group includes AJ Jenkins, Nelson Agholor, Phillip Dorsett, Laquon Treadwell, and Jalen Reagor - but also Calvin Ridley, Brandon Aiyuk, Zay Flowers, and Brian Thomas. That range alone doesn't give us a clear answer. Other factors help. One is age - as an early declare, Concepcion still isn't 22 years old. Another is versatility, specifically his rushing usage, which feeds into the model. Among 10 wideouts since 2011 in Concepcion's age bucket who were drafted between Picks 16 and 50 and saw their ZAP scores boosted by rushing production, all but two have produced at least one 10-PPR-point-per-game season. The exceptions are Jalen Reagor and Luther Burden, and Burden's only played one NFL season so far. Three players in that cohort - Brandin Cooks, DJ Moore, and Jordan Addison - delivered fringe WR1 seasons, with Robert Woods and Wan'Dale Robinson also in the mix. There are real concerns, though. Concepcion never posted a dominant peak in college - his best season in receiving yards per team pass attempt was just 2.25. Prior to this year's draft, first-round wideouts in the ZAP Model database who failed to hit 2.50 include Henry Ruggs, Kadarius Toney, Zay Flowers, Ricky Pearsall, and Matthew Golden. Concepcion also ran over 70% of his routes from the slot in college, but I'd be less concerned with that given his 2025 campaign with Texas A&M saw him line up in the slot at a 34% rate. And that's when we saw him have his most productive season. This isn't a flawless profile. There are signals pointing in both directions. But the combination of versatility and age-adjusted production gives him a legitimate path to becoming a meaningful NFL and fantasy contributor. Even in Cleveland. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 39
players.etr_notes: Electric playmaker in space. Should be the top WR fairly quickly.
players.elevator_pitch:
Concepcion is a savvy receiver who can excel at flanker or the slot, and he also delivers in the return 
game. He has some of the best releases from the line of scrimmage in this class because of the range of moves he offers 
and the expertise in his delivery. 
Concepcion understands how to incorporate pacing, violence, and subtlety with paired movements to set up breaks during 
his stems. His breaks are technically sound in both the short and intermediate ranges with timing routes. 
65
players.pre_draft_advice:
Concepcion is a first-round value in rookie drafts. While I'd rather see him slide to the late 
turn of the first and second rounds because of his lapses as a pass-catcher, I'd live with him in the middle of the first round.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Concepcion must cut down on the drops and fine-tune areas of his game, but he is a versatile playmaker with an innate feel for getting open before the catch and creating after it. He has the receiving talent to become a starting slot or Z while also providing value as a punt returner.

players.name: Jadarian Price
players.lateround_profile: Jadarian Price isn't going to look great in any analytical prospect model. He played behind Jeremiyah Love in college, so his production profile is heavily suppressed - like, really suppressed. Price finished with a best-season reception share of just 2.8%. In ZAP Model history, 18 backs have fallen below the 3% threshold, and none became fantasy-relevant. Even if you expand that to backs below 5%, there are 46 of them, and only Derrick Henry produced a double-digit PPR points-per-game season within his first three years. Unsurprisingly, Price also posted low marks in adjusted yards per team play (10th percentile) and adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt (12th percentile). In most cases, this is a profile you'd fade. But 32nd overall draft capital keeps him on the radar. Since 2011, 20 running backs have been selected between Picks 20 and 40. Nine of them - nearly half - produced at least 14 PPR points per game in one of their first three seasons. Three of those backs were drafted just last year: TreVeyon Henderson and Quinshon Judkins didn't get there, while Omarion Hampton did. The landing spot in Seattle also creates early opportunity. Zach Charbonnet is expected to miss time after a late-season ACL tear, which could open the door for Price right away. And with running backs, talent isn't always everything - if a coaching staff wants to feed a player, they'll feed him. It's probably still worthwhile to temper expectations. Seattle ran hot last year and still ranked just 12th in running back PPR scoring, and they frustratingly split the backfield. In 16 games with Kenneth Walker and Charbonnet active together, Walker led with a 43.2% rushing share to Charbonnet's 38.8% - 12.8 to 11.5 carries per game. Meanwhile, Charbonnet handled about 73% of the team's goal-line work, leading to 11.8 expected touchdowns versus 4.7 for Walker. There are also legitimate concerns from film evaluators - Price has question marks in pass protection and the receiving game. If he can't carve out a role as a pass-catcher, it's fair to ask whether his ceiling is much different from what Walker gave fantasy managers for stretches of 2025. If you're in an active league, Price becomes more interesting. An immediate workload could boost his short-term value and give you a window to capitalize in the dynasty market. Just don't get carried away - this profile comes with clear red flags. Or, at least, it comes with a ton of volatility. Draft capital and teammate score are doing a lot of the heavy lifting. You could argue they're doing almost all of it. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 84
players.etr_notes: Will have a chance to dominate the backfield early-on as a rookie.
players.elevator_pitch:
The talented sidekick to a star player in a backfield often gets a pre-draft bump from media 
evaluators. Often, it has proven unwarranted. I don't know if I have a good feel for the dynamics driving this bias, but Felix 
Jones, Roschon Johnson, and Sony Michel come to mind. 
Jadarian Price could join this list, although I'm inclined to believe Price will transcend it. I don't take many liberties with the 
RSP's grading system. There's only one point separating RB2-RB6 on the board, and they are all in the same tier. 
There are projected improvements baked into the scoring system already, but I tend to underestimate the value of those 
likely improvements, because it's my grading philosophy to make a player prove what he can do instead of presuming he 
will. 
In Price's case, a player who is slated to have a higher NFL Draft capital than most of the names above him on the RSP's 
board, I have no problem endorsing Price as one of your top three RBs. He'll get an early opportunity due to the capital and 
that will facilitate experience and potentially quicker growth. 
Price is a scheme versatile runner who doesn't take creative liberties that could hurt the offensive game script, but he is 
adept at finding cutback lanes, and he's creative in space. 
Price has mobile hips, he adjusts stride length and pace to set up rushing lanes, he keeps his knees and feet high as he runs 
past low shots, and he drops his weight deep enough for strong deceleration. Pace also possesses curvilinear speed so he 
doesn't have to make hard cuts with every run. 
Price must keep his feet tighter when setting up moves while tight to the line otherwise, he doesn't operate from a place of 
balance, and it limits his skills. 
Price has the speed, acceleration, power, and ability to bounce off glancing shots like a starting NFL runner. 
Other than his occasional lapses with his wide stance, Price has all the tools necessary for him to develop into a high-end 
contributor. This includes blocking, routes, and catching the ball. All three have minor issues that need refining, but they 
aren't fatal flaw on their own. 
If Price can add 10-15 pounds to his frame and either maintain or increase his speed, he'll eventually start in the NFL. If 
Price doesn't add the muscle, he could still deliver as a productive RB2 in an offense. 
Vision: Price reads the leverage of a double team and the unblocked end and sticks tight to the interior block to maximize 
his space away from the unblocked end. He identifies interior penetration as he's taking the exchange and finds the bounce 
or cutback lane. 
Price sets up zone and gap blocking effectively. He finds cutback lanes between the tackles and hits the decisively. 
Price doesn't try reversals of field, taking what the design and the defense gives him if there are no nearby cut back or 
bounce lanes. He's creative in the open field and finds cutback lanes in the second and third level for big plays. 
Still, Price doesn't take unnecessary risks and manages the game well. He identifies good leverage in dense traffic and hits 
the crease decisively. 
84
players.pre_draft_advice:
Although Price's ranking is slightly lower than RBs 2-5, his ceiling is as high and his 
opportunity due to NFL Draft capital will be better. If you want to consider Price as a top three RB pick, I have no argument 
with you. He'll likely find his way there in the RSP Post-Draft if he earns the draft capital and a good fit.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Price isn’t a proven workhorse, but he has NFL starting-caliber talent, with his natural instincts and contact balance. Ball-security issues and a lack of pass-catching production will be areas he needs to address during the draft process.

players.name: Kenyon Sadiq
players.lateround_profile: Kenyon Sadiq became just the seventh tight end drafted 16th or earlier in the NFL Draft since 2011, placing him in rarefied air. The others: Eric Ebron, TJ Hockenson, Kyle Pitts, Brock Bowers, Tyler Warren, and Colston Loveland. Each logged at least one season with 9.3 PPR points per game or more within their first three NFL seasons - a 90th- percentile max early-career season in the database at a minimum. Warren and Loveland have played just one season, too. This group also owns a 54% rate of top-12 seasons in that early-career window while averaging an 18.9% target share. Sadiq's comps look underwhelming, though, so it's worth revisiting how productive Fant, Howard, and Engram actually were early in their careers - B2S scores of 10.2, 9.6, and 12.6, respectively, all 94th-percentile or better. Even Kyle Pitts, perhaps the most obvious "legendary" recent prospect, logged a 93rd-percentile B2S of 9.6 despite a slow start. You can cherry-pick nominal legends like George Kittle (15.6), Mark Andrews (13.2), and Travis Kelce (11.6), but none were even top-60 NFL Draft picks - they simply hit bigger than most tight ends do. Sadiq is a better prospect than any of them. The truth is that there are only a handful of difference-making tight ends every decade, and Sadiq has the building blocks to be one. Sadiq played three seasons at Oregon, running behind significant NFL-caliber talent before breaking out in Year 3 with 51 receptions, a school record at the position. He then broke the tight end record in the 40-yard dash (4.39 seconds) at the Combine - and was drafted just after turning 21. Among first-round tight ends drafted before age 22 in the ZAP Model, even the weakest max early-career season (David Njoku, 29th overall pick) ranked in the 87th percentile. The landing spot with the Jets isn't ideal for reaching a huge offensive ceiling, and for anyone concerned about the production profile for Sadiq, it's a justifiable out. Frank Reich, the team's new coordinator, has historically been friendly to the position, and across 10 seasons where he was a team's coordinator or head coach, his squads have just once ranked outside the top 20 in tight end target share, while ranking in the top five four times. The draft capital, combined with his age-adjusted profile and athleticism, gives Sadiq one of the best ZAP scores the position has ever seen. The model isn't projecting peak Kittle performance in Year 1. It's telling us that Sadiq certainly belongs in the high-end tier of tight end prospects in recent years.
