players.etr_notes: Played in-line under 30% of the time in 2025, likely more receiver than tight end.
players.elevator_pitch:
Sadiq lit up the NFL Combine with speed, vertical leaping, and bench press performances. He's an explosive athlete, 
which is what NFL teams seek as potential compensatory factors for a short and light move tight end. 
When you click on the highlights at the end of Sadiq's scouting report you'll see displays of speed and acrobatic catches. When Sadiq 
is playing to his potential, he can change the game with his big-play skills. He's essentially a big vertical receiver who can draw 
mismatches from H-Back alignments. 
33
players.pre_draft_advice:
Pre-draft, I'd take a chance on Sadiq if I can get him in the late half of the second round in a 1.5 
PPR format. Ideally, I'd prefer the third, but that may not prove realistic due to the buzz surrounding his athletic ability. It might be 
best to avoid most tight ends until we see their post-draft landing spots and focus on receivers and backs.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Sadiq has mismatch-creating potential in the NFL, because of his ability to out-quick and out-tough defenders as both a pass catcher and blocker. He projects as a versatile, Vernon Davis-like weapon and has his best football ahead of him.

players.name: Omar Cooper
players.lateround_profile: The best tackle-breaker in this wide receiver class is probably Omar Cooper. He avoided an absurd 0.31 tackles per reception across his career, a 97th percentile mark among ZAP Model wideouts since 2020. His efficiency profile is solid, too. Cooper posted a 79th percentile career yards per route run and an 82nd percentile first downs per route run. He split time between the slot and perimeter - a 48% career slot rate, per PFF - but was similarly effective in both alignments. The analytical concern comes from his trajectory. Cooper is a four-year player who didn't produce early, finishing with a 41.7 Breakout Score (55th percentile). That's not bad, but for a first-rounder, it's concerning. Of the 14 first-round wide receivers in the ZAP database with a Breakout Score below 50, just one has posted a 14-plus PPR points-per-game season within his first three years. And it was Kelvin Benjamin. To be fair, with 30th overall draft capital, Cooper is just as similar to earlier second-round picks as he is some first-round ones. And once you move into that range, the Breakout Score hit rate improves. Rashee Rice is a strong comp, and Deebo Samuel is another - Cooper is smaller, but several of his production metrics line up favorably with Samuel's collegiate profile. There's some minor versatility here, too. Cooper logged 97 rushing yards and a pair scores over his final two seasons and even returned a few kicks as a freshman at Indiana. He's unlikely to have that role in the NFL, but it's at least a signal of his athleticism. The bigger issue is landing spot. Cooper goes to the Jets - and, well, it's the Jets. Garrett Wilson is already a target hog there, and the team also spent a first-round pick on tight end Kenyon Sadiq. Geno Smith is projected to start in 2026, and while he should improve behind a better offensive line compared to last year, this still isn't an offense you're banking on for volume or efficiency. Cooper's profile looks more like a second-rounder than a true first, but it's workable. The concern is the short-term outlook - both in terms of earning targets and the quality of those targets. He just doesn't have the same kind of allure as the top-four wide receivers in this class. Analyticaly, at least. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 40
players.etr_notes: Explosive athlete, but a bit crowded right now. Jets have the resources to secure a long-term QB.
players.elevator_pitch:
I've heard in multiple spaces that the popular comparison for Cooper is Deebo Samuel. This is a 
figurative comparison rooted in playing style and how that might project to an NFL scheme if the player is used the same 
way they were in college. 
Cooper is a physical runner after the catch, and he makes athletic plays at the catch point. Samuel does the same. When 
you make a more literal comparison of them physically before going straight to playing style and scheme, you uncover 
factors that might make it a misleading comparison. 
87
players.pre_draft_advice:
If Cooper becomes a Deebo Samuel clone it means he struggled and failed to become a 
strong route runner. I don't think this going to happen. Consider Cooper at the turn of the first and second round.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Cooper will be an attractive draft target for the NFL teams that put high value on ball skills and run-after-catch ability. He projects as an inside-outside receiver who can be weaponized when featured.

players.name: Ty Simpson
players.etr_notes: Blocked for the forseeable future, but the potential future QB for Sean McVay is intriguing.
players.elevator_pitch:
Simpson has as much potential to become a good NFL starter as any quarterback from the 2025 class. He would have 
edged Cam Ward for the No. 1 spot if Simpson had this grade last year and declared for the draft. Simpson would have been QB3 for the 
2024 RSP, just a sliver ahead of Bo Nix. 
Simpson has a good arm, he maneuvers pressure well, and he follows up with strong accuracy in the short, intermediate, and often in the 
vertical ranges of the field after avoiding defenders. Other than some leaning through his weight transfer during releases that might impact 
his post routes, Simpson has clean enough mechanics to start in the NFL and refine them as he goes. 
Simpson's arm strength is good enough to make all the throws one would reasonably expect from a starting quarterback. It's not a plus-
arm, but it's better than average. He can drive the ball 50-55 yards on the deep post with pinpoint accuracy. Beyond that, the accuracy and 
velocity become erratic. 
Simpson is a capable scrambler who can break the pocket and earn chain-moving gains. He's agile enough to extend some of these gains 
for big plays. He's not a breakaway threat, so we're talking about 15-25 yards. 
Then NFL will likely regard Simpson as a first-year starter and co-starring with Fernando Mendoza on the draft-day billing. This reminds me 
of the 2009 NFL Draft when Mark Sanchez was the co-star with Matthew Stafford. 
Just as I thought Stafford was the clear No. 1 option in that QB class, there's a healthy gap separating Mendoza from Simpson for me. The 
differentiating factors are Simpson's issues with reading/processing coverage and situational issues with his pocket clock and reactions to 
pressure. 
51
players.pre_draft_advice:
Simpson is a second-round value in one-QB drafts and a mid-to-late first-round option in Super Flex. If 
Simpson goes in the NFL Draft before Mendoza or in the same range, he'll be overpriced.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Simpson has the command and process of an NFL quarterback, but he needs valuable experience to be more efficient in his reads, and to better understand what is open and what isn’t. He projects as a low-level NFL starter, with the ceiling of a mid-level starter and floor of a backup (reminiscent of Daniel Jones with lesser physical traits).

players.name: Denzel Boston
players.lateround_profile: The expectation was that Denzel Boston would be a first-round pick. He didn't get that top-32 capital, but he still landed early in Round 2 with Cleveland. Boston spent all four years at Washington, sharing the field with Rome Odunze, Jalen McMillan, and Ja'Lynn Polk during his first two seasons. Naturally, his production was limited during that stretch. Even accounting for that, his eventual breakout was a bit underwhelming. In 2024, Boston posted a 2.00 receiving yards per team pass attempt rate. That climbed to 2.58 the following season - 2.75 when adjusted for strength of schedule. The result was a 43.8 Breakout Score, which sits around the 60th percentile. That's more acceptable for a second-rounder than a mid-first-round prospect, where some projected him back in March. But you'd still prefer to see a stronger mark. Beyond Breakout Score, the analytical profile doesn't offer much help. Boston's career yards per route run is just 2.02, a 48th percentile figure. Since 2020, 29 Day 2 wide receivers have posted a career mark below 2.20. Only Nico Collins and Jayden Reed from that group managed to score more than 13 PPR points per game in one of their first three seasons. The landing spot doesn't do him many favors, either. Cleveland used a first-round pick on the more well-rounded KC Concepcion, and Boston will have to compete with him throughout his rookie contract. It's also an offense with a featured tight end and, most likely, an unsettled quarterback situation. Boston would've been an easy fade had he gone in Round 1, given where his rookie draft cost would've landed. Now, the market will probably be less aggressive. Just understand that this is a highly volatile profile. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 42
players.etr_notes: Big-bodied target with great redzone ability.
players.elevator_pitch:
Boston is a skilled boundary receiver with excellent acceleration for his size that gets his open early in 
a route and then he uses his size, physicality, and knowledge of positioning at (and after) the catch point to impose his will 
on defenders earn the sideline. Depending on how well Boston develops his route game, he could aspire to become a 
featured option like Nico Collins or he develops into a skilled secondary sidekick like Tee Higgins. The variable is how much 
Boston adds and improves to his promising game. 
115
players.pre_draft_advice:
A quality pick in the late half of the first or early second rounds in rookie drafts. Josh 
Cameron, Germie Bernard, Antonio Wiliams, and Chis Bell have higher ceilings, but Boston's floor is safer. There's a good 
argument that the only safer prospects ranked ahead of Boston are Carnell Tate and Makai Lemon. 
So, if you find that the RSP's ranking is lower on Boston than the consensus, remember that Boston is in the same tier as 
WR6-WR13 and 3.5 points separate him from the WR4 on the board - which is the value of an upside bonus in the RSP's 
Receiving category. If you want safety over upside and still get a high floor and high ceiling, I wouldn't begrudge you 
considering Boston as your WR3-WR5.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Boston builds his speed with smooth, long strides and naturally adjusts with the hand strength of a ball winner, which should quickly earn him the trust of an NFL quarterback. With his ability to win inside or outside, his game has shades of 2025 Houston Texans draft pick Jayden Higgins.

players.name: Eli Stowers
players.lateround_profile: Eli Stowers was getting steam entering the NFL Draft, and his consensus big board rank was nearing the first round by late April. He ended up going 54th overall, a little later than expected, which led to a larger draft capital differential between Stowers and Kenyon Sadiq than we may have anticipated. That's relevant because, throughout the pre- draft process, Stowers and Sadiq became linked, as the former's profile really started to shine. The buzz for Stowers really ramped up after the NFL Combine. Stowers' 4.51-second 40-yard dash was tied for the second-best at the combine at the position, trailing Sadiq's 4.39. Additionally, Stowers broke the all-time vertical leap record and the tight end broad jump record, too. So, while Sadiq had the best straight-line speed we'd ever seen at tight end, Stowers stole the show overall. Stowers also had a better Breakout Score (66.5, 91st percentile) than Sadiq (62.3, 86th percentile). All things considered, they're both elite athletes with high-end age-adjusted production. The draft capital difference between the two is relevant and hard to brush off completely. Of course, Stowers is still in the Elite Producer category, and it's also a little easier to like his landing spot in terms of his long-term outlook. Incumbent tight end Dallas Goedert is set to be a free agent next season, and Stowers should be able to play alongside him anyway because he projects as a "big receiver" more than he does an inline tight end. Stowers is a playmaker. He recorded a first down on 12.4% of his routes, via PFF, in his college career. That puts him third on a list of Harold Fannin, (coincidentally) Goedert, Mark Andrews, and Tucker Kraft among Day 2 picks since 2014. Last year, the Eagles were a top-12 team in 12 personnel usage, and Stowers brings a different profile to the position than Goedert. The more notable pairing might actually be with the team's first-round wide receiver draft pick, Makai Lemon. Lemon played a lot in the slot at USC, and Jalen Hurts has historically thrown more to the sidelines than to the middle of the field. Still, only three other tight ends in the ZAP Model database had top-64 draft capital, a Breakout Score over 65, and a height-adjusted Speed Score over 115: Kyle Pitts, Noah Fant, and Evan Engram. All were first-round NFL Draft picks. LLaattee--RRoouunndd FFaannttaassyy FFoooottbbaallll:: 22002266 PPrroossppeecctt GGuuiiddee JJuummpp TToo:: ZZAAPP MMooddeell || YYeeaarr 22 MMooddeell 110066
players.etr_notes: Impressive Combine even though he is probably is not a traditional TE. Long-term answer here.
players.elevator_pitch:
Formerly the No.12 high school quarterback in the country, Stowers had offers to play quarterback for UGA, LSU, 
Clemson, and Auburn. A knee injury during his last game as a high school junior began a series of events that changed his future. 
Stowers wound up at New Mexico State as their future quarterback. Instead, Stowers tore the labrum of his throwing arm in 2021. 
23
players.pre_draft_advice:
Stowers is technically an instant starter with his RSP grade, but he's still on the cusp of a rotational 
talent. His landing spot will dictate if he's an immediate impact player. He's the safest move tight end on the board, but is 2026 the 
most dangerous year for the offensive trends to work away from the move tight end? Probably not, but 2-3 years from now, Stowers 
might find his production declining if these changes take root.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Stowers will need to prove himself as a serviceable blocker at the NFL level, but he is explosive as a pass catcher, and I love the way he maximizes his catch radius. He has mismatch-creating potential and can eventually develop into an NFL starter.

players.name: Chris Bell
players.lateround_profile: Some respected draft analysts believe Chris Bell has first-round talent, and that a late-season ACL tear was the main reason he didn't get that kind of capital. The film is enticing - he's built like AJ Brown, and the upside is obvious. Seeing him fall to Pick 94 is at least a little concerning. That said, among the run of Round 3 wide receivers in this class, Bell offers one of the better analytical profiles. It's not elite, but his 48.2 Breakout Score lands in the 68th percentile - and it tops every other Day 2 wideout in this class. Historically, this combination of size and Breakout Score has been boom-or-bust. In the ZAP Model database, 10 wide receivers drafted after Round 1 and before Pick 100 weighed at least 215 pounds and posted a Breakout Score between 40 and 60. That list includes Courtland Sutton, DK Metcalf, Michael Pittman, and Tee Higgins - but also Greg Little, Devin Funchess, Dorial Green-Beckham, Chase Claypool, and Jonathan Mingo. This is a classic swing-for-the-fences profile. Bell spent all four seasons at Louisville, with production climbing each year: 0.29, 1.00, 1.73, and 2.59 in receiving yards per team pass attempt. That steady progression - along with a tougher senior-year schedule - is doing a lot of the work in his Breakout Score. One area of concern is how he earned his targets. Bell finished with a 57% short target rate, meaning most of his looks came within 10 yards of the line of scrimmage. At the same time, his contested target rate was in the 92nd percentile. He converted those opportunities well - his contested catch rate was in the 86th percentile - but that combination can point to separation issues. And if that's the case, you're looking at more of a Jonathan Mingo outcome than a DK Metcalf one. The landing spot in Miami is a bit tricky. In some ways, it might've been better for Bell to land on a deeper roster, where he could ease back from the ACL and develop during his rookie year. Instead, he now may see the field relatively early with the Dolphins, despite still being somewhat of a developmental player. Even if the ZAP score doesn't fully reflect it, Bell offers more upside than his new teammate Caleb Douglas. There's just more to work with here, even if the floor is really low. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 51
players.etr_notes: Likely won't do much as a rookie, but lands with a team who can afford to be patient.
players.elevator_pitch:
If there's a player in this draft where I have equal fear about passing or drafting, Chris Bell is a strong 
candidate for that designation. He may be the only candidate I feel this way about this year. 
Bell's game sears a hole into the film one moment and needs adult diapers the next. Sometimes these alternating thrills and 
horror are part of a learning process. After all, progress may appear more linear at times, but it never is. 
Add Bell's surgically reconstructed ACL to the mix - a ligament he tore late in the 2025 season - and it's just another layer 
to the internal drama surrounding Bell's game, his potential, and his timeline to make an impact in the NFL. 
92
players.pre_draft_advice:
If Bell falls to the second round in fantasy drafts, he's worth the risk. You'll have to wait a 
year to see the athlete he was before his injury, so maintain realistic expectations - including a risk that Bell never returns 
to pre-injury form. This is rare with first-time ACL injuries. They are usually just a time-consuming rehab process.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Bell calls himself a “positionless player,” which is both intriguing and concerning for his projection. He needs to land in the right situation, with an NFL staff that sees his value as a catch-and-go weapon.

players.name: Germie Bernard
players.lateround_profile: Pittsburgh has needed a reliable slot option, and they got one in Alabama's Germie Bernard. Honestly, it's one of the most Steelers picks imaginable. Bernard is tough. He's a solid 206 pounds and forced missed tackles at an 84th percentile rate across his collegiate career. Coaches moved him around, too - he logged 36 rush attempts for 184 yards, including an 18-carry, 101-yard, 2-touchdown performance in 2025 at Alabama. Bernard dealt with strong competition at every stop of his career, which probably had some impact on his overall production. In 2022 at Michigan State, he played alongside Keon Coleman and Jayden Reed. In 2023 at Washington, it was Rome Odunze, Ja'Lynn Polk, Jalen McMillan, and Denzel Boston - and Bernard actually outproduced Boston in that offense. Over the last two years at Alabama, he shared the field with Ryan Williams, a probable first-rounder in 2027. Despite all that, Bernard still posted a 40.4 Breakout Score, which lands in the 52nd percentile. It's not ideal for a second-round pick, but given the context, it's at least understandable. The bull case is straightforward here: Bernard can step in as a go-to slot option and fill a needed role alongside DK Metcalf and Michael Pittman. And he may have a little bit of juice to play outside - his efficiency metrics were better on the perimeter throughout his career than in the slot. The bear case is unfortunately a little easier to make. Bernard finished with a career yards per route run below 2.0, and 22% of his targets came behind the line of scrimmage. Since 2018, the list of top-100 wide receivers with both a sub-2.0 yards per route run and a behind-the-line target rate above 20% includes Jalen Hurd, Lynn Bowden, Amari Rodgers, Velus Jones, and Kyle Williams. If you loosen the yards per route run threshold to 2.2, you get more viable names - Christian Kirk and Zay Flowers, for example - but Bernard's 1.90 career mark still raises concern. Then there's the comp list. It's light on hits, even beyond the three that are provided above. That's not everything, but it's also not something that boosts confidence. Overall, Bernard is fine. The versatility matters, but this looks like more of a higher- floor, lower-ceiling player for fantasy. Pittsburgh does have potential outs with both Metcalf and Pittman's contracts next season, though, which could make things more interesting in 2027. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 41
players.etr_notes: Looks every bit the "classic" Day 2 Steelers WR.
players.elevator_pitch:
I'm hesitant to place a receiver like Stevie Johnson or Robert Woods into a comparison spectrum for 
Germie Bernard without reminding readers that while a part of these comparisons is about talent, the point of the exercise 
is about style of play, and the players listed are checkpoints with how the player's development path could evolve, plateau, 
or regress. 
I heard from a friend that NFL.com's Lance Zierlein used Robert Woods as his comparison for Bernard. I know and respect 
Lance, and I understand how he would consider Woods because there are common elements between how Woods was used 
and the potential fit Bernard could have in an NFL offense. 
111
players.pre_draft_advice:
Bernard has a medium floor with a high ceiling - not an elite (top 3-5 at his position) 
ceiling. He's a solid second- or third-round pick in rookie drafts this year.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Bernard has a well-rounded, pro-ready skill set, with the frame, ball skills and smooth athleticism to be a dependable target. He projects as a starting Z receiver and as an NFL team’s second- or third option.

players.name: Antonio Williams
players.lateround_profile: In an underwhelming rookie class, Antonio Williams is at least somewhat interesting. Williams spent his entire collegiate career at Clemson and produced right away. As a freshman, he caught 56 passes for 604 yards and 4 touchdowns, leading the team in receiving. Clemson was fairly pass-heavy that season, though, so his 1.26 receiving yards per team pass attempt wasn't especially notable. Injuries limited him to five games as a sophomore. Then, over his final two seasons, Williams never exceeded 1.70 receiving yards per team pass attempt. Even when adjusted, his peak was just 1.82. Put differently, he was consistently productive, but never dominant. You'd like to see at least one standout season. His 38.4 Breakout Score reflects that - it's basically average. Historically, that kind of production hasn't translated well. Among Day 2 wide receivers since 2011, just 15 entered the league with a best-season adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt below 2.0. Only Terry McLaurin and Ladd McConkey have become hits from that group. And McConkey - notably Williams' top comp - was incredibly efficient on a per- route basis. Williams doesn't have that going for him, which makes the profile harder to buy. Williams ran 78% of his routes from the slot, but Clemson did use him on the ground, which gives him a small bump in the ZAP Model. Top-100 picks with that kind of versatility tend to outperform expectation at a higher rate. The landing spot helps, especially in a class like this one where there aren't obvious directions to go in rookie drafts. Washington has very little established at wide receiver outside of Terry McLaurin, who turns 31 in September. Williams has a real chance to step in as the primary slot option right away and become an easy outlet for Jayden Daniels. And maybe there's a path to more - someone like McConkey has shown that a player with a similar frame can play inside and out at the next level. I just wouldn't bet on it. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 45
players.etr_notes: Led Clemson in receiving as a true freshman. Figures to be a slot option for Washington.
players.elevator_pitch:
Jayden Reed has been an RSP fave since he was playing at Michigan State. Williams compares well to 
Reed at the same points of their careers. 
Primarily a slot receiver, Williams has the speed, leaping ability, and skill after the catch to become a productive NFL flanker 
- perhaps a leading producer in his passing game if he continues refining his skills. 
Williams' grade isn't far from a designation of an immediate starter. He's a reliable pass-catcher who wins the underneath 
zones and makes plays working across the field that leverages his skill as a ball carrier. 
101
players.pre_draft_advice:
Williams is worth consideration in the second round and a potential bargain in the third 
round. Depending on his NFL landing spot and draft capital, you could bump those valuations up by a round. 
104

players.name: Jonah Coleman
players.lateround_profile: Jonah Coleman is a true do-it-all back. He began his collegiate career at Arizona, logging over 200 combined carries across his freshman and sophomore seasons. After transferring to Washington for his final two years, he elevated his profile further. Coleman's peak in total yards per team play came as a junior, though that season included a reception share below 8%. As a senior, he put it all together, posting a similar yards- per-team-play mark while nearly doubling his prorated reception share to 13.2%. Stylistically, he looks like a powerful workhorse. According to PFF, no running back in this class forced missed tackles at a higher rate per attempt. He also ranked top-five in yards after contact per attempt and earned one of the strongest pass-blocking grades among Combine invitees. There are some questions about long speed - and he didn't test athletically - but his career explosive run rate is still above the class average. He may not be a burner, but he's not lacking big-play ability. The landing spot in Denver is especially interesting. The Broncos drafted RJ Harvey in Round 2 last year, but his rookie season was underwhelming. Per Next Gen Stats, Harvey ranked 46th in rushing yards over expected per attempt among 48 qualified running backs, and his success rate ranked 32nd - well behind J.K. Dobbins, who was 12th in the same offense. And Dobbins himself could be gone after the 2026 season. There's a real scenario where Coleman ends up being the best running back in this backfield. That doesn't mean it happens right away - or that it's a certainty - but the combination of his profile and the uncertainty around him makes it possible. If the market discounts that in rookie drafts, it's worth taking advantage. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 85
players.etr_notes: Interesting option to add to this backfield. Could be the long-term Dobbins replacement.
players.elevator_pitch:
Coleman is a nimble back with a low center of gravity. He's a patient enough zone runner, reads 
leverage well, and finds lanes to bounce or cutback. 
Coleman also manages the game well as a ballcarrier, taking what the defense gives him and limiting his riskier decisions to 
down-and-distance and game-script scenarios that are open to creative license. He's not as patient of a runner with Duo 
blocking as he is with zone. 
Once Coleman is past the line of scrimmage, he must leverage his size and get downhill. He's prone to bouncing plays 
against outside pursuit slanting inside, thinking he'll find openings against the grain of the defense's flow. Coleman is 
neither that explosive nor agile enough for this ploy. He's big and he should be sticking his foot in the ground and forcing 
the pursuit to hit him from angles that won't bring Coleman to the ground as easily. 
Coleman has enough acceleration and speed to reach the open field, but linebackers routinely catch Coleman. What would 
be a field-flipping gain for many backs is a chain-moving gain for Coleman. What would be a breakaway run of 70-80 yards 
for his peers is a field-flipper for 35-45 yards for Coleman. 
If Coleman can prove more explosive from what I have observed, he could be worth the pre-draft buzz I've seen from him 
since last summer. Based on what I've seen, Coleman's burst and speed are befitting an NFL's RB3 or RB4. 
Combine this with pass protection that lacks sound technique at the point of attack and power and contact balance that's 
contributor quality, and Coleman's acclaim doesn't match his film at this point. He's a reserve runner who will get what's 
earned and protect the ball who might develop his passing-down skills to become useful on the field. 
Vision: Coleman is a patient runner who works within 1-2 steps of his blocks. He presses advantageous leverage for the 
defender to set up the block to the favorable side for the blocker. He's willing to press within a step of a defender in his face 
to set up a block. 
Coleman must do this more often with Duo. He abandons the press too soon and it doesn't allow his other combo block to 
set before bounces or cuts back toward it. He leaves yards on the field in this situation. Overall, Coleman is a decisive Duo 
runner who reads interior penetration and finds the bounce or cutback lane. 
Coleman rides the wave well on zone runs, incrementally working his way inside-out to keep pursuit honest with their 
angles as he goes along his path. 
When Coleman reaches the flat, he tries to bounce outside flat defenders slanting inside, rather than sticking his foot 
downhill and having the leverage advantage to run through contact or even accelerate past it. In this respect, he can 
improve his decision-making with bounce-outs. 
Coleman reads leverage and penetration between the tackles and finds the bounce lane. He's effective at setting up double 
teams on inside zone and hits the crease with confidence. He's effective working with his pullers on sweeps-reading the 
heads of the defenders positioned against the blockers. 
159
players.pre_draft_advice: Monitor Coleman from afar despite your peers considering Coleman a draftable player.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Coleman falls short in terms of size and play strength but makes up for it

players.name: DeZhaun Stribling
players.lateround_profile: In the pre-draft version of the prospect guide, De'Zhaun Stribling's blurb read, "Unless draft capital surprises in a meaningful way, Stribling isn't someone you'll need to prioritize in rookie drafts." Well, about that... Stribling going 33rd overall to San Francisco was one of the biggest surprises of the draft - at least among skill position players. Because his profile, quite frankly, doesn't match that kind of capital. It may not even be a Day 2 profile. The analytics are underwhelming. He posted a 28.5 Breakout Score, driven largely by a 2024 season at Oklahoma State where his adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt barely cleared 2.0. That ended up being his peak. In 2025 at Ole Miss, that number dropped to 1.70. Across five collegiate seasons, his best mark ranks in just the 32nd percentile among ZAP Model wide receivers. If there's something working in his favor, it's after the catch. Stribling posted a 60th percentile yards-after-catch-per-reception rate, and he forced missed tackles at an above- average clip. That fits what Kyle Shanahan tends to value, and it likely played a role in the selection. And to be fair, we have seen Day 2 hits from older prospects with poor Breakout Scores - Rashee Rice, Michael Thomas, and Terry McLaurin are examples. But the difference is what's under the hood. Since 2018, 12 Day 2 wide receivers have entered the league with career yards per route run below 2.0, per PFF data. Only one of them - McLaurin - has delivered a truly fantasy-relevant season across his first three years. Stribling's career yards per route run sits at just 1.68. There may be some opportunity in San Francisco as early as 2026, but it's not a situation where you're expecting an immediate return. Mike Evans and Ricky Pearsall are in place, and Christian McCaffrey will continue to command targets out of the backfield. Maybe things open up in 2027 - Evans could be gone - but that would mean holding a shaky profile on your bench for a while. Chances are, Stribling won't go as early in rookie drafts as he did in the NFL Draft. And analytically, he shouldn't. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 43
players.etr_notes: Pick 33 felt like a massive reach in the draft, but still could be some long-term ceiling here.
players.elevator_pitch:
Well-built with straight-line speed, Stribling has the hands and power to earn the ball in tight windows 
and deliver yardage after contact. His short-range routes are crisp enough for him to earn separation and generate yards 
after the catch when there's a cushion. 
Stribling's intermediate game lacks precision as a route runner. At this point, his game is about quick-hitting passes, 
straight-line routes, and vertical shots up the rails. This has a place in the NFL. It doesn't always have a place in a starting 
lineup. 
225
players.pre_draft_advice:
Stribling will be a mid-to-late pick in fantasy drafts because there's projected draft capital 
that Stribling has starter potential. He's a bigger projection as a starter than characterized, even if there are some grains of 
truth about his game having that upside.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Stribling isn’t the most well-rounded in terms of his route success, but he is a big-bodied athlete with strong ball skills and a pro mindset. He has the talent and competitive toughness to settle into a WR2/3 role for an NFL offense.

players.name: Zachariah Branch
players.lateround_profile: Back in March, I thought Zachariah Branch might be the player I'd fade most in rookie drafts this year. But given how this class shook out, he's almost...interesting. There are some positives. Branch is an early declare, and the model gives him credit for his versatility and unique usage. He's explosive, shifty, and can operate in short areas. The negatives? There are plenty. Branch never truly produced at a high level. His raw receiving yards per team pass attempt failed to reach 2.0 in a season, and even when adjusted for strength of schedule, his peak was just 2.11. That led to a below-average 31.2 Breakout Score. The usage profile is even more concerning. A massive 45.5% of his career targets came behind the line of scrimmage. That doesn't mean he can't develop into something more, but it does mean you're betting on a significant leap. Historically, that's been a dangerous bet. With top-100 draft capital, Branch becomes the 11th wide receiver since 2020 to enter the league with a career behind-the-line target rate above 30% - and the first to exceed 40%. Only two players in that group, Wan'Dale Robinson and Luther Burden, posted Breakout Scores above 75. The rest? Lynn Bowden, Amari Rodgers, Anthony Schwartz, Kadarius Toney, Rondale Moore, Velus Jones, Tre Tucker, and Malachi Corley. A big reason for that is screen-heavy usage. Over 35% of Branch's career yards came on screens. Among top-100 picks since 2020, only two receivers have cleared 30% of their career yardage from screens: Malachi Corley and Anthony Schwartz. This is an archetype that's burned teams before. To be fair, though, the athleticism is intriguing, and the landing spot could've been worse. Atlanta's depth chart entering the draft featured Drake London (a stud), Jahan Dotson (not a stud), and Olamide Zaccheaus (not a stud). Branch will likely start in a gadget role, but it wouldn't be surprising if he earns more meaningful reps by the end of the 2026 season than he would have on most teams. Still, this is a tough profile to buy into. In a stronger class, Branch would be closer to an avoid than a target. But in this one, where you're throwing darts earlier than usual, he's at least in the mix. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 46
players.etr_notes: Screen merchant WR lands with a QB who hates to throw deep.
players.elevator_pitch:
Branch has a boom-bust profile embedded into his grade that requires an explanation. He's explosive 
enough to eventually play inside or outside in the NFL. To do both, there's some development Branch will need with his 
game. In fact, there's additional development Branch will need to play either role at a full-time level. If he doesn't handle 
his business here, his career will take the shape of Mecole Hardman's as a special teams option who can deliver as an 
offensive gadget. 
The issue is Branch's routes. They are good enough to get on the field, but not good enough remain on the field every 
down. 
124
players.pre_draft_advice:
Branch will have a volatile ADP range pre-draft. Post-draft, his draft capital could dictate 
how much of boom-bust risk he is. Leagues that offer points for returns mitigate some of the risk Branch's profile offers. 
Despite this volatility, there aren't any prospects ranked below Branch that I'd clearly take ahead of him. 
The candidates are Ja'Kobi Lane, Eric McAlister, and Bryce Lance. After the NFL Draft, Kevin Coleman might join this list.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Branch has dynamic speed and the linear twitch to threaten the defense before and after the catch, but there is a lot of good-faith projection required to envision him as more than a gadget player. He projects as a versatile slot receiver with return value (in the Rondale Moore mold).

players.name: Chris Brazzell
players.lateround_profile: Chris Brazzell slipped a bit on draft weekend, landing with Carolina at Pick 83. As a second-round prospect, there were questions. As a late Day 2 pick? The upside becomes easier to chase. Brazzell played in Tennessee's offense under Josh Heupel, which hasn't always translated cleanly to the ZAP Model. It's burned us before, so there's some inherent risk here. At 6'4", 198 pounds, Brazzell has the prototypical vertical build. He used it to stretch defenses, too. Among receivers in this class, he saw deep targets at a top-15 rate, and nearly 18% of his receptions came on those throws - a top-10 figure. His career average depth of target, per PFF, ranked top-five. The usage aligns with that role. Brazzell lined up in the slot on fewer than 7% of his snaps, the lowest rate in the class. He's a true boundary receiver tasked with winning downfield. From a ZAP Model standpoint, the profile is solid, but not overwhelming. After starting at Tulane, Brazzell transferred to Tennessee in 2024, with his breakout coming in his final season - the first time he cleared a 2.0 adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt mark. That led to a 45.7 Breakout Score, good for the 63rd percentile. We've seen this archetype hit before. Bigger-bodied vertical receivers taken on Day 2 - players like Nico Collins, Tee Higgins, George Pickens, and Christian Watson - show the ceiling that exists here. The issue is that most of those hits had stronger efficiency profiles than Brazzell's 2.09 career yards per route run. Once you get into that range, the comps start to look more like Denzel Mims or Cedric Tillman. Then you factor in the landing spot. Carolina isn't built to support multiple fantasy- relevant wide receivers right now - they ranked 20th in wide receiver PPR production last season. Tetairoa McMillan should command targets, and Jalen Coker looks like a legitimate volume earner as well. This move feels more like a real-life fit than a fantasy-friendly one. Brazzell can help stretch the field and open things up underneath for McMillan and Coker - a role Carolina didn't really have. That could matter for the offense, but it may limit his immediate fantasy ceiling, even if there's maybe some longer-term upside. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 49
players.etr_notes: Tennessee WRs have not translated well, but is a possible vertical threat to get Tet open.
players.elevator_pitch:
And the winner of the "Who Looks the Most Like George Pickens Contest," is... 
There are three receivers in this draft class whose physical dimensions and style of play are like the Cowboys' new arrival in 
2025. Brazzell has the highest score of the three. 
While the comparison may be a superficial layer of information working in his favor, his stint at Tennessee raises doubts. 
Logo scouting is frowned upon, but it also becomes a thing when information meets the desire to simplify. 
82
players.pre_draft_advice:
Brazzell is a first-round fantasy selection in rookie drafts unless an unanticipated event 
changes his value.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Brazzell still has a long way to go before becoming a complete receiver, but he is a long-striding athlete and dangerous vertical threat with surprising savvy for his size. He has the talent to become a team’s No. 2 or No. 3 option.

players.name: Elijah Sarratt
players.lateround_profile: The Ravens took Ja'Kobi Lane on Day 2 and followed it up with Elijah Sarratt in Round 4. That's later than his projected Round 2 to Round 3 capital, so his statistical comps may look a bit more optimistic than they should. There's a real argument that Sarratt is the better prospect, though. After beginning his career at Saint Francis as a zero-star recruit, he finished with a 51.8 Breakout Score - a 71st percentile mark and one of the stronger figures in this class. That's largely due to his consistency. Across his three non-Saint Francis seasons - one at James Madison and two at Indiana - Sarratt posted an adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt between 2.58 and 2.75 each year. He's one of just three receivers in this class with multiple seasons above 2.5 - and the only one to do it three times. The efficiency metrics back it up. Sarratt had a 2.56 career yards per route run, a top- six mark in the class, and generated a first down on 12.5% of his routes, trailing only Makai Lemon. There may be some separation concerns, though - similar to what you could argue with his new teammate, Ja'Kobi Lane. And that's highlighted in a high (95th percentile) rate of seeing contested targets. That likely explains some of the comps, like Michael Thomas and Pat Bryant - bigger receivers who have spent time operating from the slot in the NFL. Thomas obviously became much more than that, but it's fair to wonder if Sarratt settles into a big-slot type of role. Overall, in a class full of middling age-adjusted production profiles, Sarratt's stands out. The issue is the landing spot. Baltimore hasn't posted a positive pass rate over expected in any season since Lamar Jackson became the starter, and the target competition is just strong enough to limit Sarratt's path to volume. Just don't be surprised if Sarratt ends up being the best wide receiver from this 2026 class for the Ravens. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 53
players.etr_notes: 15 TDs for the National Championship winners. Produced at three different levels.
players.elevator_pitch:
Sarratt is a well-built possession receiver who can deliver plus-yardage plays with the help of play-
action, double moves, or constraint plays that fool the tendencies of a coverage thanks to good scouting and coaching. 
Sarratt's style of play has some aspirational comparisons to Cardinals' receiver Michael Wilson. 
Sarratt is a more reliable pass-catcher than Wilson was at this stage of his career, but it was because Wilson's technique 
was still a work in progress. This led to higher highs and lower lows for Wison as he was still in the early stages of 
addressing the issue with a program he created and shared with the media as a senior. 
97
players.pre_draft_advice:
Sarratt may be the safest option by the RSP's scoring criteria among WRs 10-16 on the 
board - and you could probably expand the range to WR23 - but there are several options ranked below Sarratt with higher 
ceilings. 
As you read through the rankings, you'll see who I've noted in this way so you can make an educated decision whether to 
roll with the safest picks or take a slightly larger gamble on a player with a potential WR1 profile.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Sarratt will be saddled with the “possession receiver” label, but he is an underrated athlete and plays with the urgency and toughness to own the catch point — which translates to any level of football. He has the tools to develop into an NFL starter, either on the outside or as a “big slot.”

players.name: Nick Singleton
players.lateround_profile: The Senior Bowl is invaluable for evaluating prospects up close. Unfortunately, injuries are part of the deal. That's what happened to Penn State's Nick Singleton, who broke his foot during practice in January. It may have contributed to his slide to Pick 165, where Tennessee scooped him up. According to the ZAP Model, that could be a value. Among Combine-invited running backs, Singleton ranks sixth in best-season prorated reception share, fifth in adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt, and sixth in adjusted total yards per team play - out of 20 players. That's even more impressive given that he shared a backfield with Kaytron Allen for all four seasons at Penn State. Singleton was clearly the better pass-catcher, highlighted by a 41-catch season in 2024 compared to Allen's 18. There are some concerns outside the model. Per PFF, Singleton forced missed tackles at a below-average rate, and despite strong athletic traits, his rate of runs gaining 10 or more yards was below average among Combine invitees. The tools are there - the consistency wasn't always. Still, this is a rare profile for a Day 3 pick. In ZAP Model history, just 13 running backs drafted after Pick 100 have entered the league with a prorated reception share above 12%, a best-season total yards per team play above 1.6, and an adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt above 1.0. Singleton cleared all three with room to spare. Of those 13 backs, 9 exceeded their draft-capital-based expectation, and on average, the group outperformed expectation by 3.5 PPR points per game. When Day 3 running backs hit, it's often because they land in ambiguous backfields. You can see that reflected in seasonal average draft position - when a team's top running back isn't a high-investment player, it signals opportunity. That's exactly the situation in Tennessee. Tyjae Spears and Tony Pollard are both set to hit free agency next year, and there's no clear long-term answer in place. There may not even be a short-term one in place. Singleton is absolutely worth a look in rookie drafts. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 89
players.etr_notes: Pollard is a cut candidate, making this raw talent intriguing.
players.elevator_pitch:
Singleton burst on the college football scene a few years ago as one of the top recruited backs in the 
country. By year's end, Singleton and the less-heralded Quinshon Judkins were the marquee freshman performers. 
My former podcast co-host, Felix Sharpe, asked me to watch the two later in the year. I thought Judkins was a more 
powerful runner with twitchier movement. Still, I liked Singleton's game and believed he deserved his early acclaim. 
Fast-forward to the present and Singleton played in a committee, his coach got fired, and he was paired with a quarterback 
who didn't perform to his physical potential. This may contribute to production declines and lead draft analysts to be down 
on Singleton's draft stock. 
Singleton isn't flash, but he's a smart, patient and scheme versatile runner. His footwork is well connected with what his 
eyes see and his brain processes. 
Singleton has strong acceleration and good speed. He'll beat defensive backs - including some cornerbacks - into the 
secondary and hold them off to flip the field, if not breakaway for a long score. 
He's a well-built runner who can pull through reaches from all three levels of defenders. When he encounters defenders in 
his path that he can't avoid, he accelerates into the collision with good pad level. 
Whatever the angle of the collision, Singleton can bounce off the hits of linebackers. He forces opponents to gang tackle 
him. 
Singleton shines as an outlet receiver as well as a first read on screens and RPOs. There are lapses with clap attack when 
reaching wide for a throw as well as focus drops, but these are first-world problems for otherwise high-volume receivers. 
Singleton's blocking is competent and there's room to clean up details that will make him reliable for the NFL. 
Overall, Singleton has a versatile game. He's a candidate to build on his college career and become a capable NFL starter. 
Vision: Singleton is patient with gap blocking, reads the leverage of defenders in the box, and finds the open hole-this 
includes cutbacks. When running a gap play to the perimeter, Singleton gives a brief press inside to set up the outside 
crease. 
Singleton is patient with zone blocking, reading the leverage of defenders on the play side and finding a cutback to set up 
with a press of a block that gets within 1-2 steps of the blocker and defender. He sets up double teams on the backside of 
his approach with a deep press, demonstrating an understanding of the play design. 
There are off tackle plays where the backside crease is coming open where he can continue to press outside 1-2 steps 
longer. 
Singleton identifies penetration as he approaches the exchange, and he avoids some of the contact so he can continue 
forward. He also identifies linebacker run blitzes between the tackles pre-snap. 
80
players.pre_draft_advice:
Before we see a landing spot in the NFL, consider Singleton a second- or third-round pick 
in rookie formats. Any value change will depend on his NFL Draft stock. He's probably higher on the RSP's board than those 
enamored with the likes of Mike Washington, Jr. and Le'Veon Moss.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Singleton can be a frustrating watch because of his inconsistent run discipline and vision, but he offers big-play potential with an exceptional blend of size and speed. From day one, his pass-catching and kick-return skills will expand the ways in which an NFL team can utilize his talent.

players.name: Ted Hurst
players.lateround_profile: Ted Hurst gained a lot of steam during the pre-draft process, rising from a projected 180th overall pick in mid-January to a top-80 selection by April. He ultimately went 84th overall - a big win for a small-school prospect. Analytically, Hurst is tough to pin down. His Breakout Score sits around 42, which is basically average, but he's a younger prospect (still under 22) with good length. He was also used aggressively downfield in college. His 28.6% career deep target rate ranks top- 10 in this class, and more than 21% of his receptions came on deep throws - fourth-highest among Combine invites. That usage drove a top-10 average depth of target. That all sounds fine, but we should keep in mind that it came against weaker competition. His best season in adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt was just 2.08. Among 26 Day 2 wide receivers in the ZAP Model database with a mark below 2.2, only 5 have produced a best season above 10 PPR points per game within their first three years. It's not an awful hit rate when you consider context, but it's not encouraging either. Layer in the deep-ball profile, and the risk increases. Since 2020, 22 non-first-round wide receivers have entered the league with at least 20% of their receptions coming on deep targets and a deep target rate above 25%. Only one - Nico Collins - has produced a season above 12 PPR points per game within his first three years. At least Collins shows up as a comp! In a class without many obvious targets, Hurst offers some intrigue. Tampa Bay wouldn't be the most ideal landing spot for fantasy, but there's a path. Mike Evans, their primary perimeter downfield threat, is gone. Chris Godwin could be out next year given his contract. Jalen McMillan has flashed, and Tez Johnson has shown something as well, but Hurst's range of outcomes includes being the best of that trio. There's at least a scenario - and it's not the most likely one - where Hurst is earning targets behind only Emeka Egbuka for the Buccaneers by 2027. Just don't draft him as if that outcome is guaranteed. And certainly don't draft him with the expectation that he'll have a monster 2026 campaign. You're getting him for 2027 and beyond. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 50
players.etr_notes: Fun small-school option.
players.elevator_pitch:
Hurst's development could go in two distinct directions. If he emphasizes precision, refines his routes, 
and adds a little muscle to his frame, Hurst could become an aspiring A.J. Green - a big-play primary receiver who can 
defeat press-man coverage and run the full route tree. 
If Hurst bulks up further and his route tree is limited, he could become a big-play possession option like Tee Higgins who 
isn't asked to run as full of a route tree and wins with his size at the boundary. 
151
players.pre_draft_advice:
Hurst is worth considering as a mid-to-late pick in rookie drafts. The NFL will draft him 
and likely before Round Five. Consider him a patience play who might deliver an early taste of what he could eventually 
become at various points during his rookie year.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Hurst faces a steep learning curve versus NFL press and coverage traffic, but his physical traits and ball-tracking skills — especially as a downfield target — are exciting. He is a developmental X/Z receiver with down-the-road starting upside.

players.name: Max Klare
players.lateround_profile: Despite being considered the TE3 on plenty of big boards entering the draft, Max Klare actually wound up as the TE5, going 61st overall to the Rams, who drafted a fun prospect in Terrance Ferguson (46th overall) just last year. Klare, though, is a fantasy football manager's tight end. That is to say: he should be able to handle a lot of receiving volume - if it comes his way. Klare leads this year's tight end class in Breakout Score, thanks to some big production at Purdue (specifically a 51-catch, 685-yard, 4-touchdown stat line as a redshirt sophomore) before tailing off a bit in his lone year at Ohio State. That's understandable when you're playing alongside Jeremiah Smith, Carnell Tate, and Will Kacmarek (a fellow tight end who was drafted 87th overall this year). So, he carved out great market shares in an easier situation and remained relevant with competition upgrades at Ohio State (he was third on the team in catches behind Smith and Tate this past season). That's good stuff. Playing alongside target-earners like that will also prove to be good practice, as Klare is joining a team with Puka Nacua, Davante Adams, and a crowded tight end room (for now). Klare has some versatility. He was also used out wide fairly often (13.4% wide rate, per PFF, in 2025 - highest among tight ends in the class). While he's unlikely to be the highest-ceiling option we've seen in recent years (especially within this offense, where elite target shares are spoken for already), he should be able to answer the call when the ball is thrown his way. Also, Colby Parkinson and Davis Allen are free agents after this year, so wheels should be up for Klare soon enough, even if he shares the field with Ferguson a lot in the future. Remember, the Rams shifted quite a bit to heavier tight end sets in 2025. Klare almost drew a Mark Andrews comp - just for a little more context. players.elevator_pitch:
A converted quarterback, there's a lack of muscle memory with areas of Klare's new position that should improve 
with experience, but it could limit his playing time early in his career. There are also questions about his size: Will he add muscle and 
will it make him more explosive or slow him down? 
Klare makes starter-worthy plays as a pass receiver, but he has lapses that could be due to lack of experience at the position. This 
could be a muscle memory problem that improves with practice reps and time. 
58
players.pre_draft_advice:
Klare has some boom-bust baked into his game because of his inconsistencies with his hands, 
limited size as a blocker, and how team fit could impact his opportunities. Is he his new team's future TE1 or is he a talented TE2 
with limited volume in an offense that doesn't prioritize him in the receiving game? 
60
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Klare has a low ceiling as a blocker and needs to continue adding refinement as a route runner, but his combination of athleticism, toughness and ball skills gives him a modern-day skill set. He projects as an F tight end, similar in ways to Dalton Kincaid.

players.name: Eli Raridon
players.lateround_profile: Eli Raridon was the ninth tight end drafted within the first 95 picks of the 2026 NFL Draft, which really put things into context. With 27 tight ends invited to the NFL Combine and most of them grading out well athletically, some of them were going to be drafted earlier than expected, and Raridon was another such case, vaulting up from a fifth-round projected pick in mid-March to being a third-round pick on draft day. Raridon played four seasons at Notre Dame, yet despite his size, he scored just 3 times on 48 catches over 40 games, and there are some blocking concerns. That didn't seem to affect his draft stock too much, though. The Patriots seem good with the upside. Raridon is 70th-percentile or better in max yards per team attempt and Breakout Score among the tight end database, and there's still a bit of that big-bodied, basketball- background archetype in Raridon's profile that we've seen lead to some really nice fantasy football production in the past. We shouldn't ignore him (the NFL clearly didn't), and there's a lot of room for him in New England's tight end room. Hunter Henry is 32 years old and has just one more year on his contract. Julian Hill was an undrafted free agent from the 2023 class. Other than that, Raridon should get chances. He's a good athlete (110 height-adjusted Speed Score), he's big, and he is going to be playing alongside an MVP-caliber quarterback in Drake Maye. It's a promising landing spot for a prospect with fantasy football potential in a class with some questionable team fits and some reaches on primary blocking tight ends. LLaattee--RRoouunndd FFaannttaassyy FFoooottbbaallll:: 22002266 PPrroossppeecctt GGuuiiddee JJuummpp TToo:: ZZAAPP MMooddeell || YYeeaarr 22 MMooddeell 111111
players.elevator_pitch:
Raridon is what NFL scouts call a "projection pick." NFL organizations often elevate a player's grade ahead of his 
portfolio of film, because they like his athletic ability and his potential for additional physical development. Projection picks can work 
out, but sometimes the teams underestimate which skills are most difficult to develop. 
Raridon has the height of an every-down tight end and could have the frame to add the 15-20 pounds that he'll need to handle that 
role. He's fast enough to stretch a seam and beat corners outside due to his height and length advantage. 
90
players.pre_draft_advice:
Raridon may get that Notre Dame bump with his draft capital. If so, he could earn early 
opportunities based on that draft capital that aren't commensurate with his current skill set. 
It means you might be overpaying for Raridon in dynasty formats. That said, if you see Raridon in the TE10-TE15 range in this class, 
there's not a huge difference in grade among them. 
Monitor Raridon from afar. He might become a starter at some point, but not one you'll have difficulty acquiring.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Raridon is still figuring things out, but he gets to top gear quickly and has promising ball skills and blocking chops. His college tape shows the building blocks of an intriguing pro-level tight end, although the medicals will influence his draft projection.

players.name: Emmett Johnson
players.lateround_profile: Emmett Johnson is one of my favorite prospects in this class, but he didn't find the cleanest landing spot with the Chiefs. The long-term offense is appealing, sure, but Kenneth Walker's arrival this offseason creates an immediate obstacle. The good news is that they're very different backs. Walker wins with speed and explosive runs, while Johnson profiles more as a grinder - even at his size - with a strong receiving skill set. From a production standpoint, Johnson checks just about every box in the ZAP Model. His best-season prorated reception share ranks in the 94th percentile, his top adjusted yards per team play sits in the 95th, and his adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt lands in the 89th percentile. It's no surprise he earned a low-risk designation. It's not all butterflies and rainbows, though. Among Combine-invited running backs, Johnson ranked third-worst in career yards after contact per attempt, per PFF. There's some context worth noting. According to Rich Hribar of Sharp Football Analysis, Johnson was hit at or behind the line on just 33.9% of his runs - the lowest rate in the class. And when he was hit there, he still gained yardage on a class-high 75.3% of those attempts. That points to some efficiency that traditional metrics may miss. Johnson's also unlikely to be a big-play threat. His career explosive run rate sits in the 19th percentile, and his 4.56 40-yard dash at 202 pounds resulted in a below-average Speed Score. But that doesn't automatically disqualify him. Since 2011, 10 running backs under 210 pounds have posted a season above 12 PPR points per game after being drafted outside Round 2. Only four of them had a Speed Score above 94. In other words, elite straight-line speed hasn't been a requirement for this archetype. We've seen receiving-driven backs with modest athletic profiles hit - Kyren Williams, Bucky Irving, Aaron Jones. We've also seen plenty miss. The range of outcomes is wide. Johnson is an intriguing dart throw in rookie drafts. The path to immediate production isn't clear, but there's not much in his way behind Walker on the Chiefs depth chart. And Walker hasn't exactly stayed healthy throughout his time in the NFL. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 88
players.etr_notes: Could wind up being a Charbonnet-lite for Walker if he proves himself.
players.elevator_pitch:
Emmett Johnson's pre-draft trajectory reminds me of Harold Fannin's. They gained acclaim during the 
season based on their on-field performances. 
There was legitimate excitement to see them perform at the NFL Combine. When they didn't perform to expectations of the 
public, the excitement plummeted to the point that you could hear an echo out in the world if you broached their names. 
Last year, I detailed how Fannin's "bad NFL Combine" was an overstated and overrated analysis point. Fannin had a strong 
rookie year and could be taking over a lead role in the Browns' passing game. 
The gist of the analysis was about interpreting the tests, knowing how much weight to give them compared to other sources 
of information, and who else compared stylistically to Fannin in the NFL with similar production. 
Johnson is this year's candidate for "The NFL Combine Threw a Red Herring into the Collective's Draft Analysis." 
Let's begin with Johnson's 4.56-second, 40-Yard Dash. It's the same time as Aaron Jones and they had similar dimensions 
at that point in their careers. The number is fast enough. 
So is the 4.29-second, 20 Yard-Shuttle. A 4.30-second mark is the RSP's minimum standard guideline for the Starter Tier. 
Both marks are in the Starter Tier, for whatever that's worth when considering that the timing technology is either second- 
or third-tier technology with a high variation that makes it much less reliable than they want you to believe. 
These were underwhelming marks for Johnson because the media and draft culture have been brainwashed to overvalue 
Relative Athletic Score percentiles. RAS is an entertaining and informative resource. 
There's value to knowing the percentile that an NFL Combine participant's performance is relative to his peers. It 
unintentionally creates noise for the signal. 
That signal is knowing what the minimum expected times should be for each position based on physical dimensions, style of 
movement, and expected role (elite starter, starter, rotational contributor, situational contributor, reserve, and free agent. 
These factors provide valuable context well beyond RAS percentiles. When Johnson ran a 7.32-second, Three Cone Drill, the 
RAS Percentile Apostles of the Pseudo-Science Church of Oblong Money Ball that Bounces Too Funny for Predictability in 
Everything Baseball Does immediately cited the 32nd percentile like they were saying, "A-men," from the pews of Lucas Oil 
Stadium. 
Let's dig deeper than the good book, shall we? 
A Three-Cone Drill time commensurate with a starting RB in the RSP is seven seconds-flat or less. Here are backs who 
contributed at a high level with no better than Reserve Tier Three-Cone marks of 7.20 seconds. 
Kenneth Gainwell - 7.26 seconds 
• 
• Dalvin Cook - 7.27 seconds 
• 
• 
Samaje Perine - 7.25 seconds 
Jamaal Williams - 7.25 seconds
