players.pre_draft_advice:
Johnson might be available in the third or fourth rounds of rookie drafts ahead of the NFL 
Draft - maybe even the fifth round if the Apostles of the Oblong Money Ball poisoned the well. After the NFL Draft, there 
might be a rebound in his ADP that goes as high as the second round. 
The likely range is rounds 2-4, depending on the size of your rosters and round allotments. If I had to narrow it further, late 
second to mid-third.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Johnson is quick and balanced between the tackles to stack cuts, and his pass-catching savvy will be an asset at the next level, ideally for a zone scheme. He is an intriguing change-of-pace back who will bring versatility to an NFL backfield.

players.name: Ja'Kobi Lane
players.lateround_profile: Ja'Kobi Lane is one of just five early-declare wide receivers in this year's class, spending all three collegiate seasons at USC. After catching just seven passes in six games as a freshman, he jumped to 43 and then 49 receptions over the next two years. The production profile, though, is underwhelming. His 2024 adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt was just 1.14, rising to 2.35 as a junior. Even that peak ranked outside the top 20 in the class. The result is a 35.9 Breakout Score - a below-average mark in the ZAP Model. To be fair, Lane shared the field with likely first-rounder Makai Lemon, so Teammate Score gives him a modest boost. But even with that context, the résumé looks pretty ordinary. His career yards per route run, per PFF, ranks 26th in the class. He was fine at generating first downs, but nothing here signals a difference-maker. While he has length, Lane's frame is leaner than you'd expect for a true contested-catch specialist - and that was a big part of his profile. Among this year's Combine invitees, he ranked first with a near 33% contested target rate. With top-100 draft capital, he's just the fourth wide receiver since 2020 to enter the league with a contested target rate above 30%. The other three are Josh Palmer, Nico Collins, and Alec Pierce - not bad company. But each of those players had stronger Breakout Scores and more size to support that play style. Lane's contested usage seems to stem more from an inability to separate. In Baltimore, Lane is likely buried early. Zay Flowers and Rashod Bateman are ahead of him, with Bateman under contract through 2027 and Flowers potentially there long-term as well. The Ravens also drafted Elijah Sarratt, who may just be the better player. And even with a new coordinator, this is still an offense that hasn't posted a positive pass rate over expected since Lamar Jackson became the starter in 2019. Volume is unlikely to be a strength. There are a couple of things to like - mainly his age and early declare status - but this isn't a profile that demands attention in rookie drafts. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 44
players.etr_notes: Broke out within three years despite great competition.
players.elevator_pitch:
Lane can become primary receiver in the NFL - possibly a star receiver. Details of his game grew as 
the season progressed, including his releases from the line of scrimmage, savvy methods for setting up breaks, and flatter 
speed turns. 
Lane isn't far from developing into an NFL starter. At the same time, there are details lacking with his game that could hold 
him back depending on his landing spot. 
137
players.pre_draft_advice:
Lane is a medium-floor, medium-ceiling option because there's consistently a role for tall-
rangy ball-getters in offenses who can win at the boundary or in a window of zone coverage, but his man-to-man game may 
never emerge to a level where he becomes a clear No. 2 option in the team's pecking order. 
Lane will likely be a top 12 pick at his position in many rookie drafts. I'm willing to stretch my stance and consider him 
among the top 15 pass-catchers, but I'd be more comfortable taking him among the top 24. Draft capital and landing spot 
could persuade me to value him higher than the top 15.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Lane is a light, linear athlete with an outstanding catch radius, but play strength and separation concerns must be addressed for him to find steady success. Not having a special teams role won’t help his chances as NFL teams stack their receiver depth charts.

players.name: Mike Washington
players.lateround_profile: Mike Washington was the biggest faller at running back in this class. According to NFL Mock Draft Database, he was expected to go around Pick 63. Instead, he landed in Las Vegas at 122. Using Chase Stuart's draft value chart, that drop equates to a 4.2-point gain for the Raiders. Since 2016, the only players with a similar gap between projected and actual capital are Kenneth Dixon, Dalvin Cook, Paul Perkins, Derrius Guice, and Damien Martinez. The fall isn't all that surprising. Washington is a freak athlete - at 223 pounds, he ran a 4.33 40-yard dash, good for the second-best Speed Score in ZAP Model history. But the production profile is far more ordinary. Washington played five seasons across three programs and never exceeded a 41% rush attempt share. And despite his size, he ranked bottom-five in this class in missed tackles forced per attempt, per PFF. His rate of runs gaining 15 or more yards was also below the class average, even with that elite speed. In other words, the production doesn't really match the measurables. There's also the situation. Washington lands behind Ashton Jeanty, one of the top running back prospects of the last decade. Realistically, that caps him as a handcuff. And there's some value in that role. If Jeanty were to go down, Washington is capable enough to carry the load. It's just easier to buy into a player like this when the profile is stronger. With Washington, it's more of a "fine, I guess" than anything else. If this wasn't within such a mediocre draft class, we'd probably want to be even less bullish. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 87
players.etr_notes: Fantastic size/speed prospect, but clearly blocked by Jeanty.
players.elevator_pitch:
There are two poles of thought I've seen pre-draft about Washington. On one end, Washington is an 
emerging player who has improved his game at every stop. On the other, Washington has never been attention-worthy until 
this year and that attention as a fifth year running back is overblown. 
There are underlying truths with both cases. Washington is a well-built athlete with resources he can draw upon as a 
running back. He has elite speed when he earns a runway to build it and his acceleration is good enough to make it happen. 
When it doesn't, Washington's burst will still get him chain-moving gains between the tackles and on the perimeter. 
Washington has elite power. He'll drag and push defenders downhill, he bounces off contact, and he breaks tackles - orhe's 
doing some combination of all three multiple times during a run. 
Washington's agility is also in the RSP's Starter Tier. He's not as efficient with his transitions downhill as the defined 
standard, but there are enough plays where Washington meets or exceeds the starter guidelines for the starter tier that are 
most meaningful. This includes his footwork combinations, lateral cuts, getting his feet and knees over shots and efficient 
upper body movement. 
If I wasn't going to veer slightly from the guidelines, which I explain as I did above when I do, Washington's Depth of Talent 
Score would be 79. He'd fall to the high-end cusp of the next tier, and he'd be ranked RB19 instead of RB14. It's not a huge 
difference - both scores project that Washington can contribute early as a situational role player, and within a short span of 
time earn a committee role. From there, he'll need to address some of his biggest flaws to become a worthwhile starter. 
Washington's catching and blocking are good enough for situational appearances and with some improvements that we 
often see from young NFL prospects, he could become a reliable asset in all phases of pass protection and receiving from 
the backfield. 
From this perspective, there's little not to like. Still, one can make the argument that "little bit" carries a lot more weight 
than the optimists realize. 
Washington's ball security is on the low-end of the Committee Tier, bordering on the Reserve Tier. When examining the 
RSP's tracking of ball security among prospects over the past decade, only 10 percent of the prospects with a fumble rate of 
1 per 61 attempts or worse earned fantasy production on par with an RB2-RB5. 
Only 3 percent of the prospects with this low fumble rate earned RB1 value during a season. In contrast, 81 percent of the 
prospects from the RSP's 10-year sample at a ball security rate of at 1 fumble per 121 attempts or higher. 
I'm not telling you this is a direct or strong correlation. There are possible other factors that could be driving or having 
heavier influences on these outcomes. I will be looking further into this sometime in the future. 
Still, we know that ball security woes can lead to prospects losing roles, and ultimately jobs. Washington's ball security rate 
is 1 fumble per 51 touches. There were no mitigating factors. Washington has had sub-par ball security every year of his 
career. 
This is a problem on the field and holding back the rest of his game in this evaluation. We've seen running backs improve 
their ball security in the NFL and deliver production value. 
111
players.pre_draft_advice:
Washington will be valued as one of the top 3-5 backs in rookie drafts. A better 
investment is a top receiver and take a less expensive shot on a runner with a cleaner game and starter upside later. Unless 
Washington falls to the NFL's fifth or sixth round, you'll be overpaying for his risk/reward volatility.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Washington is a good-sized athlete who gets through the crease and can be a punisher behind his pads, although he will need to improve his third-down skills to increase his NFL value.

players.name: Kaytron Allen
players.lateround_profile: When you first look at Kaytron Allen's production profile, the lack of receiving output stands out immediately. His best-season prorated reception share was just 8.6%, sixth-worst in the class, and his adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt was 0.50 - also sixth-worst. Allen spent all four seasons at Penn State alongside Nick Singleton, who was drafted just before him. Singleton handled more of the passing-game work, which naturally suppresses Allen's receiving résumé in the model. Stylistically, Allen was the thunder to Singleton's lightning - a downhill, physical runner who forced missed tackles at a slightly higher rate. That said, his tackle-breaking and yards-after-contact numbers were more average than standout relative to this class. When you combine that type of rushing profile with limited receiving involvement, it can be concerning. Historically, it often is. The ZAP Model database includes more than 170 running backs who entered the league with a best-season prorated reception share below 10% and a peak adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt under 0.60 - Allen fits both thresholds. Of that group, just seven produced a season of at least 14 PPR points per game within their first three years: Alfred Morris, Andre Ellington, CJ Anderson, Carlos Hyde, Jordan Howard, Chris Carson, and Isiah Pacheco. This is where Allen's overall production matters. He was highly productive on the ground - he's Penn State's all-time leading rusher - and posted a 1.96 adjusted total yards per team play mark. Among those seven hits, only Jordan Howard and Carlos Hyde reached that level. Add in the fact that Allen shared a backfield with legitimate competition throughout his career, and the profile looks a bit better than the receiving numbers alone suggest. The landing spot in Washington helps, too. There's no entrenched backfield leader, and we know ambiguous situations can create early opportunity for Day 3 picks. Allen is the type of player who could see a short-term spike in dynasty value. If that happens, he may be more of a sell candidate than a long-term hold after Year 1, given the lack of receiving upside - similar to what we've seen with players like Dameon Pierce or even Jordan Howard. But you can still take him in rookie drafts and hope his early-down work wins out in 2026. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 93
players.etr_notes: Not a supreme physical specimen, but does the things coaches like that can get him on the field.
players.elevator_pitch:
Allen should be a stalwart RB3/RB4 on several rosters for as long as he stays healthy and maintains 
his athletic prowess. He could have some years as a committee back or even a low-end starter for teams in a desperate 
situation because he's a powerful runner with good decision-making, agility, and reliable ball security. 
Allen is physical. He breaks tackles, bounces off hits, and pushes piles. His pad level can be late at the point of attack, and 
it costs him yardage he'd otherwise earn. 
Whether it's gap or zone, Allen earns what's blocked for him and finds bounce lanes and cutbacks when defenders fill the 
gaps. Allen can overreact with his bounces in short yardage when a more incremental move to the next available gap is 
wise. Otherwise, he's a smart runner who rarely places his offense in a bad situation with an unforced error. 
Allen has mobile hips to make 90-degree changes of direction efficiently. He adjusts his stride and pace to set up blockers 
and pursuit. When in the open field, Allen can deliver combinations of moves to set up unblocked defenders. If there's a 
move, Allen has it and he delivers it with competence and occasional dynamism. 
Speed and acceleration are the weaknesses of Allen's ball carrying. They are good enough for him to make an active roster 
in the NFL and even contribute as an RB2 - at least on paper. 
The current reality of the NFL is that there's a glut of RB talent. Most backs on depth charts in the league have starter-level 
acceleration and speed - not what most NFL Combine followers think starter-level explosion is, but the RSP's minimum 
standards for a starter and a committee-level contributor. 
Allen's speed and acceleration are closer to the minimum for a contributor, and not a starter. Most reserves are closer to the 
starter tier. 
Think Trey Sermon, who has a lot of starter-level skills, but his lack of explosiveness hindered his play and his opportunities 
in the NFL. Sermon's top speed in 2020 at Ohio State was 21.7 mph. This should be closer to his average and not his 
outlier. His top five plays were between 20.2 mph and 21.7 mph. 
Kaytron Allen's top speed was 20.6 mph and low range of his top five plays was 19.9 mph. Based on what I've seen on film 
Allen isn't fast enough to become a starter in most systems. 
The rest of his game is good enough to make a roster, but he may fall out of favor at the end of a season once coaches 
trust the younger player with more explosion in his athletic profile. Then, Allen will be on the next town as the RB3/RB4 
insurance policy who can handle volume, hold onto the ball, and execute the scheme, but isn't fast enough for their long-
term needs. 
Vision: Allen reads leverage well and presses creases within a step of his blockers to access cutback lanes between the 
tackles. 
Allen finds bounces after attacking interior rushing lanes that get clogged. He doesn't arbitrarily bounce to the farthest lane, 
but he rides the wave to the nearest outside lane and works his way there incrementally. There are some short yardage 
120
players.pre_draft_advice:
Allen is a late-round selection in most leagues in case Allen plays faster than he did at 
Penn State. He's a good all-around back, but doesn't project to become a good fantasy contributor.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Allen gave his draft profile a boost at the Senior Bowl, and he shows the quickness and ball skills that will stand out during training camp.

players.name: Carson Beck
players.etr_notes: Should get some starts as a rookie, but seems unlikely to be a long-term option.
players.elevator_pitch:
The NFL will covet Beck more than the initial press he earned in 2025 after transferring from Georgia to Miami. A top 
prospect heading into 2024, Beck had what the media has characterized as an up-and-down career at Georgia. 
Despite a respectable overall statistical profile in 2024, Beck didn't follow up an even stronger 2023 campaign with similar highs in 
completion percentage and yardage. Beck also doubled his interception count from 6 to 12. 
Beck ended his career at Georgia after tearing a ligament in his throwing elbow before the college playoffs. Beck rebounded at Miami and 
either came close to matching or exceeding most box score figures media values and led the Hurricanes to the college football championship 
game. 
Still, the narrative makers cite that Beck was a fifth-year passer, and his strong statistical performance last year was a baseline requirement 
instead of an impressive layer to his evaluation as a pro prospect. The NFL will value these bullet points, especially team executives who 
subsist on them, especially when they're infused with risk management data points that will cover their asses - I mean assets. 
Prototypical physical dimensions 
Top college programs that are playing winning football 
Arm strength 
• 
• 
• 
• Good box score statistics 
• Good NFL Combine and Pro Day performances with scripted football ballet vs air 
• 
Paid for interview training that comes with the correction dog collar and remote 
• 
Reasonable track record of health 
The more of these data points a GM, VP, and President can check off, the less risk they feel about taking the player in the earliest rounds of 
the NFL Draft. If things go wrong with the player's development, they can at least point to the list of things about the player that were low 
risk. They can then point the finger elsewhere. 
Beck's career checks most of these boxes on the list. There's still a pervasive belief that because you can't teach size, athletic ability, and 
arm talent at the levels they covet them, so you draft these Robo QBs. Then, you can teach them how to play the quarterback position at 
an advanced level. 
Except there's a flaw to this mindset. There's little to no teaching. Talk of coaches developing players is mostly lip service. 
The same scene plays out at most NFL organizations every year. A franchise considers drafting an early-round rookie quarterback: Owners 
and executives ask coaches if they can develop the quarterback. The coaches reflexively say, "yes," because they know the question is the 
executives' coded way of saying, "We want to draft him and you're going to be the scapegoat if the player doesn't develop." 
The coach's affirmative response of "Yes," the coach's version of only showing the tip of the iceberg. If you dive below the water's surface, 
you'd see a longer response vocalized in the coach's head. 
"Yes, I want to keep my job right now so I'm going to agree despite knowing we won't have the time to do much with him. I'm just saying 
yes to end this painful meeting. 
I've known all along that I'm your scapegoat for just about everything that doesn't work on the field. I'll likely blame the player when he 
can't figure out the game at this level. 
Not that it usually works, but I've seen that it can buy me a year while I make plans for my golden parachute elsewhere. I can live with 
your entrepreneurial-nepotistic-corporate cocktail of b.s. - like coming down to the sideline in the second half of games as if your presence 
is going to suddenly click all 316 team processes into place. Just please, stop sending me plays to run on cocktail napkins at halftime like 
you're a hybrid of Ceasar and Madden." 
Odds are likely this is the environment Beck will be headed to if he's a first-round selection. Whether Beck earns a first-round pick depends 
on how much franchises are being real with themselves, or they're leaning too hard the historical economics of position scarcity instead of 
working harder on figuring out what makes a good QB and what they can do to be a part of that process. 
As with most of these prospects, Beck needs time to refine his game. He has enough skills for decision-makers with personnel convince 
themselves he's better than he is right now. 
Beck plays played in system with pro concepts and he's well-versed in the drop-back and play-action game. Whether you want to use Beck 
under center, shotgun, or Pistol, he has done it and shown competent work with all the bells and whistles that come with misdirection out of 
these alignments. 
79
players.pre_draft_advice:
Beck's fantasy value could increase if the NFL gives him first-round draft capital. Based on what we've 
seen play out with quarterbacks for decades, it's better to target Beck in a few years via trade or as a free agent than spend capital on him. 
Target receivers.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Beck is a cerebral pocket passer who can lead drives when the offense stays in rhythm, but he isn’t a proven second-reaction quarterback when things don’t go according to plan. He projects as an NFL backup and potential spot starter.

players.name: Malachi Fields
players.lateround_profile: Malachi Fields' production profile is lacking. He began his collegiate career at Virginia in 2021, catching just 11 passes as a true freshman before a foot injury limited him to one game as a sophomore. His junior season showed some promise - 58 receptions for 811 yards and 5 touchdowns - resulting in a 2.24 adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt mark. That ended up being his best year. Fields played two more seasons - one at Virginia and one at Notre Dame - but neither produced a stronger campaign. Now, a little behind-the-scenes note: in the pre-draft version of the guide, Fields had the wrong birthday in the ZAP Model, which lowered his Breakout Score by about 10 points. So, yes, he looks better now than he did then. He still doesn't look great. Fields' Breakout Score comes in at a very average 43.1, and the ZAP Model still tags him as a high-risk prospect. The advanced metrics back that up. His career yards per route run sits at 1.94 - a major concern. Since 2020, 18 Day 2 wide receivers have entered the league with a career mark below 2.0. None of them have produced more than 12.5 PPR points per game in any of their first three seasons. There are some positives. Fields has prototypical size, and there's opportunity in the Giants offense, where Malik Nabers is the only long-term fixture. If you really want to stretch for an outcome, you can squint and see something like Michael Pittman, one of his top-three comps. But that's closer to a ceiling than an expectation. Fields is a risky bet. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 48
players.etr_notes: Power forward at WR, impressed at the Senior Bowl.
players.elevator_pitch:
Relative Athletic Scores (RAS) are a fun and informative layer of information because we can see how 
players compare to each other athletically over a history timeline. It's a great conversation starter and an entertaining 
reference point. 
This is catnip for beat reporters and feature writers. Give them information and let them weave them into something 
magical. 
Here's the thing about RAS. They are based on the NFL Combine. 
59
players.pre_draft_advice: Fields is a top-10 rookie pick in most formats.

players.name: Skyler Bell
players.lateround_profile: Skyler Bell played just one game as a freshman at Wisconsin, but broke out in Year 2 with 30 receptions for 444 yards and 5 touchdowns. After a dip as a junior, he transferred to UConn. The production improved, as expected. Over his two seasons with the Huskies, Bell posted prorated receiving yards per team pass attempt marks of 2.17 and 2.82. The issue is that UConn's strength of schedule dragged those numbers down after adjustment, and because his peak came later in his career, his Breakout Score sits at a mediocre 22.2. Bell is slightly undersized, but he showed some flexibility by playing both inside and outside. Interestingly, his perimeter yards per route run ended up a bit stronger than his slot mark. His 2025 season is doing a lot of the work in his profile. That 2.82 receiving yards per team pass attempt ranked top-10 in the class, and his 3.13 yards per route run was a top- five single-season mark among Combine invites. There's also some added versatility. As a redshirt freshman at Wisconsin, Bell logged 13 carries for 160 yards. That role didn't carry over to UConn, but it points to his athletic profile - something that also shows up in a fringe top-10 yards-after-the-catch mark. That said, he wasn't especially strong at forcing missed tackles, per PFF. Can he carve out a role in Buffalo? Entering the draft, the Bills' top wide receivers were DJ Moore, Josh Palmer, and Khalil Shakir, with Shakir operating in the slot. Bell's ability to line up everywhere gives him a path to back up multiple roles, which likely factored into the selection. There's something to work with here. Analytically, Bell looks a bit like Ricky Pearsall - an older prospect (he's nearly 24) who offers some versatility beyond traditional receiver usage. And in this class, attaching a late rookie pick to an elite quarterback situation isn't bad process. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 55
players.etr_notes: Elite agility testing at his pro day.
players.elevator_pitch:
The prospect who is the exception to the rule is a dangerous game in the world of prospect evaluation. One 
of the common "exceptions to the rule" invoked with wide receiver prospects is the productive college player who exhibits 
lackluster technique as a pass-catcher. 
Good scouting involves projection. Determining when one is going too far with the projection or not far enough is the 
challenge. Skyler Bell is an excellent example of this challenge in 2026. 
70
players.pre_draft_advice:
If you want low-risk selections, Bell's clap-attacking rules him out. If you agree with my 
logic surrounding his likelihood to become an exception to the rule, Bell has a WR2 floor and a WR1 ceiling that makes him 
a first-round rookie pick.

players.name: Kaelon Black
players.lateround_profile: For the first time in Late-Round Prospect Guide history, a profile page is being added post-draft. What a time to be alive. It's not common for non-Combine invites to get drafted, let alone inside the top 100. But that's what happened with Indiana running back Kaelon Black. San Francisco did the most Kyle Shanahan thing imaginable and took him with the 90th overall pick. Keep in mind, the running back ZAP Model bakes in some pre-draft projected capital, and Black was expected to go closer to Pick 180. So within the model, his draft capital looks more like 106 than 90. Still, the landing spot is intriguing. San Francisco needs a backup after Brian Robinson left for Atlanta, and Christian McCaffrey is about to be a 30-year-old back coming off a 413-touch season. Unfortunately, the team's history at the position - combined with Black's profile - makes this far from a slam dunk. Black followed a familiar path for Indiana players, starting at James Madison before following Curt Cignetti. His career began in 2020, when he redshirted. He then started his second season strong before a knee injury ended it after three games. From there, he played two more seasons at James Madison and two at Indiana. He's not young - already 24 and a half - and while we've seen a few older backs find mild success of late (largely due to transfer rules and NIL), age is still a clear negative. The production profile doesn't help. Black posted a modest 10.3% best-season reception share - a number you'd expect to be higher given his experience. His best-season adjusted total yards per team play sits at just 1.12, the same as Jadarian Price's. And, as we know, Price was playing behind an elite teammate. Black was not. Since Kyle Shanahan arrived in 2017, here are the running backs San Francisco has drafted (excluding Black): Jordan James, Isaac Guerendo, Tyrion Davis-Price, Trey Sermon, Elijah Mitchell, and Joe Williams. Most of those players were reaches when comparing projected draft capital to actual. In fact, Davis-Price, Williams, and now Black are three of the top-six biggest reaches in ZAP Model database history. So yes, the landing spot is appealing. And running back is a position where talent isn't everything - if a coaching staff wants a player on the field, that player can produce. But history says to be careful. players.elevator_pitch:
Black is a smart runner with cutback ability who also attacks downhill with ferocity for his size. He 
finds cutbacks with deep presses into the line, but when it's time to hit a tight crease as designed, Black gets skinny. 
Black has good footwork, understands how to manipulate defenders with pace and stride changes, and he has dynamic 
jump stops and spins to avoid late shots. If Black can get his feet higher in traffic, he could become even tougher to bring to 
the turf. 
Black is quick enough to reach the secondary and fast enough to make defensive backs chase him down after flipping the 
field. At the end of runs, Black delivers contact with excellent pad level. He forces defenders to wrap him, and he'll push and 
drag defenders, bouncing off multiple reaches and/or hits to his frame. 
Black has enough skills as a pass-protector and pass-catcher that these facets of his game shouldn't hold him back from 
competing for playing time this year if the depth chart is thin enough or Black impresses fast enough. 
Black reminds me of Jordan James or a slower version of J.K. Dobbins without the same refinement, but enough versatility 
to continue growing into committee-level asset. 
Vision: Black displays enough patience with heavy run boxes to adjust his approach to buy time for creases to open but 
when they don't, he takes the nearest soft spot at the line available and makes the most of the play. 
Black also presses stalk blocks at the second level, pressing within a step of one side of the blocker to set up the bounce or 
cutback to the open crease. 
Black identifies interior penetration as he approaches the line and bounces the run away from the defender. 
In short-yardage situations, Black has attacked the designed crease hard, hitting it decisively with his pads low. There are 
short-yardage runs where he should have done this but opted to press deep and bounce it when there was a crease to hit 
decisively up the middle. He got the yardage, but the decision wasn't optimal. 
Black presses the back-side hip of the puller to set up gap runs working to the outside. He presses inside gaps to set up 
perimeter designs. He's a decisive gap runner whether it's an interior run or a perimeter design. 
Black gets skinny through creases after attacking the press point at a good pace and cutting back. 
Elusiveness: Black adjusts his gait and pace as he approaches the line of scrimmage on interior run designs. He points 
outside foot to the boundary to open his hips for 90-degree turns. He can do this to make transitions at the edge of the box 
with two steps and make the force defender miss. 
Black has a capable jump stop at the edge of a crease to adjust his gait and pace so he can set up a block at the second 
level. 
Black only needs two quick steps to transition from a perimeter approach to a downhill path. He spins off glancing shots to 
get downhill for an extra 1-3 yards when he doesn't keep his feet.

players.pre_draft_advice:
Black is a patience play, but he could pop if there are training camp injuries on a depth 
chart. He'll likely endear himself to coaches and teammates with his competitive demeanor, smarts, and physical playing 
style. 
Black would be an unlikely full-time starter, but he has the profile of a player who might have 1-2 massive years due to a 
season-ending injury as the reliable replacement behind a good offensive line. He's a late-round value.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Black runs with light feet and punches above his weight class to consistently get more yards than expected. Once in an NFL camp, his ability to prove himself as a pass catcher will be critical to carving out a part-time role.

players.name: Bryce Lance
players.lateround_profile: The Saints took Jordyn Tyson in Round 1, followed it up with tight end Oscar Delp in Round 3, and then added Bryce Lance late in Round 4. From a fit standpoint, it makes sense. Lance is a big-bodied perimeter receiver who can stretch the field and complement their existing playmakers - if he develops. That's a big "if." Lance spent five years at North Dakota State, redshirting as a true freshman and mostly playing special teams over the next two seasons. Through 2023, he had just one career reception. At an FCS powerhouse, he didn't see meaningful production until his fourth year on campus - after turning 23. To his credit, the final two seasons were productive. He posted a 2.70 prorated receiving yards per team pass attempt in 2024 and followed it with a massive 4.00 mark in 2025. But once you adjust for competition, that 4.00 drops below 2.50. And it's the only meaningful adjusted season of his career. With such a late breakout, Lance doesn't register a Breakout Score. That's not uncommon. What is uncommon is becoming fantasy relevant without one. Among more than 100 wide receivers in the ZAP Model without a Breakout Score, only Hunter Renfrow, Tyreek Hill, and Terry McLaurin have posted a double-digit PPR points-per-game season within their first three years. Hill is a unicorn. McLaurin had strong draft capital. Renfrow's peak didn't last long. Lance has traits. He's 6'3", 204 pounds and ran a 4.34 40-yard dash at the Combine. But this is a bet on tools over production. And those bets don't hit often, even if the upside is there. I just like this fit more from a real football perspective than a fantasy one. players.etr_notes: Older prospect from a smaller school, but shredded the Combine. Hopefully the long-term WR3.
players.elevator_pitch:
Trey Lance's younger brother has the promise to make a name for himself to the degree that media 
will either lead with, "Bryce Young's older brother," in a few years when introducing Trey, or they'll use "the Lance 
brothers," if Trey gets a second chance to emerge. 
Bryce, like Trey, has the physical skills of a top-notch NFL starter. He's big, fast, quick, and explosive, which gives him a 
high ceiling as an outside receiver.

players.pre_draft_advice:
Lance is a must-draft option for leagues with at least 35 roster spots. For smaller rosters 
with practice squads, Lance could be available in the late half of the third or the fourth round and worth stashing for year. 
There is a chance a team falls in love with Lance's ceiling and selects him on Day Two of the NFL Draft. If this happens, 
Lance's draft capital will likely force him into the lineup early. There will be heightened danger for Lance to fail for the 
reasons described above and the draft capital will likely raise Lance's fantasy ADP. There will be safer options at this point, 
but I'd still recommend him as a consideration if you have the luxury to take a chance on his ceiling.

players.name: Demond Claiborne
players.lateround_profile: You've seen it in other profiles, but for Day 3 running backs to hit, they often need to land in semi-ambiguous backfields. If their teammates aren't being drafted highly in season-long formats, it's a signal that there's no clear starter. And where there's uncertainty, there's opportunity. Demond Claiborne lands in one of those situations in Minnesota. Size could be a limiting factor. At 5'10", 188 pounds, the Wake Forest product handled a solid - but not dominant - workload across four seasons. His peak came as a junior with 228 carries, the only time he cleared 200 attempts. There's some pass-game involvement, with a 13.1% best-season reception share. But the impact was modest. He never exceeded a 0.63 adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt mark, and once you factor in age and strength of schedule, that falls below the class average. The straight-line speed is real - he ran a 4.37 40-yard dash at the Combine. The issue is that the big-play profile doesn't fully align with that speed. Per PFF, Claiborne posted a 12.3% explosive run rate, below the class average of 13.8%. Over the last five years, the only back under 200 pounds with an explosive run rate below 15% to even reach 4 PPR points per game in a season is Kyren Williams - and Williams had a much stronger overall production profile. Claiborne also didn't stand out in missed tackles forced or yards after contact per attempt. For a lighter back, you'd ideally want at least one of those traits to pop. This looks more like a complementary back than a true bell-cow - and that's fine. This class isn't loaded with high-end running back talent, and Claiborne's skill set pairs well with Minnesota's current early-down option, Jordan Mason. There's also a longer-term angle. Both Mason and Aaron Jones are set to hit free agency next year, so if Claiborne flashes at all, there's a path to more volume in 2027. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 92
players.etr_notes: On the smaller side, but ran well and has a strong pass-catching background.
players.elevator_pitch:
Claiborne has some LeSean "Shady" McCoy to his game. Definitely not the weight, we'll get to that 
soon enough. It's Claiborne's pace changes, peripheral vision, ability to flip his hips to create sudden and sharp escape 
angles. 
Shady often looked like he was walking a tightrope, a matador facing off a bull, or Oberon performing acrobatics against the 
Mountain. Claiborne possesses that similar sense of control and bravado at the razor's edge. 
Claiborne changes of direction are notably quick because he's skilled at playing with pacing. He presses slow and deep 
before entering the crease with breathtaking suddenness. 
There's a legitimate feel for the flow of the defense. Whether that comes from studying box counts pre-snap, his peripheral 
vision, or advanced processing that comes from extensive experience, Claiborne has it. 
As daring as Claiborne's runs can look, he's also a wise decision-maker who takes what the defense gives him and doesn't 
take risks in the red zone and other short-yardage situations. This may not show up early in Claiborne's game in the NFL, 
because he may have to recalibrate his gauge when it comes to the speed and quickness of box defenders. 
Claiborne pairs head and shoulder fakes with footwork better than most backs I've watched in recent years. Watching 
Claiborne move with the football is like listening to a musician who can tell a lyrical story with impressive technique versus 
musician who shreds with technical patterns but hasn't learned to make it as musical. 
While Claiborne may need to recalibrate his gauge for defensive pursuit as a mover, he has excellent speed and 
acceleration, so don't expect a massive struggle. Clairborne turns the corner all three levels of the defense, including 
cornerbacks. He'll hold off most defenders in the open field with a clean lane - or clean enough to weave his open-field 
magic. 
Claiborne's power is good for his size, but that power is ordinary by RB standards. He'll run through reaches, bounce off 
glancing shots and win some direction collision as the aggressor into contact. He can drag or push defenders when wrapped 
high. 
At his current weight, Claiborne doesn't pull through wraps, and his contact balance is limited. If there's an area that could 
limit Claiborne's ceiling as a professional, it's the 188-pound frame. 
There will be legitimate questions about how much of a workload Claiborne can handle each week. Will the league prove too 
punishing? 
Jahvid Best was a great young running back, but concussions forced him to retire after three years in the league. Best, a 
dynamic speedster, was 5'9", 190 pounds. 
There are questions about Bucky Irving's workload moving forward. The 5'10", 195-pound runner couldn't get healthy last 
year after suffering an injury that usually doesn't take so long to heal. 
71
players.pre_draft_advice:
Claiborne is a second-round pick in rookie drafts regardless of roster size. His draft capital 
might not be as early as his skills suggest it should be, but he's a strong bet for big plays and eventually a role as a 
backfield's big-play co-star or dynamic lead. 
74
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Claiborne is a bursty athlete (both linearly and laterally) who can give defenders the slip with his violent cuts, although his size profile likely will limit his role and landing spots. He has the mismatch-creating athleticism and receiving upside to be a diet version of De’Von Achane.

players.name: Matthew Hibner
players.lateround_profile: After a big run on tight ends on Day 2, it took a while for the position to be called upon on Day 3. Matthew Hibner was the first tight end taken in Round 4 when he went 133rd overall to the Baltimore Ravens, joining a tight end room that is pretty barren after the team parted ways with Isaiah Likely in the offseason. Mark Andrews is locked up through the 2028 season, so we can't gloss over that, but Durham Smythe's contract is up after 2026. And that's really it. So, there is a pretty immediate opportunity for Hibner to be the TE2 in Baltimore. What makes Hibner the TE10 in the class, though? It's a good question because he was projected to be a seventh-round pick (or undrafted) for most of the lead-in to the draft. Hibner spent six years in college (four at Michigan, including 2020 when he saw no action as a freshman, and two at Southern Methodist). Despite half a dozen collegiate seasons, Hibner failed to exceed 1.0 yards per team attempt in any season after adjusting for age and schedule, and he ultimately lands in the 20th percentile by best-season max yards per team attempt in the tight end database. However, Hibner did have competition at tight end in his career. Kind of a lot of it. The past two seasons at SMU, he shared the field with RJ Maryland, who was also invited to the NFL Combine this year. Before that, he was at Michigan, overlapping with Colston Loveland, AJ Barner, and Marlin Klein (who went 59th this year in the draft). So, no, the production isn't amazing, but the draft capital is solid - and so is the landing spot. Now, add in the athleticism, and we have a better picture of the potential here. Hibner ran a 4.57-second 40-yard dash at the NFL Combine. That was fifth-best among tight ends who ran and equates to a height-adjusted Speed Score over 115. He's yet another athletic tight end without a complete production profile that an NFL team bought in on during the 2026 NFL Draft, perhaps signaling a sea change for how franchises value tight end prospects in the modern NFL. Athleticism and traits may be winning out over raw receiving production. Hibner is already 24, though, and that's the biggest knock of all. Recalibrated comps for Hibner at better draft capital than expected would paint a picture more like a Luke Willson or perhaps a Chig Okonwko on the optimistic end of things. If there's an NFL team seeking a TE2 for 12 personnel sets who can stretch defenses when the offense runs 
misdirection and play-action, Hibner offers a combination of straight-line speed, ball-tracking, and blocking that will meet an 
offense's needs. 
Hibner's size is likely capped, and it means Hibner's best work as a blocker is against second- and third-level defenders. Still, he can 
help against defensive ends and there are isolated assignments against linebackers where, at worst, he won't fold immediately and 
at best, he could hold up. Hibner should develop into a good underneath receiver if he can develop weight drop into hard breaks and 
execute breaks further away from zone defenders.

Hibner is worth consideration in 1.5 PPR scoring formats for tight ends in large-roster leagues with 
practice squad spots during the summer when there are expanded rosters. Otherwise monitor from afar for 2-3 years.

players.name: Adam Randall
players.lateround_profile: We seem to get a wide receiver-to-running back convert in every draft class. This year, it's Adam Randall. The key difference between Randall and recent converts like Brashard Smith and Tyrone Tracy is size. Randall checks in north of 230 pounds. In ZAP Model history, he's just one of 11 backs at that weight to post a best-season reception share above 10%. Most of the players in that bucket - Saquon Barkley, Leonard Fournette, Eddie Lacy, Jeremy Hill, James Conner, Najee Harris - came with strong draft capital. The ones who didn't haven't given us much at the NFL level. That's where things get tricky. Randall's profile is easy to like on the surface: big-bodied former receiver, positional versatility, untapped upside. But the underlying data is underwhelming. His reception share clears 10%, but his best adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt is just 0.59 - seventh-worst in the class. His adjusted total yards per team play ranks fifth-worst. He only spent one of his four collegiate seasons at Clemson playing running back, logging 168 carries in 2025. With that kind of limited experience, you wouldn't expect a polished profile. And you don't get one. Despite the size, Randall ranked below average in both missed tackles forced per attempt and yards after contact per attempt. For a player built like a power back, the power metrics don't stand out. Interestingly, the landing spot might actually help, even if it doesn't look that way at face value. Most wouldn't be excited about a back sitting behind Derrick Henry, but for Randall, it's probably ideal. He needs time to learn the position, and there's no better way to do so than behind a future Hall of Famer. Randall's still under 22 and very raw as a running back. In a class lacking obvious upside swings, Randall is at least worth keeping on your radar in the later rounds of rookie drafts. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 90
players.etr_notes: Freaky athlete at 232 pounds.
players.elevator_pitch:
Big and fast for his size, Randall earns buzz for his potential to become an excellent professional. 
Former pros in the NFL Draft media have praised Randall's studious nature and approach to the game. 
A mature and professional approach to football can get a player far. Randall has some road to travel if he's going to 
graduate from an RB3/RB4 or practice squad talent to a starter in 2-3 years. 
While Randall has speed, he needs a long runway to build that speed. Some will cite Randall's size will help him break 
tackles as he makes room on that runway, but without excellent acceleration, he'll rarely reach top speed with the ball in his 
hands. 
Every great back from Jim Brown to the present has mentioned acceleration as the chief physical characteristic that matters 
for running backs. It's a form of speed and power combined, and Randall lacks it. 
A former wide receiver, Randall would have been moved to tight end if he had strong receiving skills. He doesn't. He's late 
with getting his eyes to the quarterback out of his breaks, and his 3-step breaks on timing routes need 1-2 extra steps. 
Randall has good hands when the ball is on his frame. When forced to extend high or away from his frame, his hands are 
too wide for him to attack the ball with reliability. 
Randall's size should be an asset against contact. Sometimes it is. His pad level needs work. On some runs, his pads are too 
high, and he gets caught with hits into his frame that he could have run through. On other runs, Randall brings his pads 
over his waist and loses balance before he even reaches the point of attack. 
Randall has adapted well to the decision-making required of a between-the-tackles runner. He's patient, finds cutback 
opportunities, and reads leverage well. 
Randall could become an effective gap runner soon. With time, he could also do some damage with inside zone. Randall is 
also a capable blocker, but there are a lot of small flaws with his game that needs precise details that are lacking. 
If Randall fixes the blocking improves his routes, and gauges how to use his pads better, he could become a situational 
producer. If his acceleration is worse than I've graded, and it's possible, he might never emerge as a viable option in an NFL 
backfield. 
Presuming that Randall will play quicker as he learns the position better, we could see Randall emerge as a "B" back in a 
committee in 2-3 years. 
Vision: Randall presses within a step of a puller to the puller's outside shoulder to set up a cutback to the inside. He 
presses with a two-step move downhill after bouncing outside to set up a safety and then bounce outside him. 
Randall identifies cutback opportunities on gap plays, pressing to the play side of the puller, reading the leverage of the 
defender, and accessing the cutback lane. 
Randall can press within a step of the line on an inside run and bounce the play one gap over for a significant gain. 
142
players.pre_draft_advice:
Randall is worth a stash in deep leagues with practice squads - think at least 30 roster 
spots. If the NFL Draft capital is somehow strong (and absurd), Randall could be worth a pick in the final rounds if he lasts 
that long, just because he'll get opportunities and it could translate to his learning curve being faster than expected. 
I'm monitoring from afar, but I'm trying to be open to the good possibilities.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Randall plays like more of an athlete than a natural running back, but he has the versatility that can be useful at the next level. If he develops in pass protection, he can earn a role as a third-down NFL back with special teams potential.

players.name: Oscar Delp
players.lateround_profile: Oscar Delp was selected 73rd overall by the New Orleans Saints, so he'll be joining an older tight end room and have a chance to flash his athleticism in a dome for half of his games. That's fun. Delp ran a 4.48-second 40-yard dash at his Georgia pro day, and even adjusting that for some potential pro day bias, he grades out with the second-best height-adjusted Speed Score in the class behind only Kenyon Sadiq. Delp's production data is pretty weak, though. His max yards per team attempt and his Breakout Score hover around the 25th percentile within the tight end database. But he also had a lot of competition at Georgia. Brock Bowers and Ladd McConkey were there for his first two seasons, and Darnell Washington was fifth on the team in receptions in Delp's freshman season. So, did he surge after they were gone? No, not quite. In Delp's final season alongside Bowers and McConkey, he reeled in 24 catches for 284 yards and 3 touchdowns. After they left, he put up lines of 21-248-4 and 20-261-1. Overall, though, the tight end model likes Delp a bit, given that it forgives some of the production concerns because of the combination of elite athleticism and strong draft capital. There's more to Delp than a pure bet on traits (he had some production in college, just not a ton). The Saints' tight end room has just one player younger than 25 (Moliki Matavao, a seventh- rounder from last year) in it. They bought into the offense with Jordyn Tyson in Round 1, and while Juwan Johnson and Noah Fant are each under contract for two more years, they're 30 and 29 already, respectively. Delp likely isn't projecting for a huge rookie season, but in a few years, he could be a cornerstone for the Saints' offense. There's a lot to like about Oscar Delp's game, but I was hoping I'd love it. The reason that love falls short has to do 
with his receiving performances against contact. 
Delp can earn the ball against tight coverage. He can take a wrap to his frame while catching a target. When a defender delivers a

players.pre_draft_advice:
Consider Delp a worthwhile mid-to-late stash in your drafts depending on the scoring system. 
Injuries, playing behind Brock Bowers, system changes, and instability at quarterback are potential reasons why Delp didn't emerge 
as Georgia expected when they recruited him. 
There's a lot more refinement to Delp's game than his lack of buzz indicates. If he can't add muscle, it might only lead to Delp 
having a long career as a skilled backup, but the price will probably be right to find out.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Delp has an underwhelming college resume, but grading to the flashes shows an NFL player who can impact games as a receiver and blocker. He is a prime candidate to be a better pro than he was a college player.

players.name: Brenen Thompson
players.lateround_profile: The ZAP Model doesn't love Brenen Thompson because he's 164 pounds and had a Breakout Score of 35.4, a 41st percentile tally. In the ZAP Model database, the only sub- 180-pound wide receivers to post a 15-plus PPR points-per-game season within their first three years are first-rounders DeVonta Smith and Jordan Addison. Smaller receivers can still offer usable production - think Josh Downs or Darnell Mooney - but many of those mini-hits came with significantly stronger Breakout Scores than Thompson's. But screw it. This is a fun player. Thompson spent one year at Texas, two at Oklahoma, and one at Mississippi State, where he finished his college career. He didn't eclipse 250 receiving yards in a season until that final campaign, when he broke out for 57 receptions, 1,054 yards, and 6 touchdowns. His size also shouldn't automatically remove him from consideration. Interestingly, he barely played in the slot in college, running just 12.2% of his routes there, per PFF. On only 86 slot routes, though, he posted a ridiculous 4.15 yards per route run, the highest mark in the class. It's a small sample, but it hints at some untapped deployment flexibility. And then there's the 4.26 speed. Thompson's ability to stress defenses vertically - he posted the fifth-highest deep-ball target rate in the class - is something the Chargers will try to utilize. New offensive coordinator Mike McDaniel loves speed, and there's no doubt in my mind that he played a role in selecting Thompson. The Chargers aren't exactly loaded at wide receiver, either. Ladd McConkey is a beast, but Quentin Johnston still has plenty of question marks attached to him, and Tre Harris didn't get a lot done as a rookie. Thompson, at least, can play the role of burner in that offense, likely in heavier wide receiver packages as a rookie. The odds of Thompson becoming a high-volume receiver are small - just being honest. We don't see this kind of profile hit very often. But the pairing with McDaniel makes him more intriguing than he would've been elsewhere. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 56
players.elevator_pitch:
Thompson has elite speed, good route breaks that should get better, and sound hands with only rare 
technical glitches. Wrap Thompson in the open field and he's done, but that's the trick that is difficult for defenders to figure 
out because of his stop-start agility, decision-making, quickness, and speed with the ball in his hands. 
196
players.pre_draft_advice:
Monitor Thompson from afar in all but the largest leagues - 50-plus roster spots and 
practice squad slots - unless return specialists have value in your scoring system.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Thompson is undersized and not built for traffic but offers the score-from-anywhere type of speed to be a dynamic contributor in the quick game or downfield. If he stays healthy, he has upside as a No. 3 WR for an NFL offense looking for his specific skill set.

players.name: Josh Cuevas
players.lateround_profile: Josh Cuevas was the second tight end that the Baltimore Ravens drafted during the 2026 NFL Draft. They first took Southern Methodist's Matthew Hibner (older, bigger, and more athletic than Cuevas) with pick 133 and then circled back to the position, grabbing Cuevas 40 picks later, 173rd overall. But it kind of makes sense for Cuevas to have an indirect path to an NFL team: he played for three different schools - Cal Poly, Washington, and Alabama - in five years. As you may expect, with a trajectory like that, the Breakout Score for Cuevas isn't anything to get excited about (31st percentile). Obviously, there was increased competition with each of his landing spots, but we're tying a lot of the receiving belief in Cuevas' profile to his fifth college season at Alabama, where he was able to post a 50th-percentile max yards per team attempt rate after adjusting for schedule and age. When seeking fantasy football potential, a level of production like that is enticing for a late-round pick. And after a deeper look at that receiving data, there are some red flags, like that Cuevas' average depth of target in 2025 wasn't great (it was better than for other Alabama tight ends - but not by a lot). There are also some green flags, like that Cuevas' career yards after the catch per reception was better than average for incoming tight end prospects, and you see that potential on tape. He split out wide at the highest career rate (in his three years from Washington and Alabama, via PFF) among this year's class. Given his size and skillset, Cuevas is very much in the fullback/H-tight end category and might wind up being more of a safety valve-type tight end than a primary pass-catcher in the NFL. This puts him into a bit of a high-floor, low-ceiling tier. Not only will there be competition behind Mark Andrews, but there will also be competition with Hibner, who is more athletic (but older) than Cuevas (still just 22 years old). This again suggests more of a hybrid (i.e., low-fantasy ceiling) role for Cuevas, who just barely sneaks into the Waiver Wire Add category and who just narrowly avoids a "high risk" profile. players.name: Justin Joly
players.lateround_profile: Justin Joly is a converted wide receiver with really strong age-adjusted production numbers (including an 88th-percentile Breakout Score among the tight end database). He was a borderline top-100 pick at various points in the pre-draft process but was finally taken off the board 152nd overall by the Denver Broncos. While the draft capital was lower than we'd like, it's actually a solid landing spot in a tight end room led by Evan Engram and featuring just one name younger than 27 years old (Caleb Lohner is 25, and he was a seventh-round pick last year). Joly hasn't turned 22 yet. Joly didn't test athletically, and he doesn't look like a game-changer on film due to pure speed and burst. Also, he wound up weighing 241 pounds at the NFL Combine despite being listed over 260 in his final year at North Carolina State. It just wasn't the best pre- draft process for Joly, and his stock dropped. However, if you want age-adjusted production, he has that. I mentioned the 88th-percentile Breakout Score already, and that pairs with 84th-percentile max yards per team attempt data. And the trajectory itself is intriguing. A high school wide receiver, Joly began his college career slowly with 18 catches as a freshman at Connecticut. As a sophomore, he led the team with 56 receptions for 578 yards and 2 touchdowns. He then transferred to North Carolina State, continuing his rise with a team-high 661 yards and 4 touchdowns on 43 catches while playing alongside KC Concepcion, a first-round pick in 2026, during the 2024 season. As a senior, Joly led the Wolfpack in receptions (49) and touchdown catches (7). His NFL career path just might come down to the draft capital. In the tight end ZAP Model, around 10% of prospects went on to log a best season (within their first three NFL years) of at least 10.0 PPR points per game. For players drafted between 100 and 200, that drops to around 6.4%. Of the ones who actually did put up good NFL seasons, they share stellar marks in max yards per team attempt. Excluding Jordan Cameron and Julius Thomas (who were basketball players in college), the hits from this draft tier averaged an 85th-percentile max yards per team attempt. Joly (84th) has that in his favor. The age-adjusted production and the landing spot in particular keep me reasonably optimistic about Joly's outlook in Denver. players.elevator_pitch:
Joly is one of my favorites in this tight end class. He's an elite pass catcher with a route game that can become 
starter-caliber with a year of work. 
The biggest concerns with Joly are his stature, and his lack of top-end speed. Both issues place caps on his potential despite having

Joly is worth a late-round pick in deep PPR formats and a mid-round pick after the third round in 
1.5 PPR systems.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Joly is a versatile pass catcher, because of his athleticism, ball skills and upside as a three-level threat. Built in the mold of Jonnu Smith, he is more Robin than Batman and ideally suited as a “move” tight end in a 12-personnel offense.

players.name: Caleb Douglas
players.lateround_profile: Entering the draft, Miami's wide receiver depth chart was arguably the weakest in the league. Taking Caleb Douglas in Round 3 didn't do much to change that. Maybe Douglas proves the ZAP Model wrong, but there's not a lot to like here. He was projected to go outside the top 200, according to NFL Mock Draft Database, and the underlying metrics reflect that. Douglas posted a 25th percentile Breakout Score, a 28th percentile career yards per route run, a 15th percentile best-season yards per route run, a 23rd percentile first downs per route run, a...you get the idea. One of the bigger concerns is where and how he wins. Douglas ran just 15% of his routes from the slot, but his efficiency on the perimeter was especially poor - just 1.65 yards per route run, a 21st percentile mark. At 6'4", 206 pounds, he's almost certainly going to be asked to play outside in the NFL. According to PFF data, Douglas is just the ninth Day 2 wide receiver to enter the league since 2020 with a career perimeter yards per route run below 1.80. None of the previous eight have produced more than 12.5 PPR points per game in any of their first three seasons. He'll get a chance to see the field - that depth chart all but guarantees it. But if you're throwing a dart at a Miami wide receiver from this draft, Chris Bell offers a more appealing ceiling. We'll just have to see where the dynasty market places Douglas to know if he's a clear target or avoid - all we do know is that this is clearly a high-risk profile. players.etr_notes: Opportunity abounds.
players.elevator_pitch:
A tall speedster with good length, Texas Tech featured Douglas in one-on-one scenarios at the 
boundary where he could use his dimensions and body control to earn big plays. The Red Raiders also manufactured 
opportunities for Douglas to work uncovered up the seams and flats of a zone stretched too thin. 
Douglas has the athletic ability to become a competent NFL big-play threat, but he must develop greater nuance with how 
he executes the abundance of footwork techniques he uses to release from the line of scrimmage. Douglas is better at 
delivering his route setups with greater nuance, but he still has refinement ahead there, too.
