players.name: Eli Heidenreich
players.lateround_profile: Chances are, as a seventh-round pick in a pretty throwaway landing spot, Eli Heidenreich won't become anything meaningful for fantasy football. But, man, what a cool story and profile. Heidenreich went to Mt. Lebanon High School, right outside Pittsburgh. (I went to North Allegheny, also right outside Pittsburgh, but we won't get into which high school is better. Now isn't the time.) He then spent his college career at Navy. And, well, service academies don't throw the ball. Since 2011, Army, Navy, and Air Force have combined to reach 300 team pass attempts in a season just twice. So when you're evaluating a player in that kind of environment, the numbers can get... weird. That's exactly what happened with Heidenreich in 2025. Heidenreich was a productive piece of Navy's offense for three seasons, but 2025 was the breakout: 51 receptions and a staggering 48.1% prorated reception share. That's the highest mark in ZAP Model history. By roughly 23 percentage points. He's not a traditional running back. He's more of a Swiss Army knife - a movable chess piece who can align in the backfield, in the slot, or out wide. He carried the ball 77 times last season but was deployed all over the formation as a receiver. Any team drafting him is doing so with that versatility in mind. And the receiving metrics? They're absurd. His adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt sits at 4.96 - the best mark in the model's database. Second place? Christian McCaffrey at 2.36. Yeah. For real. Like I said at the top, the odds aren't great that Heidenreich becomes anything meaningful for fantasy purposes. These tweener profiles usually need a very specific situation to hit. But this class isn't very good. At all. So I'm going to say something I don't say often as a process-oriented analyst: Why not have a little fun and shoot for the outlier? Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 91
players.etr_notes: High-upside athlete in a fairly unsettled backfield.
players.elevator_pitch:
Heidenreich has special teams promise as a return specialist and/or coverage option who can 
contribute to an offense as a fourth or fifth receiver but more likely as a utility player who earns as many touches as a 
running back as he does a WR3-WR5. 
Heidenreich has the ball-carrying skills on the perimeter and the open field to deliver as an extension of the ground game 
from a variety of spots. He tracks, adjusts, and catches the ball effectively in the capacity of a zone receiver, an outlet from 
the backfield, or on schemed plays up the seam or boundary for big plays. 
For Heidenreich to become a wide receiver, he must develop a complete route game, and he's not as far from that 
possibility as his role with Navy might lead you to believe. He has developed techniques for releases, setups, and breaks, 
but he must expand the precision of their execution beyond the shallow range of the field. 
Developing these skills may prove easier than trying to develop a ground game between the tackles and add 10-15 pounds 
of muscle to try to become an every-down back. Heidenreich has limited experience as a runner who works between the 
tackles. This is a bigger challenge to learn than what he must learn to compete at wide receiver. 
Expect Heidenreich to begin his career as a return specialist or coverage player while competing for situational opportunities 
in an offense in a utility role. 
Vision: In the open field, Heidenreich is patient with luring the safety downhill before delivering a stick and spin 
combination at the boundary to the inside. 
He's primarily an option runner taking tosses or jet sweeps. 
Elusiveness: When earning a run to the near-side edge, Heidenreich can transition downhill from a perimeter approach 
with one step. To the far-side edge, he needs two steps. 
In the open field, Heidenreich is patient with luring the safety downhill before delivering a stick and spin combination at the 
boundary to the inside. He gets his feet over low shots. 
Heidenreich has good curvilinear bend when working around end and still inside the hash. He has a nice two-quick cut inside 
downhill pursuit from a safety to get by the defender. 
He's quick enough and has enough moves with spins, pressure, cuts and two-quick footwork to make multiple defenders 
miss during a run. 
Acceleration: Heidenreich can turn the short corner on linebackers, but the backside linebacker will run him down after 
getting 5-10 yards downhill. The same is true with runs to the long corner. He'll turn the corner, but the backside linebacker 
will run him down 5-10 yards later. 
Speed: Heidenreich has the speed to flip the field and hold off defensive backs for long gains, if not breakaway runs 
depending on the angle. 
Power: Heidenreich can run through reaches from defensive backs. He uses a stiff arm to swath away from reaches. He 
keeps his feet moving when wrapped high. 
Direct Contact Balance: He can stalemate a safety. 
Indirect Contact Balance: Heidenreich will drop his pads and finish, colliding with a cornerback and running through the 
hit to extend past the defender. 
Releases: Heidenreich has a staggered stance with 80/20 weight distribution favoring his front foot. His hands rest at 
either side of his front knee. When he begins his release, he sinks into his stance and rolls off his front foot without waisted 
movement. 
Heidenreich uses sticks at the top of stems to set up a break. He has a read step that he pairs with a sudden two-quick 
inside to break outside on a speed turn. He counters the defender's hands with a wipe or a swim. 
Heidenreich must create a greater contrast between patience and suddenness with his releases. He'll uses footwork to be 
patient and then accelerate into his stem, but he must develop patience and suddenness with the actual footwork move. 
194
players.pre_draft_advice:
While there's a chance he could develop in full-time offensive player with fantasy value, 
Heidenreich is likely a utility option with potential special teams value who you can monitor from afar in most formats. 
Heidenreich has a nominally higher score than some of the players in his tier who I like more as investments because of his 
versatility.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Heidenreich has dynamic pass-catching ability that can be weaponized in a hybrid role by an NFL offense (think closer to Danny Woodhead than Kyle Juszczyk). He won’t be a fit for everybody, but he will be a fit for somebody.

players.name: Zavion Thomas
players.lateround_profile: Zavion Thomas was one of those picks that had me doing the confused Nick Young meme. I even mentioned him on a podcast recently as an interesting late-round dart throw in rookie drafts, but never in a million years did I expect Day 2 draft capital. The only reason he came up at all was because this class lacks clear later-round wide receiver targets. There are a couple of positives. Carnell Tate is the only receiver in this class younger than Thomas. He's not an early declare, though - he spent two seasons at Mississippi State and two more at LSU. Thomas also brings speed and versatility. He logged 40 carries for 198 yards in college and handled 60 punt returns and 35 kick returns. That kind of usage screams gadget player - someone who can carve out a role and occasionally give you a spike week. That's likely what Ben Johnson and the Bears had in mind. Because Thomas doesn't profile as a traditional wide receiver. He failed to register a Breakout Score despite being one of the youngest players in the class. His best season in receiving yards per team pass attempt didn't even reach 1.60 - a mark that all but five receivers in this class surpassed. That's a tough starting point. Historically, just three wide receivers in ZAP Model history have reached 12 PPR points per game in one of their first three seasons without ever posting a Breakout Score. The efficiency metrics don't help. Thomas posted a 1.52 career yards per route run, per PFF, the sixth-worst mark in this class. On the perimeter, he was even less effective, ranking fourth-worst among Combine invites in yards per route run. All in all, this looks like a move by Chicago to add a player who can make things happen with the ball in his hands, but he's also one who may not have the ball very often. Especially in an offense loaded with young talent. As my friend Matt Harmon said to me after the pick was made, this could be Ben Johnson's Kalif Raymond role replacement. (Raymond is currently a Bear, for the record.) That's a great way to view Thomas. Despite the draft capital, Thomas is still just a dart throw. But I'm sure most fantasy gamers feel that way, so maybe he'll find himself onto some of our teams. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 52
players.etr_notes: Likely overdrafted for his special teams ability, but is blazing fast and could earn WR reps long-term.
players.elevator_pitch:
Thomas's RSP Comparison Spectrum is one of athletic ability and versatility. Matthew Golden will likely 
develop into a leading starter for his team-an outside receiver who can play the cat-and-mouse game of deep threat who 
earns big plays as a short-yardage receiver after the catch that places defenses in a bind. 
A version of this player who has had a nice career in the NFL, but lacks annual appeal is former Texas star, Devin Duvernay. 
A skilled deep threat with a running back's background, Duvernay has become a Pro Bowl return specialist and viable 
reserve in the receiving game who has posted stretches of strong production when called upon. I always through teams 
192
players.pre_draft_advice:
Thomas is a viable late-round pick in drafts with rosters that have at least 25 spots. If 
Thomas goes to a team that uses 11 personnel (three receivers and one tight end) as its base offense, Thomas's 
opportunity to shine is better than if he goes to an offense that uses a lot of sets with two and three tight ends.

players.name: Taylen Green
players.etr_notes: Impressive athlete with solid peripheral numbers despite no real weapons.
players.elevator_pitch:
If you're not going to follow the RSP's strategic approach to rookie quarterbacks as a fantasy GM or you're in NFL personnel 
reading this material as a cross check of information, Green and Carson Beck - QB6 and QB7 on the RSP's board - are realistically closer to 
QB4 and QB5 when accounting for factors other than on-field performance. 
The NFL evaluation community hasn't been shy about leaking Diego Pavia's exploits. Pavia is central casting's pick to reprise Shane Falco 
with a bad-boy edge in "The Replacements." If that doesn't pan out, Pavia could be the QB of the dirty rival team in a Will Ferrell football 
sendup in a similar vein of "Talladega Nights." 
Pavia has pissed away, literally, the NFL's consideration of him as anything more than a late-round pick. It's more than just the incident 
linked to here, but this incident is allegedly indicative of what they fear about Pavia. 
There are questions about Cade Klubnik's velocity and Dabo Sweeney's Clemson offenses being too basic. These factors will also limit 
Klubnik's draft capital. 
It means Green and Beck will be included among the first or second tier of quarterbacks that the NFL will consider in April. Beck, as you'll 
read, will likely earn value for what he already has to offer while hoping the immediate experience will season his game. 
Green's value is a long-term bet on high-ceiling physical skills as he develops and/or refines the techniques and concepts of the position. 
Any quarterback that scores at least a 75 on the RSP's Depth of Talent Criteria has the skills to develop into a starting quarterback. 
Green operates well from Pistol because the alignment supports a stronger ground game than shotgun while also leveraging a quarterback's 
rushing ability from spread concepts. Green already possesses a sound play-action game with thorough and detailed fakes. 
These fakes can slow the defense and allow Green to assess whether he keeps the ball. When he does, he's a dynamic runner. 
Green drops his pads into contact, bounces off glancing shots to his frame and keeps his feet moving to extend forward after the contact. 
Green beats unblocked defensive ends and middle linebackers around the far-side corner on a keeper. He has also shown that he can slide, 
but he should do it more often. 
Green can transition downhill with one step or use tight curvilinear bend around the short-side end. He hurdles defenders when he can earn 
that final meaningful bit of space to cross the marker or pylon. 
Green also manipulates pursuit with good footwork. His burst and speed help him turn the corner on perimeter runs, split defenders up the 
middle, and extend the runs into open space. He can break away from pursuit for field-flipping runs as well as full-field gallops of more than 
60 yards. 
We've seen this combination of play-action and running work as a basis for an offense when Jalen Hurts, Lamar Jackson, and Colin 
Kaepernick were the quarterbacks. Green must develop and refine the rest of his game to emerge as a starter. 
Green's drops need refinement. He reaches a balanced stance, but there's a lot of wasted movement that disrupts efficient flow from the 
snap to the setup as he's trying to read the field. 
He's off to a good start with his release mechanics, but he has lapses where he doesn't point his front toe to the target and the ball travels 
closer to where his toe is facing. His arm motion is compact and he gets weight transfer, so velocity, balance, and stroke are all good 
enough as starting points for additional refinement as his QB coach will deem necessary. 
There is no question Green has arm talent. He also has moments of strong accuracy at NFL distances. There are strong examples of Green 
layering the ball over defenders with pinpoint accuracy in the intermediate and vertical ranges of the field. 
Green holds flat defenders and safeties in various areas of the field depending on the coverage. Green can cycle though a few reads against 
coverages like C3 and C2 and discern what's covered before going off-script and finding an open option. He targets receivers in sync with 
the progression and route. 
If you're an optimist, you can characterize Green as being on his way to developing more advanced quarterback skills that will serve him as 
a future NFL starter. Much of what's left to fix are common ills with prospects: 
Predetermining check-downs when greater awareness and patience would have led to a better decision. 
Failing to account for coverage on the periphery of the targeted receiver. 
• 
• 
• Greater selectivity with targets based on his throwing stance, pressure, and field position. 
• 
• 
• 
• 
Poor judgment with the leverage safeties are playing over the receiver. 
Poor judgment with the leverage corners are playing while trailing the receiver. 
Taking unnecessary risks with low-odds efforts when in the black zone. 
Checking down more often. 
74
players.pre_draft_advice:
If you're in a Super Flex format or league with big rosters, Green is a second-tier QB after Mendoza who is 
worth stashing on a practice squad for the potential upside.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Green needs to take better care of the football and refine his mechanics and passing rhythm, but he offers the physical traits and high character worth molding in a read-option, vertical passing game.

players.name: Cade Klubnik
players.etr_notes: Toolsy, may see some play time while the Jets figure out their path.
players.elevator_pitch:
Like Drew Allar, Klubnik was a nationally acclaimed high school prospect. Unlike Allar, Klubnik's game took a promising 
statistical leap forward in 2024, and the NFL Draft media touted Klubnik last spring as a potential first-round pick, if not the top prospect at 
his position. Klubnik didn't sustain that production in 2025 and led to a precipitous drop in the perceived NFL Draft stock. 
Allar is the Robo-QB who hasn't developed the craft of the position necessary to warrant an early-round pick. Klubnik is the inverse of Allar. 
He lacks prototypical size, arm strength, and athletic ability. These traits are all good enough to make an NFL team, but these are some of 
the questions teams will raise about Klubnik's potential as a starter. 
Another concern I've heard from those in the scouting game is that Clemson's offense and coaching regime doesn't advance the conceptual 
and technical skills of its passers while they are with the team. College football is big business, and development is a low priority with many 
big-time coaches. Winning is their primary job and there's a lot of pressure to do so. 
While this observation shared with me sounds like logo scouting, and my source admitted as much, one can make the subtle distinction it is 
not. These players all worked in a similar offense in the Dabo Sweeney regime. Logo scouting would be saying Oklahoma State has a history 
of great running backs but grouping Barry Sanders and Thurman Thomas with Chuba Hubbard and Ollie Gordon. 
When examining Klubnik's game, accuracy and velocity are the chief concerns. Klubnik has a habit of sailing passes in the intermediate 
zones, especially in the middle of the field. 
There are two potential reasons this is happening. Klubnik begins his release with a wide stance out of a three-step drop. His feet are wider 
than shoulder width. This contributes to targets that sail high in the short and intermediate ranges of the field. 
Another potential reason for his targets sailing is what happens when Klubnik rotates his front foot during the release. Klubnik pushes down 
on the toe of his front foot. This creates elevation upward as he follows through. This may contribute to the ball sailing on downfield routes 
in the middle of the field, and it was problematic throughout 2025 - most often early in the season. 
Klubnik must generate more velocity with far-sideline throws on intermediate outs and in the vertical game. Klubnik's late-outs require 
precise footwork to generate velocity, and while he has shown he can generate the velocity, he must do it with greater frequency. 
Klubnik has lapses with velocity where the ball is catchable, but the lack of zip forces the receiver to slow his pace and turn back inside with 
his break. This opens the passing lane for the cornerback to play a play on the ball. 
The middle of the field and timing routes to the intermediate and vertical boundary are areas where NFL starting quarterbacks must thrive. 
Klubnik's game raises questions whether it can. 
If Klubnik corrects these facets of his game, there's a lot to like - enough to like him as a future starter. His drops are efficient and have 
some advanced skills that could help him work well from center. 
Klubnik's lower body needs tweaking during his release, but his stroke is efficient and he can throw off platform. 
Klubnik exhausts three options and makes correct reads of leverage that leads to him holding the ball, resetting his position in the pocket, 
and finding an open receiver off script. When the sailing targets aren't a problem, Klubnik has demonstrated good placement in the middle 
of the field because he accounts for underneath coverage that is potentially in position to cut off the target. 
Klubnik also displays good anticipation under pressure in the pocket because he sees the leverage of defenders against zone routes that will 
break open. He'll split defenders with low placement so the receiver is the only option who can earn the ball in the shallow flat. 
Klubnik patient with the zone triangle - even after pivoting from a different quadrant of the field. He'll allow his leak route to influence the 
triangle so he can make a tight window throw a viable target with good placement against the defender's leverage. 
When breaking the pocket on the move, Klubnik is aware of backside receivers and can find the wide-open option across the field with no 
defender around. He'll also throw the ball away when routes are covered. 
Klubnik doesn't just play it safe, he makes layered throws over a dropping linebacker into the vertical ranges of the field. He even does this 
while side-stepping to his left from pressure. 
Klubnik hangs in compressed pocket for three reads, not panicking to escape as he pivots to the opposite side of the field to access that 
third read. 
These are promising field-reading skills paired with moments of advanced pocket management. The league covets players who can do this 
type of work. 
Klubnik has lapses and holes when reading the field. It's rare, but Klubnik has had moments where he doesn't account for the leverage of a 
defender on an intermediate out and tests the defender squatting on the route. 
62
players.pre_draft_advice:
Klubnik is worth a late-round pick in Super Flex formats or one-QB leagues with large rosters and practice 
squads. He's a luxury for most rosters as patience play, so most of you will monitor from afar.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Klubnik is a smooth-twitch athlete with the arm talent to make big-time throws, but his inconsistent pocket presence and urgency reading the field need to be addressed. Projecting as a backup, he would benefit from a rollout, rhythm-based passing game to help open throwing lanes.

players.name: Cole Payton
players.elevator_pitch:
Payton is an enticing athlete who possesses good speed and strong acceleration. He hurdles defenders in the open field, 
and he has the size to work through contact. 
He'll draw attention from the Robo-QB niche of NFL executives who inadvertently give double credit to athletic ability. There's a lot of work 
ahead. 
Payton's drops and releases lack precision and efficiency. His ball security exposes the team to turnovers. 
Payton identifies pressure pre-snap and finds quick solutions, but he has lapses where he overreacts to pressure and climbs pockets when 
unnecessary. He places himself in harm's way when this happens. Otherwise, he has a good pocket clock. 
Payton retreats from interior pressure and then doubts his decision mid-escape. He has plays where he stops a rollout as if he might reverse 
field or square and throw, but he then continues a full roll to the far-side boundary. Hopefully, Payton doesn't try this against NFL defenders 
with better quickness and angles of pursuit. 
Payton must become more selective with the maneuvers he chooses for the pressure that arrives. He chooses maneuvers that lead him into 
pressure he could have otherwise avoided. 
Payton cycles through 2-3 reads and breaks the pocket if he doesn't target a receiver. He understand how to use his legs to reshape an 
area of zone that's dense with coverage, stretching the window, and making the against-the-grain throw to the open receiver. He'll also 
check the ball after exhausting downfield options. 
Payton is willing to get aggressive when targeting a receiver between two defenders, placing the ball away from the defenders. He also 
places the ball away from dropping linebackers, throwing the receiver open with pinpoint placement to adjust away from the adjacent 
coverage. 
Payton has more difficulty IDing peripheral coverage above the target and peeling off a receiver to attack downhill on the intended option. 
Still, he does this well against safeties on short slants. He's also good at delivering a vertical route to a receiver breaking under a safety 
who is showing his back to the QB. 
Payton displays good anticipation in the quick game versus pressure. He can set up with one step and fire into a tight passing lane due to 
pressure. 
The upper limit of his accuracy is 47 yards (catchable, not pinpoint) to the middle of the field. He lacks a strong arm, and it may prove too 
questionable for him to become a starter if he cleans up his technical skills and refines the rest of his game. 
There's talk Payton could be the third quarterback off the board. This is probably false. 
It would be an impulsive decision based on Payton's one year as a starter at a lower level of football and the clear deficiencies he must 
address with his game. This would be an egregious upside pick based on athletic ability, and it would be better if Payton were a third-day 
pick and afforded time to work behind the scenes as a developmental project. 
Drops: He has a three-step drop with slow but long steps. He finishes with his feet at shoulder length to begin his setup. 
Payton has an effective seven-step roll to the left, getting the ball out on that seventh step to the flat. 
Ball Security: When scrambling, Payton carries the ball low and wide from his frame. When hitching or climbing to break the pocket, 
Payton holds the ball with both arms, but wide from his frame. There's also exaggerated movement with is arms as he hitches and climbs. 
When he breaks the pocket and commits to running, he carries the ball high to his chest with his boundary-side arm and his elbow is tight 
to his side. He can take a hit to the ball or his ball-carrying arm. 
Play Fakes: Payton extends the ball fully as he works through his play fake toward the I-back from center. He turns his back to the defense 
as he retracts the ball, hiding it as he completes his turn on a sprint pass the opposite side as the fake to the back. 
From Pistol, Payton will slow his movement toward the RB to simulate an exchange before he retracts the ball and begins his roll or drop. 
Pump Fakes: Payton has full range of motion with his pump fake, but the violence is minimal. 
Release: Payton holds the ball closer to his stomach than his chest. When he begins his draw, the ball dips even lower - toward his hip -- 
and winds behind his back shoulder. The ball reaches the apex of his draw about a ball's length behind his back shoulder. 
Payton takes a step with his front foot just before he begins his draw. As he brings the ball forward, Payton transfers his weight from his 
back foot to his front foot. The midline of his back foot and the toes of his front foot are aligned with the target as he begins to transfer his 
weight and bring the ball forward. 
86
players.pre_draft_advice: Monitor Payton from afar.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Payton is green and will require patience, but he’s a dynamic dual-threat quarterback. His physical tools and competitive makeup are promising qualities for a developmental prospect.

players.name: Kevin Coleman
players.lateround_profile: Given their depth chart, the Dolphins needed to add at wide receiver in this draft. They took two on Day 2 and then grabbed Kevin Coleman, a wideout who played for four different schools throughout his collegiate career, in Round 5. Coleman is a true slot receiver. Over 91% of his career routes came from the slot - the highest rate in this class. And it wasn't scheme-driven. Across four different programs, he posted slot rates of at least 87% at every stop, per PFF. His best season came at Mississippi State. As a junior, he posted a 2.53 receiving yards per team pass attempt mark, which jumped to 2.86 after adjusting for strength of schedule. That led to a 48.2 Breakout Score, seventh-best in the class. The heavy slot usage is the concern. Since 2020, receivers entering the league with a slot rate of at least 90% include KJ Hamler, KJ Hill, Dazz Newsome, Jaelon Darden, Kyle Philips, Derius Davis, Tre Tucker, Malachi Corley, and Jimmy Horn. Like Coleman, that group generally lacked strong draft capital - and it shows in the results. Coleman doesn't overlap much with Miami's earlier picks. Caleb Douglas and Chris Bell project more as perimeter options, while Coleman likely fills a dedicated slot role. That probably gives him the safest projection of the trio, even if Bell carries the higher ceiling. It comes down to preference. In the later rounds of rookie drafts, are you chasing safety or upside? In deeper formats, Coleman makes more sense as a dart. In standard dynasty leagues, he's not a priority target. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 59
players.elevator_pitch:
Coleman reminds me of two Michigan State receivers who were once teammates - Jayden Reed and 
Jalen Nailor. Both receivers have the skills to work as flankers and slots. Reed could even become a primary option at 
flanker like we saw from Stefon Diggs during the peak of his career. Nailor may get his first opportunity to become a 
significant offensive contributor with the Raiders. 
Both receivers are skilled options after the catch and run good enough routes to win against zone coverage and in specific 
depths against man-to-man. Reed has more proven skills as a situational vertical threat. 
128
players.pre_draft_advice:
At least before the NFL Draft generates its referendum on Coleman, I'd consider him in 
the third round of rookie drafts.

players.name: Tanner Koziol
players.lateround_profile: Tanner Koziol was the 12th tight end off the board in the 2026 NFL Draft and actually the second for the Jaguars, who selected blocking specialist Nate Boerkircher 56th overall. Koziol projects to be much more of a pass-catcher between the two, which is why the fantasy-focused ZAP Model likes him more than Boerkircher despite a massive difference in their draft capital. Koziol actually led two different offenses in receptions in his final two years of college, Ball State and Houston, respectively. He also pops in terms of an interesting stat: first downs per route run. Koziol might be a ball player. In total, he owns a Breakout Score and a max yards per team attempt mark at or above the 85th percentile in the model. That's good stuff. It's not just production, either. He crosses the threshold for viable athleticism. Koziol clocked a 4.70-second 40-yard dash at the NFL Combine at 6'7" and 247 pounds, good for a 105 height-adjusted Speed Score. He also graded out with a really good weight-adjusted burst score (vertical and broad jump). Although those aren't direct inputs in the model, it's nice to see there's more than just straight-line speed, especially given the lighter frame. It's also looking like there just isn't much competition for Koziol after this season in Jacksonville, either. Brenton Strange, Quintin Morris, and Hunter Long have contracts that run out after 2026. It's still late draft capital and not a ton of investment in Koziol. With that said, the names that do hit when drafted this late have possessed elite results in max yards per team attempt, and Koziol checks that box. LLaattee--RRoouunndd FFaannttaassyy FFoooottbbaallll:: 22002266 PPrroossppeecctt GGuuiiddee JJuummpp TToo:: ZZAAPP MMooddeell || YYeeaarr 22 MMooddeell 111155
players.elevator_pitch:
Koziol is an excellent pass-catcher. He had the highest rate of difficult catches I tracked among the tight ends I 
studied this year. 
At the same time, Koziol's route running doesn't generate as much separation as he needs to avoid difficult contested targets. 
There's no nuance with his releases and route setups heading into breaks. 
There's a lack of suddenness as a mover. This includes his releases, his route breaks, and as a ball carrier. 
84
players.pre_draft_advice:
Koziol is worth consideration in 1.5 PPR scoring formats for tight ends in large-roster leagues with 
practice squad spots during the summer when there are expanded rosters. Otherwise monitor from afar for 2-3 years.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Koziol will be ranked higher by teams who put a premium on ball skills or are looking for a “big slot” weapon. He’ll be lower for teams looking for more of a true Y or well-rounded tight end.

players.name: Seth McGowan
players.lateround_profile: KENTUCKY WAIVER WIRE ADD Low Risk Neutral High Risk Prior to this draft class, only one ZAP Model running back - Martez Carter - entered the NFL at age 25 or older. This year, we have two. One of them is Seth McGowan. Does age matter? Yes. The model accounts for it, and historically, older prospects have produced weaker outcomes. But context is needed. College football has shifted dramatically in recent years. Transfer rules changed. The pandemic extended eligibility. NIL deals altered incentives. As a result, we're seeing older backs more frequently. Over the last two draft classes (excluding this one), 10 running backs either attended the NFL Combine or were drafted at age 24 or older. From 2011 through 2023, there were 11 total. Last year alone, RJ Harvey, Woody Marks, and Jacory Croskey-Merritt were all 24 or older at selection. Adjusted for draft capital, the hit rates for these older prospects haven't been disastrous of late. As long as the production profiles were strong. McGowan's profile isn't bad - it's just uneven. He began at Oklahoma in 2020 before being dismissed following legal issues, though the charges were ultimately dropped. After time away from the game, he resurfaced at Butler Community College in 2023, then transferred to New Mexico State in 2024. There, he logged 152 carries in a backfield that also featured 2026 draft hopeful Mike Washington. More importantly for his analytical outlook, McGowan posted a career-best 16.3% reception share that year. That season did a lot of the heavy lifting for his pass-catching profile, showing at least some ability to function on passing downs. The question is sustainability. When a receiving spike comes after multiple stops and years in the wilderness, it's fair to wonder how sticky it really is. In 2025, McGowan transferred again - this time to Kentucky. There, he carried the ball 165 times, recorded a 9.1% reception share, and posted a career-best 1.22 adjusted total yards per team play. McGowan is a big, physical back who plays through contact. Among this year's Combine invitees, he ranks top-five in career missed tackles forced per attempt, per PFF. His explosive run rate is average at best, but when you pair the tackle-breaking ability with an acceptable receiving profile, you can at least envision a path where he sticks as a quality backup with contingent upside.
players.etr_notes: Older player, but big and athletic.
players.elevator_pitch:
I'm betting you'll see McGowan with a higher ranking elsewhere simply based on his size and timed 
speed. Fans of McGowan will be rooting for a redemption arc with McGowan. He arrived at Oklahoma as a 4-star recruit, and 
eventually served time for beating up an individual after a pot deal went awry. 
McGowan is a well-built back with straight-line speed who needs a big runway to reach that speed. If he can prove that his 
acceleration is better than it appears, McGowan could contribute to an offense that runs gap plays. 
McGowan has lapses with seeking cutbacks in situations where leverage his size with a downhill track is the better choice. 
McGowan's cut-back ability is efficient, but the issue is his choice to do so. 
McGowan's agility is good enough to contribute in a gap scheme, but he'll struggle in a high-end contributing role if asked to 
run a zone scheme, because his footwork lacks the precision and balance for the baseline creativity needed there. 
McGowan's power and contact balance is not as good as his size might lead people to believe. He can win against some 
linebackers, but most of his success against collisions comes against defensive backs. McGowan will drag box defenders and 
generate a push, but he lacks top-end power. 
McGowan could become a versatile pass protector and check-down receiver if he can clean up a myriad of fine details with 
positions, angles, and hand usage. 
McGowan could be a nice fit as a backup to a power running game who could eventually deliver some situational production 
or earn a rotational role as a first-call substitute for the starter. 
Vision: McGowan follows his pullers to the edge of the box and makes a timely transition downhill as his lead blockers 
engage. He'll stick tight to his down blocks as he works downhill. 
McGowan adjusts his stride length, identifies penetration up the middle, and presses creases within 1-2 steps of the 
penetration to set up a cutback or bounce. 
McGowan is effective at making the first defender miss in the backfield - whether it's edge or interior penetration. He's 
creative enough with his feet to find solutions to unplanned obstacles. 
There are interior runs where he reads the leverage correctly in theory, but would get more yardage sticking with the design 
against the suboptimal leverage than attempting the cutback. Matching up one-on-one with a DB that's partially blocked is a 
better choice than cutting back into an unblocked defensive tackle. 
Elusiveness: McGown can transition downhill to the short flat on a perimeter run with two-quick steps. When working to 
the edge of the box, he only needs one transition step to get downhill. When working to the far-side flat on a perimeter run, 
he needs three steps. 
McGowan has an effective head fake when heading downhill and setting up a safety. He delivers a sharp stick to set up 
pressure cuts. He uses a double up on approach to the line of scrimmage to set up a defensive tackle's shot into the 
backfield before bouncing outside. 
He delivers a two-quick juke with a head fake against the high safety to set up a lane outside the defender. 
166
players.pre_draft_advice:
A late-round consideration in leagues with over 35 rosters spots and a practice squad, or 
for expanded offseason rosters until GMs can learn McGowan's roster status by late summer-early fall.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, McGowan is a good-sized, coordinated athlete with adequate vision and run strength, although his lack of explosive traits and inconsistent third-down value will make it an uphill climb to prove his worth on an NFL roster.

players.name: Jack Endries
players.lateround_profile: Jack Endries has a strong tie to the 2025 Heisman Trophy winner, Fernando Mendoza. They played two seasons together in 2023 and 2024 at Cal before transferring to different schools. Endries, as a redshirt sophomore, led the Golden Bears in receptions (56) and yards (623). But their draft capital couldn't have been much different. Mendoza, of course, went first overall to the Raiders. Endries was selected 221st overall, in the seventh round, by the Bengals. He does possess solid age-adjusted production and an athletic baseline, however. Endries, in total, rates out with strong max yards per team attempt data (67th percentile) and good Breakout Score (73rd percentile). He also ran a 4.62-second 40-yard dash at the NFL Combine, giving him a height-adjusted Speed Score of 109 at 6'5", 245. On the flip side, despite a smaller frame and good athletic testing, Endries doesn't pop from a yards-after-the-catch standpoint. That's also tied to some average-to-below-average blocking scores from PFF and SportsInfoSolutions (and it's worth noting that he graded out with around 15th-percentile arm length). Endries had a pretty wide range of pre-draft valuations, spanning from the middle of the second round to one of the first names being called on Day 3. He wound up being one of the last names called on Day 3. The Bengals, though, have just two tight ends signed through 2027 (the 31-year-old Mike Gesicki and the 26-year-old Erick All). Endries had everything but draft capital in his profile, and at a certain point in the draft, athleticism isn't usually what wins out. It's a history of producing. That's at least some hope. The odds he hits big are incredibly low, but if he begins to find footing in a high-octane offense like Cincinnati's, well, maybe he plays more like a Day 2 pick that some thought he could be before the draft. LLaattee--RRoouunndd FFaannttaassyy FFoooottbbaallll:: 22002266 PPrroossppeecctt GGuuiiddee JJuummpp TToo:: ZZAAPP MMooddeell || YYeeaarr 22 MMooddeell 112211
players.elevator_pitch:
Endries has a lot of Zach Ertz to his game. He works the middle and the flats with enough sophistication to develop 
into a reliable starter and check-down option who can also deliver big-play receptions off-play action. 
Endries knows how to sell routes with the techniques he has learned, and he'll likely build on it as his career unfolds. He has good 
weight drop into breaks of routes that use return motion - think whip routes and certain variations of routes that work against zone 
defenders playing tight at the top of the stem. 
62
players.pre_draft_advice:
Endries is a high-floor/low-ceiling receiver for fantasy because he's fast enough to stretch the field, 
but not explosive enough to become a high-end statistical mismatch for opposing defenses. If the NFL's offensive trends make a 
large shift to 12 personnel sets, Endries' blocking might need 1-2 years of work before he can see the field as a third or fourth option 
in the passing game's pecking order. 
Endries is another late-round consideration worth stashing in 1.5 PPR formats who might be earning too much buzz as a mid-round 
value. His future will likely be that of a competent receiver but one without compelling target volume.

players.name: Jaren Kanak
players.lateround_profile: Jaren Kanak has a really noteworthy profile. It wasn't leading to much in the way of projected draft capital, but he did get selected. The Titans snagged Kanak in the seventh round, 225th overall. Kanak, a high school track athlete and dual-threat quarterback, was a four-star recruit as an athlete with interest from a lot of big programs. He ran a 4.52-second 40-yard dash at the NFL Combine, equating to a height-adjusted Speed Score of 109.2. While that's not bad by any means, virtually every tight end who ran this year graded out well by that metric. Still, the athleticism alone is noteworthy. However, he measured in at the NFL Combine at 6'2", 234 pounds, making him both the shortest and lightest of all 27 invited tight ends. Noteworthy, though. But get this: Kanak didn't have a catch at Oklahoma until his final season...because he was a linebacker before converting to tight end ahead of the 2025 season. Noteworthy. So, if he got a Combine invite, he must have been relevant this year, right? Yep. He went out and caught 44 passes for 533 yards, both third-best on the Sooners, while playing alongside Combine-invited wide receiver Deion Burks. He wound up being significantly productive when actually playing offense, so his age- and schedule-adjusted max yards per team attempt and Breakout Score are actually above average because he's still relatively young (just 22.2 years old at the time of the draft). Transparently, Kanak has an uphill battle to fantasy football production given his size and the seventh-round status, but we've seen plenty of fantasy-relevant tight ends enter the league with minimal reps at the position - guys who came over from basketball, for example. It's the type of profile that, with more buy-in, we could get more excited about. And, look, tight end isn't a strength for the Titans. Gunnar Helm, a fourth-rounder last year, is set to be the TE1 for them after a promising rookie campaign, and Daniel Bellinger is inked through 2028 after signing with the team this offseason. Kanak could get some schemed touches. He's just unlikely to morph into a high-snap, high-touch player at his size. LLaattee--RRoouunndd FFaannttaassyy FFoooottbbaallll:: 22002266 PPrroossppeecctt GGuuiiddee JJuummpp TToo:: ZZAAPP MMooddeell || YYeeaarr 22 MMooddeell 112222
players.elevator_pitch:
A former linebacker who transitioned to tight end in 2025, Kanak is undersized for a full-time starting tight end in 
this league unless he can leverage his speed with an elite receiving game. At this point, Kanak's separation, routes, and pass 
catching are good enough to contribute. None of them are at a level that will help him compensate for his size and emerge as a 
quality starter. 
94
players.pre_draft_advice: Monitor from afar.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Kanak doesn’t have a large body of work, but he put a promising snapshot on film and offers some upside in the right H-back-style role. His ability to make an impact on special teams will help him stay on an NFL roster.

players.name: Sam Roush
players.lateround_profile: Despite being the 69th overall pick in the 2026 NFL Draft, Sam Roush was the sixth tight end off the board in what became a deep tight end draft class much quicker than we expected. The fit with the Bears, though, isn't exactly ideal for Roush's immediate fantasy football impact. Of course, Colston Loveland is looking like a stud after his rookie season, and Cole Kmet is under contract for two more years. Roush is huge, though, at 6'6" and 267 pounds, and the 4.70-second 40-yard dash at that size is good for a height-adjusted Speed Score north of 112. He also tested well outside of straight-line speed. His 3-cone time was 7.08 seconds. I have only 44 players in my Combine database (since 2000) with a weight of 267 and a 3-cone of 7.08 or faster, and most are defensive ends. Again, going back to 2000, Roush is fifth among all tight ends in weight-adjusted vertical, coincidentally ranking third in this class alone. The leaders in this metric are Eli Stowers, Vernon Davis, Virgil Green, and Kenyon Sadiq. He's also second since 2000 among tight ends in weight-adjusted broad jump. He's big and athletic. What about the production? It wasn't bad by any means. His max yards per team attempt and Breakout Scores were just above the 60th percentile within the database, and the blocking grades were pretty good overall. With draft capital in the 60s, Roush actually has a lot of overlap with Travis Kelce's profile (61st-percentile max yards per team attempt for Roush and 62nd-percentile for Kelce - with a better Breakout Score for Roush). It's always bold to compare any tight ends to the ones who went on to be true outliers, but there are many similarities between Roush and some difference-makers. There are also many similarities between Roush and some unproductive, big-bodied blockers. It just may be difficult for him to find early-career production with how that current tight end room is situated. LLaattee--RRoouunndd FFaannttaassyy FFoooottbbaallll:: 22002266 PPrroossppeecctt GGuuiiddee JJuummpp TToo:: ZZAAPP MMooddeell || YYeeaarr 22 MMooddeell 110088
players.elevator_pitch:
Roush runs effective routes, but it's in a limited range of the field. It means he's not a matchup threat in the 
receiving game. Roush will do his best work in zone and not as a primary option. 
Roush will be valued most for his blocking. When I arrived at this conclusion after watching Roush's tape I also thought he'd be the 
best overall tight end in this class if the RSP's Depth of Talent Score valued blocking on equal or greater terms as receiving skills. 
46
players.pre_draft_advice:
If Roush joins a team where the scheme uses multiple tight ends at a time, increase his value 
accordingly. If Roush joins a team where the scheme is predominantly three receivers, he's a mid-round value who will be a patience 
play for fantasy GMs.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Roush isn’t without his warts, but he offers a traditional Y tight end skill set — competitiveness to handle dirty work as a blocker and athleticism to be a functional underneath target. He has the makeup and toughness to quietly become an integral part of an NFL offense.

players.name: Will Kacmarek
players.lateround_profile: Will Kacmarek was projected to go undrafted when the prospect guide was first published. The Dolphins made him a third-round pick, 87th overall. This tight end class is something else, man. The class is highlighted primarily by two smaller, pass-catching tight ends in Kenyon Sadiq and Eli Stowers. Kacmarek is certainly not that. He weighed in at 261 pounds at the NFL Combine, making him the second-heaviest tight end in the class (of the 27 players invited). Kacmarek also ran a 4.74-second 40-yard dash at a nice size for a 106.1 height-adjusted Speed Score. So, there's size and athleticism to go along with draft capital. Was there production? Not really, no. Kacmarek played three seasons at Ohio before transferring to Ohio State for two more years. Even at Ohio, he didn't average more than 19.0 yards per game in either season, and he caught just 23 passes in 26 games at Ohio State. In college, Kacmarek was primarily a blocking tight end with some good per-route receiving data (the Dolphins' Instagram post welcoming Kacmarek to the team actually starts by calling him "a run-blocking specialist"). As far as Miami's tight end room is concerned, there's not much in the sense of consistent receiving production, but Kacmarek may not be the answer there. Top-90 draft capital on a big-bodied, plus athlete isn't totally irrelevant. But the high- risk flag here suggests that Kacmarek was a reach in the NFL Draft when viewed from a fantasy football lens. LLaattee--RRoouunndd FFaannttaassyy FFoooottbbaallll:: 22002266 PPrroossppeecctt GGuuiiddee JJuummpp TToo:: ZZAAPP MMooddeell || YYeeaarr 22 MMooddeell 111122
players.elevator_pitch:
If you watched him at Ohio State, Kacmarek served as the big-bodied sidekick to Max Klare. Of course, if you 
watched Klare and Kacmarek together, you see just how underwhelming Ohio State's system makes tight ends look in the receiving 
game. It wasn't just Kacmarek's fit in the Buckeyes' system. 
Both Klare and Kacmarek looked more dynamic as receiving weapons at their former programs. This is especially true with 
Kacmarek. 
74
players.pre_draft_advice:
Kacmarek is a high-floor, low-ceiling fantasy prospect at the position because he'll likely be tied to 
the line of scrimmage and not utilized frequently enough in the intermediate passing game. 
If the NFL's offensive trends continue to shift toward 12-personnel, Kacmarek will be a safer option than some of the move tight 
ends who can't block and don't offer more compelling receiving skills than their team's WR3-WR4. Think of A.J. Barner in this 
respect. 
This translates to valuing Kacmarek as a late-round or offseason stash for dynasty teams with expanded rosters during the summer. 
There are players ranked below him with greater ceilings as a fantasy options, but lower floors due to their limited roles in the run 
game.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Kacmarek is a big, linear athlete with strong hands and the desired level of aggression as a point-of-attack blocker. He projects as a Y tight end and can be one of the better blockers in the draft class.

players.name: Malik Benson
players.lateround_profile: OREGON WAIVER WIRE ADD Low Risk Neutral High Risk Malik Benson can absolutely run. The former track athlete clocked a 4.37 40 at the Combine, and when you watch him, the vertical juice is obvious. He's built to threaten defenses over the top. The path to this point, though, has been anything but linear. Benson spent two seasons in JUCO before landing at Alabama in 2023, where he caught just 13 passes across 14 games. That led to a transfer to Florida State, where he managed only 25 receptions. He then finished his career at Oregon in 2025, his fourth college stop. That final season was technically his most productive, but even then, his adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt peaked at just 1.88. That's a bottom-10 mark in this class. Deployment helps explain some of it. Over his final three non-JUCO seasons, Benson posted a 25.6% deep-ball target rate, 11th-highest in the class. He was used as a field- stretcher. The problem is that he was rarely featured. He never reached even a 15% prorated target share in any season, the third-worst rate among Combine invite wide receivers. So what we're looking at is a 23-and-a-half-year-old prospect with plus speed, minimal target-earning ability, and no season that truly stands out from a production standpoint. The athleticism gives him a puncher's chance. But analytically, this is a thin profile. It's tough to get overly optimistic without meaningful draft capital to force opportunity.
players.elevator_pitch:
Benson is a speedster capable of stretching a defense in the college game, but he'll need to refine his 
release tools to bait NFL cornerbacks enough to win up the boundary like he does at Oregon. At this point, he has one 
reliable move that he performs with the refinement capable of doing the job against tight man coverage. 
Fortunately, there is more to Benson's game than winning go routes and fades. He has some skill with setups midway 
through his stems to work free over the middle, but he must cut down on lapses that tip off his intentions to the safety 
playing over the top. 
155
players.pre_draft_advice:
Benson is worth consideration as a late-round pick you intend on stashing for at least a 
year on a practice squad or at the end of a loaded roster where one spot taken with a seldom-used player is possible. 
Otherwise, monitor Benson from afar unless returns are part of your league's scoring system and Benson is slated for this 
duty.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Benson is undeveloped in the quick game and intermediate area, but his gliding speed and tracking skills will be valued by teams looking for a pure vertical receiver on the perimeter. His usefulness will also show on punt returns.

players.name: Marlin Klein
players.lateround_profile: This is an unfathomable post-draft ZAP score swing for Marlin Klein, who was 164th on the consensus big board when the prospect guide was first published - fantasy afterthought territory as a projected late fifth-round pick. The Texans drafted him 59th overall. That's a signal they're willing to buy in on Klein as a developmental prospect, and it's also basically earlier capital for a profile like his than we've almost ever seen (though we saw multiple reaches in the 2026 draft alone). The new tight end model reacts to different information in different ways, and Klein is a good athlete, so the model will account for production differently as a result. That matters because he has question marks there. On the athleticism front, Klein ran a 4.61-second 40-yard dash at the Combine, seventh- fastest among 18 tight ends who tested this year. His height-adjusted Speed Score clocked in at 112.7, sixth-best among official combine testers and an 89th-percentile mark in the ZAP Model database. The production was lacking, though. Klein played behind Colston Loveland for three years at Michigan before seeing his role expand in 2025, posting career-bests in receptions (24), yards (248), and touchdowns (1) - resulting in a max yards-per-team-attempt mark in just the 21st percentile. What the model is picking up on is a combination of strong athleticism with elite draft capital, which has helped uncover NFL opportunity in the past. He pairs that with strong blocking grades and film, and we've seen good blockers with great athleticism overcome weak production before. For Klein, the limited output can at least be partially explained by spending three years behind a generational prospect. Still, this is rare draft capital for a profile like Klein's. Accounting for the drastic swing, his comparison list includes Luke Schoonmaker, Drew Sample, Tucker Kraft, Jelani Woods, Dawson Knox, and Jordan Cameron - a wide array that reflects the range of outcomes here. Klein could take a few years to hit his stride - if he ever does - but with only Dalton Schultz (signed for two more years) ahead of him on the Texans' depth chart, there's a real path. He's a bet on traits, which is more than you can say for some other Day 2 tight ends from this class. LLaattee--RRoouunndd FFaannttaassyy FFoooottbbaallll:: 22002266 PPrroossppeecctt GGuuiiddee JJuummpp TToo:: ZZAAPP MMooddeell || YYeeaarr 22 MMooddeell 110099
players.elevator_pitch:
Klein should make a team as a third tight end because of his athletic ability and his baseline skills as a route runner 
and receiver. Blocking is the area where Klein has the most work to do. Fortunately, it's the area where he should make the most 
gains as a native of Germany. 
104
players.pre_draft_advice:
Monitor Klein from afar. If he earns good reviews during the spring or in early August, Klein would 
be worth adding to an expanded summer roster in 1.5 PPR formats as a potential stash for your practice squad. It's more likely that 
you'll cut Klein but keep him on your long list of players to consider for the first 2-3 seasons of their career if news warrants.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Klein needs to become more quarterback-friendly to see meaningful NFL snaps, but he is a good-sized athlete with toughness worth developing as a blocker. He projects as a potential backup who can be given both inline and slot duties.

players.name: Josh Cameron
players.lateround_profile: BAYLOR WAIVER WIRE ADD Low Risk Neutral High Risk Josh Cameron often looks like a running back playing wide receiver. He's got a thick, sturdy build, and Baylor leaned into that across his five seasons there. He wasn't used out of the backfield much, but he also wasn't asked to win deep - his career deep-ball target rate was the second-lowest in this class. His average depth of target sat a bit higher because he lived in that intermediate range. Cameron is more of a traits bet than a production one. He'll be past his 23rd birthday at draft time, and he had five seasons to post a truly strong receiving yards per team pass attempt mark. It never happened. His best adjusted season came in at 1.90, and that translated to a 28.1 Breakout Score - just 30th percentile in the model. The ZAP Model isn't overly enthusiastic. But one input does quietly like him: adjusted fantasy points per game. Among the 46 Combine invite wideouts, Cameron ranks 15th in that metric after age and schedule adjustments. That at least tells us he wasn't totally unproductive. There are some interesting attributes here - size, play strength, and a profile that suggests he can handle physical work over the middle. Cameron just never showed high-end dominance. Cameron is the type of player who could hang around as a depth receiver and surprise if things break right. But unless the draft really swings in his favor, he's not someone you need to aggressively prioritize in rookie drafts.
players.elevator_pitch:
Cameron shared in an interview that Larry Fitzgerald is the player he tries to model his game after. 
It's a great choice, even if after watching Cameron I saw different players based on physical dimensions and athletic ability. 
Cameron and Fitzgerald are roughly the same weight, but Fitgerald has two inches on Cameron. While I don't remember 
how many releases Fitzgerald had at Pitt, Cameron has the most thorough display of release types in his portfolio that I 
have seen from a prospect in many years. 
106
players.pre_draft_advice:
We should acknowledge that Cameron's floor as a potential WR3 in an offense isn't 
exciting, it's better than most of the receivers whose ceilings are WR3 value. Cameron's most realistic ceiling is team's WR2 
who could deliver top 15 fantasy production in an offense with an elite route runner opposite him and a top quarterback 
distributing the ball.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Cameron plays well through contact and has functional strength and ball-winning size, but his struggle in separating and avoiding contested windows will be a limiting factor. Though he has traits that intrigue NFL teams, development time is needed.

players.name: Nate Boerkircher
players.lateround_profile: There were going to be some surprises at tight end this year in the draft. It was just bound to happen. Nate Boerkircher being selected as the third tight end off the board qualifies as one. Not only that, but it's not like the position as a whole slid down the board. No, the Jaguars drafted him 56th overall. And, to be honest, the ZAP Model hasn't handled many profiles like his before. Boerkircher is already 24 years old and will be 25 by the time the 2026 season starts. Only two other tight ends in the ZAP Model were older than 24 and also a top-64 pick: Luke Schoonmaker and Hayden Hurst (more on them in a second). Boerkircher is now just one of six tight ends in the model with top-64 draft capital despite a Breakout Score under 30 (his is way below that at 13.2). The other five are Dwayne Allen, Hurst, Drew Sample, Schoonmaker, and Brenton Strange (coincidentally, now his Jaguars teammate). There's been sporadic fantasy relevance from those guys, but it's been short-lived and capped early in their careers. However, Boerkircher didn't even test as well athletically as most of those guys did, and that matters. The model allows prospects some leeway with poor production if it's combined with plus athleticism. Boerkircher, though, logged a 4.78-second 40-yard dash - not even at the NFL Combine officially but at his pro day - so we're looking at a 93.2 height- adjusted Speed Score for Boerkircher. Only Dwayne Allen from the aforementioned list also had a height-adjusted Speed Score below 103. Boerkircher is a very specific case in an outlier sense. Yes, he's a second-round pick, but he lacks the athleticism and age-adjusted production that we need to see to buy in. And, yes, Boerkircher will likely be earning snaps with investment this high. Additionally, the Jaguars' tight end room will be wide open starting in 2027 (Brenton Strange, Quintin Morris, and Hunter Long are all free agents next year). Being on the field is step one to fantasy football production, so he has that angle going for him. With that being said, though, his profile is not the type that has turned into consistent fantasy football production in the past. players.name: CJ Daniels
players.lateround_profile: MIAMI (FL) WAIVER WIRE ADD Low Risk Neutral High Risk My three-year-old son's favorite wide receiver in this class is CJ Daniels. And that's strictly because my son's name is CJ. The ZAP Model does not share his enthusiasm. Daniels' College Football Reference page reads like a 700-page novel. He spent four years at Liberty, where his only truly meaningful production came in Year 4. That season was legitimately impressive: a 3.14 adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt mark - the fourth-best max-season number in this class - which helped fuel a strong 47.3 Breakout Score. The issue? Every other season he played was below 1.70 in that metric. After Liberty, Daniels transferred to LSU for a year, then finished at Miami. That's six collegiate seasons. He'll be 24 when the draft rolls around. Late breakouts can work, but they usually need draft capital support. In the ZAP Model database, 32 wide receivers failed to go top-100 while posting a Breakout Score below 60 but a best-season adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt above 3.0. In other words, they had one monster efficiency year, but age suppressed the overall breakout profile. Of those 32 players, 30 never even reached 7 PPR points per game in any of their first three seasons. Then there's the age bucket itself. Prior to this class, 35 wide receivers entered the league at age 24 or older. Only one - John Brown - produced a 10-plus PPR points-per- game season within his first three years. Brown also had top-100 draft capital, something Daniels isn't likely to receive. Now, could the model be a little too mean towards older prospects given the NIL era, transfer portal freedom, and COVID eligibility ripple effects? That's fair to consider. College football has changed. But it's also fair to acknowledge that Daniels really only gave us one high-end season across six years. There's juice in that peak campaign. But the rest of the profile is just thin.
players.elevator_pitch:
Daniels is one of those players where it is hard not to like him when you watch his game on the field. 
He's a physical ball-winner who displays acrobatic skill and terrific hand-eye coordination in tight coverage. Get Daniels 
within semi-realistic area of the football, and he's better than most at making the difficult catch. 
Daniels also runs with physicality after the catch. He's not explosive or sudden, but he's powerful and determined. Get 
Daniels free in zone coverage or scheme routes that can work open with the help of misdirection, and Daniels can make big 
plays. 
221
players.pre_draft_advice:
Daniels is worth a late-round pick in leagues with large rosters with at least 45 spots, 
including a robust practice squad. Otherwise monitor Daniels from afar as a future desperation bye-week pick.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Daniels might lack explosive speed, but he creates separation with crafty instincts and finishes with dependable ball skills. He projects best inside as a “big slot” in the NFL, who can rely on his awareness and toughness to be productive.

players.name: Cyrus Allen
players.elevator_pitch:
Allen is a shifty slot receiver with big-play ability after the catch and in the vertical game against zone 
defenders. He's at his best against underneath coverage where he can serve as an extension of the ground game or as a 
receiver who works across the middle into open space as he earns the ball in the intermediate game. 
Allen has potential to become a viable boundary receiver, but there are details to address with releases, stems, setups, 
breaks, and positioning against defenders before he attacks the ball. The likelihood for Allen to develop all these skills and 
overcome the label as a late-round or UDFA space player is small. 
188

players.name: Deion Burks
players.lateround_profile: OKLAHOMA DART THROW Low Risk Neutral High Risk Projected draft capital is doing heavy lifting in Deion Burks' profile, because the production doesn't stand out. Burks spent three seasons at Purdue before transferring to Oklahoma for two more. Across that five-year career, his adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt never reached 2.0. He did manage a 34.6 Breakout Score, but that's more middling than encouraging. The deeper efficiency numbers are even tougher. Burks posted a 1.35 career yards per route run rate, third-worst in this class. Since 2020, the ZAP Model has tracked 24 wide receivers entering the league with career yards per route run below 1.50. The only one to provide any meaningful fantasy production from that group is Josh Palmer. And are we even rostering him in dynasty at this point? Burks' deployment adds another layer of uncertainty. In two of his seasons, he ran over 85% of his routes from the slot. In two others, he was below 25%. Given his smaller frame, the slot feels like the probable NFL home. That's where things get even more concerning. His slot yards per route run sits at just 1.14 - bottom-six in this class. There's not much analytical support here. If Burks sneaks into Day 2, that will be the primary argument in his favor. Otherwise, he profiles as a late-round rookie draft flier. And even then, there may be stronger bets on the board.
players.etr_notes: Speedy route-runner who fell massively in the draft.

Burks offers big plays from the slot and has potential to do work as a flanker in an NFL offense if he

Burks has emerging skills as a receiver. He has a variety of release techniques to work past tight coverage at the line of 
scrimmage. Burks executes a pair of those techniques with the contrast between patience and suddenness required of good

The safe route is to value Burks as a big-play slot receiver who will earn 500-700 yards 
and 3-5 touchdowns as his usual production during his peak. That production bye-week fantasy value, at best and not a

If the NFL team drafting Burks labels him as flanker or a flanker/slot, Burks could earn 700-900 yards and 4-6 touchdowns 
in this role. That's WR3-WR4 material and draftable for dynasty formats with at least 35-men rosters. 
If Burks becomes the flanker, he could earn 900-1,100 yards and 5-7 scores and he's in demand in any fantasy format with

players.name: Seydou Traore
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Traore is still young in football years (didn’t play 11-man football until 2020), but he offers intriguing athleticism and pass-catching talent for a developmental F tight end.

players.name: Jam Miller
players.lateround_profile: ALABAMA DART THROW Low Risk Neutral High Risk Jam Miller flashed some juice at the NFL Combine. At 209 pounds, he ran a 4.42 40, giving him a Speed Score just shy of 110. That might be the best part of the profile, if we're being honest. Miller spent four seasons at Alabama and never reached 150 carries in a year. His best rush attempt share season topped out at 29.7%. Through the air, he never hit an 8% reception share in any campaign. That combination - modest workload and minimal receiving - is historically tough to overcome. There are 34 running backs in the ZAP Model database who failed to clear a 30% best-season attempt share and an 8% reception share. Just four of them reached 10 or more PPR points per game in one of their first three seasons: CJ Anderson, Chris Carson, Dameon Pierce, and Damien Harris. All four weighed at least 215 pounds entering the league. Miller doesn't. Even if we loosen the thresholds - backs under 215 pounds with a best-season attempt share below 40% and a best-season reception share below 10% - there are 43 players in that bucket. Only Devonta Freeman, Elijah Mitchell, and Darrell Henderson produced meaningful fantasy seasons. And that's before diving into the advanced rushing metrics. Despite the strong 40 time, Miller ranked dead last in this year's class in collegiate explosive run rate, per PFF. It's one of the lowest marks we've seen in the last five draft cycles. His yards after contact per attempt also ranked dead last. Alabama's depth chart is never easy to crack, and they obviously play some tough defenses. But the overall résumé doesn't suggest we should be pounding the table for Miller this offseason. The Speed Score is intriguing. The production profile is not.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Miller has the build and athleticism to be a reliable one-cut runner, but he must improve as a receiver and blocker to see quality NFL playing time. Scouts are bullish about his potential on special teams, which will help separate him on a roster.

players.name: Garrett Nussmeier
players.etr_notes: Once thought of as a possible first overall pick, then had a disaster 25 season. Injury related?
players.elevator_pitch:
Nussmeier is the volatile boom-bust talent in this quarterback class. He's mobile and has the arm talent to deliver targets 
with velocity that belies his height and weight. 
Nussmeier has an aggressive, play-making mindset. This gets him into trouble, but he plays through the mistakes and doesn't hesitate to 
attack at the next available opportunity. 
The son of former NFL, AFL, and CFL quarterback Doug Nussmeier, who is a veteran offensive coach in the NFL, Garrett has some 
inconsistencies with drop plans that impact his timing and accuracy. There are claims that Brian Kelly is to blame for this and other woes 
from 2025. 
Kelly has a reputation for pressuring players to play when they are injured and that he makes Jon Voight's character from Friday Night 
Lights look like Pete Carroll. There's talk that Kelly cajoled Nussmeier into play with injuries that impacted his play and scape-goated the 
quarterback. 
If true, this explains some of the erratic play from Nussmeier. Even so, there are constants that span multiple seasons and much of what I 
studied was from 2024. 
Nussmeier can underestimate the periphery defender on targets outside the numbers and get too aggressive. He must at least manipulate 
these defenders. This isn't a bad habit everywhere. Nussmeier has moments that are very good in the middle of the field, accounting for 
that defender with placement that protects the target and the receiver. 
The place where Nussmeier has the most lapses is with downfield throws of 20 yards and greater, and the target is outside the numbers, or 
the defender is playing 10 yards under the target in the passing lane. 
Nussmeier capable of varying and combining stride lengths, pacing, and maneuvers to create space in traffic so he can throw an accurate 
target. The problem is that he creates more problems with his release and placement that he's forced to overcome. This is due to the lack of 
uniformity of his drops and setups from play to play. 
Talented, but erratic, Nussmeier has good accuracy and pocket skills for a player whose feet aren't giving him the reliable foundation for 
him to process and execute cleanly at the highest level. If he can clean up the leaks that spring from his game, he could become a starter. 
Drops: Nussmeier has a three-step drop, but he has a short stutter as the first two steps that isn't fluid and could impact the consistency 
with his timing because the three-step pattern is different each rep and doesn't need to be. One is as described above, the next is a short 
step preceding two large hitches. After that, he uses crossover steps for a three-step drop that appear more uniform. 
This could be an indication that Nussmeier is in an offense where his drop plan isn't linked tight enough to the routes. At least this was the 
case in 2024. 
His one- and two-step drops on short passes are uniform. His set up out of a read fake can be wider than optimal. 
Nussmeier has a five-step drop that's uniform and he adjusts the path to roll right at the top of the drop when interior pressure breaks 
containment. 
He delivers a well-paced and defined seven-step drop from center with a play-action fake mid-drop. 
Nussmeier displays all the drops but throughout 2024, his footwork wasn't uniform from one drop to the next of the same type. The step 
width often had some irregularity that hindered a smooth and uniform process. This impacted the width and uniformity of his setups. 
His feet are often too wide when he sets up and this leads to throws that should be pinpoint, but are either catchable with an awkward 
adjustment or inaccurate. 
Nussmeier has good accuracy and pocket skills for a player whose feet aren't giving him the reliable foundation for him to process and 
execute cleanly at the highest level. 
Ball Security: Nussmeier keeps two hands on the ball in the pocket and when he moves within controlled footwork in the pocket. When he 
breaks the pocket, he initially carries the ball loose and wide from his frame. When he passes the line of scrimmage, he realizes he should 
be protecting the ball, tucking it close to his chest but still loose from his elbow. He only uses his right arm. 
Nussmeier has lost the ball while maneuvering away from pressure in the pocket. The defender strikes the ball thanks to Nussmeier's loose 
security. This has happened in multiple games and in the black zone multiple times. 
Nussmeier flips the ball unnecessarily while holding it in the pocket and surveying the field. 
57
players.pre_draft_advice:
A mid-round value in rookie drafts whose value might increase if an NFL team falls in love with the pre-
injury version of Nussmeier from 2024 and takes the risk earlier. If you need a quarterback, Nussmeier is more likely a patience play, so 
don't get too aggressive with him as your target.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Nussmeier is an aggressive competitor who can throw you into or out of games, but his arm talent and command are foundational traits that generally translate well to the NFL. He projects as a high-level backup and spot starter.

players.name: Colbie Young
players.lateround_profile: Only six wide receivers in the ZAP Model database have averaged at least 14 PPR points per game in one of their first three seasons after being drafted outside the top 100: Allen Hurns, Martavis Bryant, Tyreek Hill, Hunter Renfrow, Amon-Ra St. Brown, and Puka Nacua. That's rare air. And there's no consistent size archetype among them. So while Colbie Young checking in at 6'5", 218 pounds may have appealed to Cincinnati, it doesn't really move the needle for fantasy. Young ends up as a Day 3 pick out of Georgia, where he spent two seasons after stops at Miami and Lackawanna College. Across five years, the production never truly materialized. Even at Lackawanna, he posted just a 1.68 prorated receiving yards per team pass attempt mark as a freshman - a number that fades even further once adjusted for competition. His best adjusted season came last year, also at 1.68, when he was nearly 23 years old. Because of the late production and modest efficiency, he never registered a Breakout Score. There are a few intriguing traits. Young posted elevated deep-ball and behind-the-line target rates, suggesting teams were trying to manufacture touches. He also forced missed tackles at an above-average clip, hinting at some ability after the catch. But zooming out, this is a long-shot profile. Day 3 receivers without meaningful production or a Breakout Score rarely become fantasy difference-makers. And with Ja'Marr Chase and Tee Higgins ahead of him, there's almost no path to short-term volume. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 61

Young is a well-built boundary receiver who moves with power. He's a long-striding speedster with 
build-up speed. He wins contested targets and stretch the field with on fades, double moves, and play-action vertical 
targets. 
Suddenness is not his game, but he is learning how to set up breaks with techniques at the top of his stem that bait 
defenders out of position. 
261

Monitor Young from afar. If Young earns NFL Draft capital this spring, he could be worth

players.brugler_summary: Overall, Young is a big-framed athlete with fluid, long strides and the strong hands needed to consistently come down with the rock. He is an attractive option for an NFL team seeking a player who’s stylistically similar to Courtland Sutton.

players.name: Reggie Virgil
players.lateround_profile: Reggie Virgil spent three seasons at Miami (OH) before transferring to Texas Tech in 2025. The early production was minimal - just two receptions across his first two years. As a junior, though, he flashed with a 2.20 receiving yards per team pass attempt mark, leading to a respectable 40.9 Breakout Score. The issue is that the production didn't meaningfully improve after the move to the Big 12. Virgil has a lean frame and was used primarily as a vertical threat. Among the 46 Combine invitees, his career deep target rate and average depth of target both ranked third. That role helps explain why his first downs per route run was so low - he operated in a true boom-or-bust capacity. From a fantasy perspective, that's concerning. Since 2020, 12 wide receivers in the ZAP Model have posted a career deep target rate above 30% alongside a first downs per route run rate below 8%. None have come close to becoming fantasy relevant. Could Virgil buck that trend and carve out a field-stretching role? Sure. NFL teams always need players who can stretch defenses. But historically, that archetype hasn't translated into consistent fantasy production. The landing spot doesn't help much in the short term. Marvin Harrison Jr. is locked in, Trey McBride isn't going anywhere, and Michael Wilson could still be around. Even if Wilson leaves as a free agent next year, Virgil isn't a direct replacement stylistically. On top of that, Arizona's quarterback situation remains unsettled. The ZAP Model doesn't mind Virgil at his draft cost, but this looks more like a depth piece than a priority target. He's someone to monitor, not someone you need to chase in rookie drafts.
