players.elevator_pitch:
Virgil is possession-plus receiver who can win downfield thanks to his length, ability to earn position 
on defenders at the boundary and win the ball, and capable hands. Virgil is a limited route runner who must develop a 
better man-to-man and timing-route game to emerge as more than a situational player or reserve. 
Virgil's routes are better in the shallow range of the field than the intermediate range. It's this area of the field that is the 
difference between having every-down value and being limited to situational football.

players.pre_draft_advice:
Monitor Virgil from afar. If Virgil earns NFL Draft capital this spring, he could be worth 
consideration for expanded offseason rosters as a free agent to monitor on your roster during camp.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Virgil is a tall, silky-smooth athlete with the long-striding speed and adequate ball skills that give him a fighting chance as an outside pro receiver. His versatility on special teams coverages will increase his value.

players.name: Michael Trigg
players.lateround_profile: BAYLOR FLEX PLAY Low Risk Neutral High Risk Michael Trigg's profile is that of a well-rounded athlete. He played defensive end and basketball in high school and earned four-star recruiting status before heading to Southern California, the first of three NCAA schools for which he suited up. Trigg played only six games as a Trojan in 2021 and then seven and three games at Ole Miss in 2022 and 2023, respectively. Across those 16 games, he totaled just 28 catches for 330 yards and 5 touchdowns. But then at Baylor in 2024, Trigg notched 30 catches for 395 yards and 3 touchdowns and built on it in 2025: 50 grabs for 694 yards and 6 touchdowns, ranking top-three on the team in each of those categories in his final NCAA season. Even with that slow start, his best season by age- and schedule-adjusted max yards per team attempt is about average among the tight end database, as was his Breakout Score. We do have to note, though, that Trigg missed time not only for injuries but also for off-field incidents, and while it's possible that the production profile could've been markedly better, film and scouting grades put his blocking into question. It could all be interconnected. I'm focused on the data, but this is part of the equation with Trigg, whose draft stock has been declining in the months leading up to the NFL Draft. For us as fantasy football managers, we care a lot about the receiving production, but we also know that poor blocking and commitment can mean reduced snaps. It can also translate into a new-age tight end who is tasked more with receiving than blocking. Trigg's yards per route run data was good at all his stops, including a 2.18 mark in his last season, and he led this year's Combine-invited class in slot rate, per PFF. That's what makes him appealing as a fantasy football prospect. He could be a good-to-great NFL receiver. Trigg's long arms and big hands, as measured at the Combine, help make for a highlight- level catch radius, which he certainly flashed on tape. The big board rank has been falling, but if an NFL team sees it differently and applies some capital in the late- Round-2 or middle-of-Round 3 range where he peaked, he draws some Tucker Kraft and Brenton Strange comps. Trigg could be legit if an NFL team uses him to his best abilities. players.name: Jeff Caldwell
players.lateround_profile: CINCINNATI DART THROW Low Risk Neutral High Risk Jeff Caldwell might be the biggest size-speed outlier in this entire class. He's 6'5", 216 pounds, and ran a 4.31 at the Combine. On top of that, he posted the second-best broad jump and vertical among wide receiver invitees. That's absurd movement at that frame. Those testing numbers probably shifted him from fringe-undrafted territory into "someone will take a shot" range. But Caldwell's outlook is almost entirely traits-based. Caldwell began his career at Lindenwood in 2022, and he stayed there for three seasons. On the surface, a 3.24 receiving yards per team pass attempt mark in 2024 looks impressive. Once adjusted for strength of competition, though, it falls below 2.0. We have to always remember that raw dominance at a lower level doesn't carry the same signal. Caldwell transferred to Cincinnati for his final season and finished with 32 catches for 478 yards and 6 touchdowns. His 1.31 receiving yards per team pass attempt there didn't move the needle analytically. The result is a 29th percentile Breakout Score and a best-season adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt of 1.94. That's not the kind of production profile that typically overcomes mid-to-late-round draft capital. However, there's no denying the athletic profile. Caldwell's one of the best athletes we've seen come out of the Combine. It's rare. But historically, betting on testing without meaningful, translatable production is tough. Unless draft capital comes in far stronger than expected, Caldwell is more of a last-round rookie draft dart throw than someone you need to prioritize.
players.etr_notes: Absolute freak who went undrafted, but then lands with KC as a UDFA.
players.elevator_pitch:
Tool around the Internet, and you'll see YouTube videos touting Caldwell as the wide receiver who 
"dominated the NFL Combine." At Cadwell's size, a 4.31-second, 40-Yard Dash and a 42-inch vertical leap are impressive 
marks. 
These two marks won Caldwell additional media buzz, and possibly another look from teams. Yet, the RSP is old enough to 
remember Chris Conley. 
245
players.pre_draft_advice:
In larger leagues, consider Caldwell in the late rounds. Otherwise, monitor from afar. If 
Caldwell earns NFL Draft capital this spring, he could be worth consideration for expanded offseason rosters as a free agent 
to monitor on your roster during camp.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Caldwell is still underdeveloped physically and fundamentally, but he offers rare length and speed, meaning he potentially could grow into a downfield threat. He will require a patient coaching staff that’s willing to invest in his raw talent.

players.name: Barion Brown
players.lateround_profile: The Saints kept adding weapons in this draft, selecting Barion Brown in Round 6. He's getting a profile update because the ZAP Model...oddly doesn't mind him. Brown is fast - legitimately fast. A former track athlete, he was heavily involved as a returner across four seasons - three at Kentucky and one at LSU - and he backed up the speed with a 4.40 forty at the Combine. All things considered, the production profile isn't terrible. At his size and with that speed, Brown likely projects as a gadget or space-oriented receiver at the next level. Historically, those players haven't been consistent fantasy contributors. But there's at least something here. Brown posted a 1.97 adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt mark as a freshman - the best of his career - which helped generate a 47.6 Breakout Score (66th percentile). That's a solid number. His collegiate usage matches the archetype. Brown ranked top-10 among Combine wide receivers in both behind-the-line and deep target rates, and about 18% of his receiving yards came on screens - also a top-10 figure in the class. In other words, most of his touches were manufactured or came on vertical shots. That doesn't point to a refined route runner - it suggests a player offenses tried to scheme into space or send deep to create big plays. Again...speed. There's intrigue because that kind of athleticism is rare. But this type of wide receiver usually needs overwhelming traits to truly hit in fantasy. New Orleans clearly leaned into that idea with their Day 3 receivers, adding both Bryce Lance and Brown as potential big- play specialists. Brown is the type of player you can take at the end of rookie drafts and stash on a taxi squad. With the ZAP Model more optimistic than his draft slot suggests, there's at least a non-zero chance he does something at the next level. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 58
players.elevator_pitch:
Brown is an athletic flanker-slot receiver with good route setups but lackluster breaks. He has 
impressive catches on tape against tight coverage in the vertical game, but there are lapses with tracking the ball that 
create a bad chain of mishaps. When Brown leaves his feet unnecessarily because he doesn't have a confident bead on the 
ball, he lapses into clap attacks or uses the suboptimal attack position for the trajectory of the target. 
Otherwise, Brown earns the ball against tight coverage and hard contact with a reliable frequency that one can envision an 
NFL team valuing him Brown as the third or fourth option in 11 personnel sets who can generate big plays up the seams 
against zone, manufactured plays outside against man, and from the backfield as a runner or screen receiver.

players.pre_draft_advice:
In larger leagues, consider Brown in the late rounds. Otherwise, monitor from afar. If 
Brown earns NFL Draft capital this spring, he could be worth consideration for expanded offseason rosters as a free agent to

players.brugler_summary: Overall, Brown is more specialized than well-rounded as a wide receiver, but his speed and special teams impact can be a differentiating factor in the NFL. He projects as a WR4/5 and kick returner extraordinaire.
